utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Did you hear what I was playing, Lane?

utterances.speaker: LANE
utterances.text: I didn’t think it polite to listen, sir.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I’m sorry for that, for your sake. I don’t play accurately—any one can play accurately—but I play with wonderful expression. As far as the piano is concerned, sentiment is my forte. I keep science for Life.

utterances.speaker: LANE
utterances.text: Yes, sir.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: And, speaking of the science of Life, have you got the cucumber sandwiches cut for Lady Bracknell?

utterances.speaker: LANE
utterances.text: Yes, sir.

beat_states.desire_state: to be admired and entertained by his own wit, seeking validation for his self-image as a man of style and feeling
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain a life of aesthetic pleasure and social performance while avoiding anything earnest or sincere
beat_states.obstacle: Lane refuses to play the audience role — his deflection denies Algernon the admiration he seeks

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is in his element — at home, performing, in command of the conversation. High pleasure from his own wit. Barely any vulnerability; he treats everything as material for epigram.

social_state.rationale: Clearly the master in the master-servant dynamic. Shows a casual, playful warmth toward Lane but it's the warmth of someone who needs an audience, not genuine connection.

known_facts: Lady Bracknell is expected for tea
Cucumber sandwiches are required for the visit
He was just playing the piano
Lane is his manservant
hidden_secrets: His double life as 'Bunburying' — using a fictional friend to escape obligations
false_beliefs: He believes his piano playing is expressively wonderful
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon is aware of the social obligations ahead but is more interested in performing his philosophy of life. His self-assessment of his piano playing is likely inflated, which Wilde uses for comic irony.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — transforms Lane's polite dismissal into a platform for a witty aphorism, reframing rejection as an opportunity for display
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: claims sentiment is his forte at the piano while immediately pivoting to the 'science of Life' (cucumber sandwiches) — his supposed depth of feeling is entirely performative
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon may genuinely be fishing for honest feedback about his playing and Lane's diplomatic non-answer lands as a small wound he must quickly cover with wit

beat_states.desire_state: to maintain professional propriety while navigating Algernon's demand for engagement
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to preserve his position and dignity by performing the role of the perfect servant — managing his master without appearing to do so
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon keeps trying to draw him into personal, non-functional conversation that threatens the servant's carefully maintained neutrality

affect_state.rationale: Lane is calm, composed, operating with practiced ease. Low arousal — this is routine. High certainty about how to handle Algernon. Slight positive valence from the dry pleasure of his own wit.

social_state.rationale: Formally subordinate but wields an understated power through his deadpan responses. Minimal warmth — professional cordiality only.

known_facts: Lady Bracknell is expected
Cucumber sandwiches have been prepared
Algernon's piano playing is not particularly good
His master craves an audience
hidden_secrets: He likely has opinions about Algernon's lifestyle and character that he keeps entirely to himself
epistemic_state.rationale: Lane understands the social dynamics perfectly. He knows more than he lets on — his diplomatic answer about the piano is itself evidence of sharp perception masked by servility.

beat_states.defense_state: formality as armor — uses the conventions of servitude ('I didn't think it polite to listen, sir') to sidestep any genuine response
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: his 'polite' excuse is itself a devastating critique disguised as deference — he is simultaneously subservient and subtly insubordinate
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Lane may be genuinely indifferent to Algernon's playing rather than diplomatically withholding criticism — his answer could be literal rather than ironic

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Oh! . . . by the way, Lane, I see from your book that on Thursday night, when Lord Shoreman and Mr. Worthing were dining with me, eight bottles of champagne are entered as having been consumed.

utterances.speaker: LANE
utterances.text: Yes, sir; eight bottles and a pint.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Why is it that at a bachelor’s establishment the servants invariably drink the champagne? I ask merely for information.

utterances.speaker: LANE
utterances.text: I attribute it to the superior quality of the wine, sir. I have often observed that in married households the champagne is rarely of a first-rate brand.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Good heavens! Is marriage so demoralising as that?

utterances.speaker: LANE
utterances.text: I believe it _is_ a very pleasant state, sir. I have had very little experience of it myself up to the present. I have only been married once. That was in consequence of a misunderstanding between myself and a young person.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I don’t know that I am much interested in your family life, Lane.

utterances.speaker: LANE
utterances.text: No, sir; it is not a very interesting subject. I never think of it myself.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Very natural, I am sure. That will do, Lane, thank you.

utterances.speaker: LANE
utterances.text: Thank you, sir.

beat_states.desire_state: to playfully confront Lane about the missing champagne while maintaining his pose of aristocratic indifference
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to sustain a life of effortless pleasure and wit without ever appearing to take anything seriously
beat_states.obstacle: Lane's unflappable composure deflects every attempt to catch him out, turning each accusation into a philosophical exchange

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is amused and in command of the social situation; he enjoys the sparring but never lets it touch him emotionally. His dismissal of Lane's personal life reasserts control when Lane begins to hold too much conversational ground.

social_state.rationale: Clearly the master, maintaining hierarchical distance. Shows mild warmth through the willingness to banter, but snaps back to dismissiveness ('I don't know that I am much interested in your family life') when Lane oversteps the implicit boundary of the game.

known_facts: Eight bottles and a pint of champagne were consumed Thursday when only two guests dined
The servants are drinking his champagne
Lane has been married once
hidden_secrets: He likely doesn't truly mind the champagne theft — it gives him material for wit
false_beliefs: He believes he is asking 'merely for information' when he is actually performing a social ritual
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon knows perfectly well the servants drink the champagne. His question is rhetorical — a setup for epigram. He collects Lane's answer about marriage as ammunition for his worldview.

beat_states.defense_state: deflection through wit — transforms a genuine household concern into an epigram about marriage to avoid appearing earnest or concerned about money
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: wants to hold Lane accountable for the champagne but simultaneously needs to appear too sophisticated to care about such things
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon may be genuinely annoyed about the champagne expenditure given his later-revealed financial difficulties, and the wit is a more active cover for real anxiety about money

beat_states.desire_state: to deflect blame for the champagne consumption while maintaining his dignified position
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to preserve his standing and comfort by perfectly mirroring and enabling his master's performative worldview
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon's direct questioning about the champagne ledger puts him in a position where he must account for clear evidence of servant indulgence

affect_state.rationale: Lane is calm, practiced, and nearly imperturbable. His low arousal reflects his mastery of the servant's art of composure. Slight vulnerability emerges only when personal territory (his marriage) is briefly exposed before Algernon shuts it down.

social_state.rationale: Formally subordinate but exercises considerable covert social power through his wit. His 'Thank you, sir' mirrors Algernon's 'thank you' — a subtle equality in the verbal game. Warmth is minimal but present in his willingness to play along.

known_facts: The servants drank the champagne
Exactly eight bottles and a pint were consumed
He has been married once
Algernon expects witty answers rather than honest confessions
hidden_secrets: He and/or the other servants drank the champagne and he knows exactly what happened
The details of his marriage and its dissolution
epistemic_state.rationale: Lane is the most epistemically clear character in the beat — he knows the truth about the champagne and deliberately obscures it. He understands the rules of the master-servant performative game perfectly.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectual reframing — transforms a theft accusation into an aesthetic observation about wine quality, making the transgression sound like connoisseurship
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: presents marriage as 'a very pleasant state' while simultaneously describing his own marriage as a 'misunderstanding' he never thinks about — performing both conventional propriety and Wildean detachment at once
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Lane's remark about marriage being 'a misunderstanding' could contain genuine bitterness beneath the comic surface, suggesting deeper emotional scarring he has learned to aestheticize in imitation of his master's style

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Lane’s views on marriage seem somewhat lax. Really, if the lower orders don’t set us a good example, what on earth is the use of them? They seem, as a class, to have absolutely no sense of moral responsibility.

utterances.speaker: LANE
utterances.text: Mr. Ernest Worthing.

beat_states.desire_state: to perform witty superiority and deflect any moral scrutiny from himself onto the lower classes
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain his position as an effortlessly superior, entertaining aristocrat unburdened by sincerity
beat_states.obstacle: Lane's candid views on marriage have inadvertently exposed the absurdity of upper-class morality, threatening Algernon's pose of authority

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is thoroughly enjoying his own paradoxical wit; he feels in command of the social dynamic, performing for himself as much as for Lane, with no genuine emotional exposure

social_state.rationale: He reasserts class hierarchy through ironic condescension; warmth is low because he treats Lane as a prop for his epigrammatic performance rather than as a person

known_facts: Lane has expressed a cynical view of marriage
Champagne was consumed on Thursday when Lord Shoreman dined
Ernest Worthing is expected to visit
hidden_secrets: His own 'Bunburying' double life
He likely has no genuine moral convictions about marriage or class
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon is self-aware enough to know his moralizing is performative; he holds no false beliefs here but deploys irony deliberately

beat_states.defense_state: projection — attributing moral failings to the lower classes to avoid examining his own
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He inverts the social expectation (lower orders should set the example for upper orders) precisely because he knows his own class has no moral ground, yet he performs indignation as if they do

beat_states.desire_state: to professionally transition away from a conversation in which he has been made an object of satire and announce the arriving guest
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain his position and dignity by performing impeccable, unflappable service
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon's condescending commentary on 'the lower orders' directly includes Lane, putting him in a position where responding would be insubordinate and silence is complicit

affect_state.rationale: Lane is slightly stung by being classified with 'the lower orders' who lack moral responsibility, but he is well-practiced at suppressing any reaction; his certainty and control come from the clarity of his professional role

social_state.rationale: Firmly in the subordinate position; warmth is neutral — he is neither hostile nor affectionate, simply executing duty

known_facts: Mr. Ernest Worthing has arrived
Algernon's views on morality are performative and ironic
His own marriage ended practically
hidden_secrets: His private opinions about Algernon's hypocrisy
The extent to which he may have consumed or redistributed the champagne
false_beliefs: The visitor's name is Ernest Worthing
epistemic_state.rationale: Lane knows the visitor as 'Ernest' — this is Jack's deception, which Lane has no reason to question; Lane also likely has private views about Algernon he will never voice

beat_states.defense_state: professional detachment — he uses the announcement of a guest to redirect and terminate an uncomfortable line of discourse
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He may privately find Algernon's inverted moralizing amusing or insulting, but his role requires total impassivity
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Lane's announcement could also be read as a subtle, perfectly timed act of ironic punctuation — cutting off Algernon's moralizing with mundane business, a quiet form of servant wit

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: How are you, my dear Ernest? What brings you up to town?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Oh, pleasure, pleasure! What else should bring one anywhere? Eating as usual, I see, Algy!

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I believe it is customary in good society to take some slight refreshment at five o’clock. Where have you been since last Thursday?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: In the country.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: What on earth do you do there?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: When one is in town one amuses oneself. When one is in the country one amuses other people. It is excessively boring.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: And who are the people you amuse?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Oh, neighbours, neighbours.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Got nice neighbours in your part of Shropshire?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Perfectly horrid! Never speak to one of them.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: How immensely you must amuse them! By the way, Shropshire is your county, is it not?

beat_states.desire_state: To probe Jack's double life and catch him in inconsistencies about his identity and whereabouts
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain intellectual superiority and control over social situations through wit and knowledge of others' secrets
beat_states.obstacle: Jack deflects with witticisms and evasive generalities, refusing to give straight answers

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is enjoying the cat-and-mouse game. He senses something is off about Jack's story and is energized by the pursuit. He controls the rhythm of questions, keeping Jack on the defensive while appearing casually sociable.

social_state.rationale: As host, Algernon holds the higher-status position and uses it to direct conversation. His warmth is genuine but instrumentalized—friendly enough to keep Jack talking, pointed enough to corner him.

known_facts: Jack goes by 'Ernest' in town
Jack has been in the country since last Thursday
Jack claims to be from Shropshire
Jack has some country existence with neighbors he claims to despise
hidden_secrets: Algernon suspects Jack is leading a double life
Algernon likely knows or suspects the name 'Ernest' is fabricated
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon is assembling pieces of a puzzle. His specific question about Shropshire being Jack's county suggests he already has information that may contradict Jack's claims and is testing him.

beat_states.defense_state: Maintains aristocratic nonchalance and wit to disguise that he is deliberately investigating Jack
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Greets Jack warmly as 'dear Ernest' while simultaneously setting traps to expose his deceptions

beat_states.desire_state: To enjoy his town persona as 'Ernest' without being scrutinized about his country life
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain the freedom of his double identity so he can pursue pleasure in town and escape his responsibilities in the country
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon keeps asking probing questions about the country, forcing Jack closer to revealing the truth about his dual existence

affect_state.rationale: Jack enters in good spirits seeking pleasure, but becomes increasingly uneasy as Algernon's questions narrow toward uncomfortable truths. His contradictory answers (never speaks to neighbors / amuses them) reveal growing carelessness under pressure.

social_state.rationale: As guest, Jack is in a slightly subordinate position. He is friendly and bantering but increasingly reactive rather than leading the conversation. His teasing about Algernon's eating is an attempt to reclaim social parity.

known_facts: He is not actually named Ernest
He leads a double life between town and country
He has a ward and responsibilities in the country
Algernon knows him only as Ernest
hidden_secrets: His real name is Jack Worthing
He invented the name Ernest for his town life
He has a ward named Cecily in the country
His country life involves serious moral responsibilities
false_beliefs: He believes Algernon has no reason to suspect his double identity
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack is operating under the assumption that his cover is intact, not realizing Algernon is systematically probing. His self-contradictions about Shropshire neighbors suggest he is not fully guarding his story.

beat_states.defense_state: Uses witty paradoxes and generalizations to avoid giving specific or truthful answers
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims the country is boring and neighbors are horrid, yet voluntarily maintains an elaborate life there with responsibilities he clearly takes seriously enough to sustain
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Jack may be slightly aware Algernon is fishing and is deliberately giving absurd contradictory answers as a form of playful resistance rather than genuine evasion

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Eh? Shropshire? Yes, of course. Hallo! Why all these cups? Why cucumber sandwiches? Why such reckless extravagance in one so young? Who is coming to tea?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Oh! merely Aunt Augusta and Gwendolen.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: How perfectly delightful!

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Yes, that is all very well; but I am afraid Aunt Augusta won’t quite approve of your being here.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: May I ask why?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: My dear fellow, the way you flirt with Gwendolen is perfectly disgraceful. It is almost as bad as the way Gwendolen flirts with you.

beat_states.desire_state: to tease Jack and assert intellectual superiority by exposing Jack's transparent romantic motives
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain his position as the cleverest person in every room and to control social situations through wit
beat_states.obstacle: Jack's attempt to seem casual and innocent about his interest in Gwendolen

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is in his element — hosting, teasing, wielding information. He's amused and confident, enjoying the power of knowing more than Jack thinks he does. Very low vulnerability; he's on home turf performing his favorite role.

social_state.rationale: Algernon holds the high ground as host, information-holder, and family gatekeeper (cousin to Gwendolen). His warmth is present but secondary to his enjoyment of dominance through wit.

known_facts: Jack has been flirting with Gwendolen
Gwendolen has been flirting back
Aunt Augusta likely disapproves of Jack as a suitor
Jack claims to come from Shropshire
hidden_secrets: He suspects Jack is leading a double life (the cigarette case inscription)
He himself maintains the Bunbury fiction
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon is shrewdly observant and already suspects more about Jack than he lets on. He's strategically deploying what he knows to keep Jack off-balance before pressing harder.

beat_states.defense_state: preemptive superiority — by naming the flirtation before Jack can act on it, Algernon maintains control
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He warns Jack about propriety regarding Gwendolen while himself living an elaborate double life as Bunbury's friend
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon's mention of Aunt Augusta's disapproval could be genuine protective concern for his cousin Gwendolen rather than pure teasing, though the playful tone makes this less likely.

beat_states.desire_state: to discover that Gwendolen will be present and to position himself to see her
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to win Gwendolen's love and secure a respectable identity worthy of her
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon's teasing scrutiny and the social obstacle of Aunt Augusta's disapproval

affect_state.rationale: Jack is delighted to learn Gwendolen is coming — his 'How perfectly delightful!' is barely concealed excitement. But Algernon's warning about Aunt Augusta and the exposure of his flirtation puts him on the back foot, uncertain and slightly exposed.

social_state.rationale: Jack is in Algernon's home, on Algernon's territory, and Algernon is controlling the information. Jack's warmth is genuine — he's among friends and near the woman he loves — but his status is lower as the one being interrogated and teased.

known_facts: Gwendolen and Lady Bracknell are coming to tea
Algernon has noticed his flirtation with Gwendolen
He is presenting himself as 'Ernest' in town
hidden_secrets: His real name is Jack, not Ernest
He has invented a brother named Ernest to escape to London
He is a guardian to Cecily in the country
false_beliefs: He believes his double life is still reasonably secure from Algernon's deeper scrutiny
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack knows his romantic intentions but doesn't yet realize how much Algernon suspects about his double life. He is maintaining the Ernest fiction while his true purpose — proposing to Gwendolen — remains partially concealed.

beat_states.defense_state: deflection through enthusiastic cheerfulness to mask his romantic eagerness
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is living under a false identity ('Ernest') while genuinely pursuing sincere romantic attachment
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Jack's rapid questions about the sandwiches and cups could be a deliberate tactic to change the subject from Algernon's earlier probing about Shropshire, rather than genuine curiosity.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I am in love with Gwendolen. I have come up to town expressly to propose to her.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I thought you had come up for pleasure? . . . I call that business.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: How utterly unromantic you are!

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I really don’t see anything romantic in proposing. It is very romantic to be in love. But there is nothing romantic about a definite proposal. Why, one may be accepted. One usually is, I believe. Then the excitement is all over. The very essence of romance is uncertainty. If ever I get married, I’ll certainly try to forget the fact.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I have no doubt about that, dear Algy. The Divorce Court was specially invented for people whose memories are so curiously constituted.

beat_states.desire_state: to puncture Jack's romantic pretensions and assert intellectual superiority through paradox
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain aesthetic detachment and pleasure-seeking freedom, resisting the constraints of earnest commitment
beat_states.obstacle: Jack's genuine emotional conviction, which resists pure intellectual deflation

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is thoroughly enjoying himself, high on his own wit, feeling in command of the conversation; Jack's Divorce Court barb barely dents him

social_state.rationale: Holding court in his own flat, wielding wit as dominance; warmth is performative — he's affectionate toward Jack but primarily as an audience for his epigrams

known_facts: Jack claims to be in love with Gwendolen
Jack has come to town to propose
He is Gwendolen's cousin
hidden_secrets: He knows about the cigarette case inscribed 'from little Cecily' and suspects Jack's double life
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon is already suspicious of Jack's identity but hasn't revealed this yet; he's gathering information while performing nonchalance

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — converting emotional terrain into philosophical epigram to avoid engaging with sincerity
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: claims romance requires uncertainty while systematically eliminating uncertainty through cynical analysis; fascinated by the very institutions he professes to disdain
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon's cynicism about proposals may mask genuine anxiety about intimacy and commitment, making his philosophizing a preemptive defense rather than pure intellectual play

beat_states.desire_state: to declare his sincere romantic intentions toward Gwendolen and be taken seriously
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to secure love, legitimacy, and social acceptance through marriage to Gwendolen
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon's cynical deflation of romance and refusal to treat the proposal with gravity

affect_state.rationale: Jack is buoyed by being in love and sure of his intentions, but exposed by having to confess them to someone who mocks the very concept; his final retort restores some control

social_state.rationale: Slightly lower status as the one making an earnest confession that is being deflated, but the Divorce Court riposte reasserts parity; warmth is present but strained by irritation

known_facts: He is in love with Gwendolen
He has come to London specifically to propose
Algernon is Gwendolen's cousin and a potential gatekeeper
hidden_secrets: His name is not actually Ernest
He leads a double life as Jack in the country and Ernest in town
false_beliefs: That Algernon will ultimately support the proposal without complications
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack is operating under his Ernest disguise, revealing his genuine feelings while concealing his fundamental identity fraud

beat_states.defense_state: counterattack through wit — redirecting Algernon's cynicism back at him via the Divorce Court jab
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: genuinely earnest about his love yet fully capable of matching Algernon's cynical wit, undermining his own claim to romantic sincerity
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Jack may be performing earnestness partly to provoke Algernon's wit, enjoying the sparring as much as he claims to be annoyed by it

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Oh! there is no use speculating on that subject. Divorces are made in Heaven— Please don’t touch the cucumber sandwiches. They are ordered specially for Aunt Augusta.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Well, you have been eating them all the time.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: That is quite a different matter. She is my aunt. Have some bread and butter. The bread and butter is for Gwendolen. Gwendolen is devoted to bread and butter.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: And very good bread and butter it is too.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Well, my dear fellow, you need not eat as if you were going to eat it all. You behave as if you were married to her already. You are not married to her already, and I don’t think you ever will be.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Why on earth do you say that?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Well, in the first place girls never marry the men they flirt with. Girls don’t think it right.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Oh, that is nonsense!

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: It isn’t. It is a great truth. It accounts for the extraordinary number of bachelors that one sees all over the place. In the second place, I don’t give my consent.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Your consent!

beat_states.desire_state: To maintain dominance over Jack, control the social situation, and obstruct Jack's romantic pursuit of Gwendolen
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To preserve his sophisticated, untouchable position as arbiter of social propriety and avoid the threat that others' marriages pose to his lifestyle
beat_states.obstacle: Jack's persistence in eating the sandwiches and in pursuing Gwendolen despite Algernon's objections

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is thoroughly enjoying himself — he's performing, testing, provoking. He feels in control of the exchange, wielding paradoxes as weapons. His arousal increases as he escalates from food policing to withholding consent. Very low vulnerability; he's playing a game.

social_state.rationale: Algernon positions himself as the host, the arbiter, and even a quasi-guardian figure by claiming consent rights. His warmth is low — affectionate in form but actively obstructing Jack's happiness for sport.

known_facts: Jack is in love with Gwendolen and wants to propose
The cucumber sandwiches are for Aunt Augusta
He himself has been eating the cucumber sandwiches
He suspects something about Jack's double life (Bunburying)
hidden_secrets: He has discovered the inscription on Jack's cigarette case suggesting a double identity
He himself is a Bunburyist
false_beliefs: He genuinely believes he has some authority to give or withhold consent regarding Gwendolen
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon is building toward a confrontation about Jack's identity. The consent gambit is partly a strategic trap to force Jack to reveal more. He holds knowledge Jack doesn't know he has.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — transforms personal possessiveness into witty aphorisms and invented social rules
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He devours the cucumber sandwiches himself while forbidding Jack from touching them; he claims authority over Gwendolen's future while having no real power over it
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon may be genuinely protective of Gwendolen beneath the performance, using wit to mask real concern about Jack's suitability

beat_states.desire_state: To be taken seriously as Gwendolen's suitor and to resist Algernon's attempts to gatekeep both the engagement and the social occasion
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure love, legitimacy, and a stable identity through marriage to Gwendolen
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon's relentless teasing, gatekeeping of food and of Gwendolen, and sudden claim of authority over the engagement

affect_state.rationale: Jack starts the beat relatively at ease (enjoying bread and butter) but becomes progressively more agitated and wrong-footed. By the end, Algernon's claim of consent authority genuinely shocks him. His certainty and control drop as the beat progresses.

social_state.rationale: Jack is being managed by Algernon throughout — told what to eat, told he won't marry Gwendolen, told he needs consent. He's the guest being controlled by the host. His warmth remains moderate; he's not hostile, just flustered.

known_facts: He loves Gwendolen and intends to propose
Algernon has been eating the cucumber sandwiches meant for Lady Bracknell
Algernon now claims to have authority over his engagement
hidden_secrets: His name is not actually Ernest
He leads a double life between town and country
His identity as 'Ernest' is a fabrication
false_beliefs: He believes his double identity is completely secure from Algernon
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack is unaware that Algernon has already found the cigarette case evidence. He doesn't yet realize Algernon's consent gambit is a setup to extract information about his true identity.

beat_states.defense_state: Indignation — deflects Algernon's provocations by expressing outrage rather than engaging with the substance
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He wants to appear confident and assured as a suitor but is increasingly reactive and on the back foot, revealing how much Algernon's opinion destabilizes him
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Jack's exasperated 'Your consent!' could be read as amused rather than genuinely threatened — playing along with the game rather than being destabilized by it

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: My dear fellow, Gwendolen is my first cousin. And before I allow you to marry her, you will have to clear up the whole question of Cecily.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Cecily! What on earth do you mean? What do you mean, Algy, by Cecily! I don’t know any one of the name of Cecily.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Bring me that cigarette case Mr. Worthing left in the smoking-room the last time he dined here.

utterances.speaker: LANE
utterances.text: Yes, sir.

beat_states.desire_state: To corner Jack with the evidence of the cigarette case and force him to reveal the truth about Cecily and his double life
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain intellectual and social superiority through wit and the exposure of others' hypocrisies
beat_states.obstacle: Jack's vigorous denial of knowing anyone named Cecily

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is thoroughly enjoying this moment — he holds all the cards, knows the cigarette case will expose Jack, and relishes the cat-and-mouse game. He is maximally in control and certain of his evidence.

social_state.rationale: He wields his position as Gwendolen's cousin to claim authority over Jack's proposal, and his warmth is thin — this is playful domination disguised as friendly concern.

known_facts: Jack left a cigarette case inscribed to 'Uncle Jack' from someone named Cecily
Jack claims to not know anyone named Cecily
Gwendolen is his first cousin
Jack's real surname is Worthing
hidden_secrets: He has been Bunburying — maintaining a fictional invalid friend to escape social obligations
He has already examined the cigarette case and knows its inscription
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon has the physical evidence and has been sitting on this information, waiting for the perfect moment to spring the trap.

beat_states.defense_state: Using familial authority ('my first cousin') to assert gatekeeping power over Gwendolen
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He delights in exposing Jack's deception while maintaining his own elaborate deceptions (Bunburying)

beat_states.desire_state: To deny any connection to Cecily and prevent Algernon from derailing his proposal to Gwendolen
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure Gwendolen's hand and maintain the respectability of his constructed identity
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon's unexpected knowledge of Cecily and his insistence on 'clearing up' the matter before allowing the marriage

affect_state.rationale: Jack is rattled and alarmed. The mention of Cecily threatens to unravel his entire double life. His repeated exclamations ('Cecily! What on earth do you mean?') betray escalating anxiety beneath the denial.

social_state.rationale: Jack has suddenly lost the upper hand; Algernon has shifted from host to interrogator, and Jack is now on the defensive, his status diminished.

known_facts: He is guardian to a young woman named Cecily Cardew in the country
He leads a double life as 'Ernest' in town and 'Jack' in the country
He left a cigarette case somewhere but may not realize the significance yet
hidden_secrets: Cecily Cardew is his ward living at his country estate
He uses the name 'Ernest' in London as part of his Bunbury-like double life
His entire identity as 'Ernest' is fabricated
false_beliefs: He believes his denial will be sufficient to shut down Algernon's line of questioning
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack knows exactly who Cecily is but is desperate to keep his country life completely separate from his London identity. He doesn't yet realize Algernon has physical evidence.

beat_states.defense_state: Feigning total ignorance and indignation — performing shock and confusion to stonewall the inquiry
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He absolutely knows who Cecily is but must pretend complete ignorance, creating panic beneath his outward bluster
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: A small possibility Jack is genuinely momentarily confused about how Algernon could know the name, rather than purely performing ignorance — but his triple repetition strongly suggests defensive bluster.

beat_states.desire_state: To comply with the request while maintaining his characteristic professional distance from the escalating conflict
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To perform the role of the perfectly invisible, unflappable manservant
beat_states.obstacle: None significant — the request is straightforward

affect_state.rationale: Lane is entirely neutral, operating in servant mode with practiced calm. He knows exactly what is being asked and where the item is.

social_state.rationale: Lane occupies the lowest social status in the room as a servant, maintaining just enough warmth to be properly deferential without personality.

known_facts: Mr. Worthing left a cigarette case in the smoking-room
He knows where the cigarette case is kept
He has observed the dynamic between Algernon and Jack
hidden_secrets: He may have observed the inscription on the cigarette case
epistemic_state.rationale: Lane is privy to household details and likely aware of more than he reveals, consistent with the play's satirical treatment of servants who know everything but say nothing.

beat_states.defense_state: Professional detachment — complete emotional non-engagement with the drama unfolding between his employer and guest
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He likely knows more about the cigarette case and the interpersonal dynamics than he lets on, but professionalism requires total blankness

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Do you mean to say you have had my cigarette case all this time? I wish to goodness you had let me know. I have been writing frantic letters to Scotland Yard about it. I was very nearly offering a large reward.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Well, I wish you would offer one. I happen to be more than usually hard up.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: There is no good offering a large reward now that the thing is found.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I think that is rather mean of you, Ernest, I must say. However, it makes no matter, for, now that I look at the inscription inside, I find that the thing isn’t yours after all.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Of course it’s mine. You have seen me with it a hundred times, and you have no right whatsoever to read what is written inside. It is a very ungentlemanly thing to read a private cigarette case.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Oh! it is absurd to have a hard and fast rule about what one should read and what one shouldn’t. More than half of modern culture depends on what one shouldn’t read.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I am quite aware of the fact, and I don’t propose to discuss modern culture. It isn’t the sort of thing one should talk of in private. I simply want my cigarette case back.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Yes; but this isn’t your cigarette case. This cigarette case is a present from some one of the name of Cecily, and you said you didn’t know any one of that name.

beat_states.desire_state: To catch Jack in a lie and expose the mystery of his double life, particularly the identity of 'Cecily'
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To penetrate surfaces and expose the artifice behind people's respectable performances — his deepest pleasure is in unmasking
beat_states.obstacle: Jack's persistent deflection, moral indignation, and refusal to engage with the evidence

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is thoroughly enjoying himself. He holds all the cards — the physical evidence, the damning inscription, and the momentum of the interrogation. He is delighted by Jack's squirming and feels in total command.

social_state.rationale: Algernon has seized the dominant position through possession of the cigarette case and rhetorical agility. His warmth is minimal — he is playful but merciless, treating Jack's discomfort as entertainment.

known_facts: The cigarette case inscription mentions someone named Cecily
Jack claimed not to know anyone named Cecily
Jack has been using the name Ernest
Jack's defensiveness confirms the inscription is significant
hidden_secrets: He himself leads a double life via his invented friend 'Bunbury'
false_beliefs: He believes Jack's real name is Ernest
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon has key evidence but not the full picture yet. He senses a deception but doesn't yet know its exact nature. Ironically, he doesn't realize Jack's double life mirrors his own Bunburying.

beat_states.defense_state: Wit and paradox deployed as intellectual armor — transforming every serious accusation into an epigram to maintain superiority
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He genuinely wants money (the reward comment is sincere) yet immediately pivots to the detective game, suggesting his appetite for social intrigue overrides material need
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon's 'hard up' comment could suggest a genuine financial anxiety beneath the wit, making his teasing partly motivated by resentment at Jack's wealth

beat_states.desire_state: To recover his cigarette case and shut down Algernon's line of inquiry before the inscription is examined
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his double identity so he can marry Gwendolen and keep his respectable life intact
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon possesses the cigarette case and has already read the inscription revealing 'Cecily' — a name Jack denied knowing

affect_state.rationale: Jack is increasingly cornered. His escalating indignation masks rising panic as Algernon closes in on the Cecily connection. He senses he is losing control of the conversation and his secret identity.

social_state.rationale: Jack adopts a morally superior tone (reading private inscriptions is 'ungentlemanly') but this is a losing gambit — Algernon holds the physical evidence and the rhetorical upper hand. Warmth decreases as Jack grows defensive and terse.

known_facts: The cigarette case was a gift from Cecily
He told Algernon he doesn't know anyone named Cecily
Algernon has read the inscription
The inscription connects him to a life Algernon doesn't know about
hidden_secrets: He leads a double life as 'Ernest' in town and 'Jack' in the country
Cecily is his ward who lives in the country
The inscription likely reads 'From little Cecily' with some affectionate dedication
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack knows exactly what the inscription says and why it's dangerous. He is fully aware that Algernon is about to unravel his deception but tries every rhetorical maneuver to prevent it.

beat_states.defense_state: Indignation and moral outrage used as smokescreens — accusing Algernon of ungentlemanly behavior to redirect attention away from the damning inscription
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is simultaneously outraged at the invasion of privacy AND desperate to retrieve the evidence, revealing that his anger is strategic rather than genuine
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Jack's outrage about Scotland Yard letters could be partly genuine annoyance at Algernon's carelessness, not purely strategic deflection

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Well, if you want to know, Cecily happens to be my aunt.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Your aunt!

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Yes. Charming old lady she is, too. Lives at Tunbridge Wells. Just give it back to me, Algy.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: But why does she call herself little Cecily if she is your aunt and lives at Tunbridge Wells? ‘From little Cecily with her fondest love.’

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: My dear fellow, what on earth is there in that? Some aunts are tall, some aunts are not tall. That is a matter that surely an aunt may be allowed to decide for herself. You seem to think that every aunt should be exactly like your aunt! That is absurd! For Heaven’s sake give me back my cigarette case.

beat_states.desire_state: To catch Jack in his lies and extract the full truth about the cigarette case inscription and Jack's secret life
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To uncover hidden truths and leverage social intelligence for his own amusement and advantage
beat_states.obstacle: Jack keeps constructing new (if absurd) explanations that require further dismantling

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is thoroughly enjoying himself. He holds all the cards — the cigarette case, the evidence, the questions. He feels certain Jack is lying and is delighted by the increasingly absurd evasions.

social_state.rationale: Algernon holds dominant status by possessing the cigarette case and controlling the interrogation. His warmth is playful rather than hostile — he's teasing a friend, not destroying an enemy.

known_facts: The cigarette case is inscribed 'From little Cecily with her fondest love'
Jack claims Cecily is his aunt at Tunbridge Wells
'Little Cecily' contradicts the idea of a charming old aunt
Jack is clearly fabricating
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon doesn't yet know the full truth but he can smell the lie. Each of Jack's answers opens a new logical gap that Algernon identifies with precision. He's building his case methodically.

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is genuinely curious and intellectually engaged but also savoring Jack's discomfort as entertainment — detective work as sport

beat_states.desire_state: To recover his cigarette case and shut down Algernon's line of questioning about Cecily's identity
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his double life and secure his path to marrying Gwendolen without exposure
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon holds the physical evidence (cigarette case) and is asking increasingly pointed questions that threaten to unravel the deception

affect_state.rationale: Jack is increasingly cornered. His irritation and urgency mount as each fabrication creates new holes. He's losing control of the narrative and knows it, producing anxious bluster disguised as righteous indignation.

social_state.rationale: Jack's status is slipping as Algernon controls the situation by withholding the case. His warmth drops as frustration replaces friendliness — he's snapping and demanding rather than asking.

known_facts: Cecily is actually his young ward, not his aunt
The cigarette case inscription contradicts his story
Algernon is catching inconsistencies
hidden_secrets: Cecily is his young ward living at his country estate
He leads a double life as Ernest in town and Jack in the country
His entire identity in London is fabricated
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack is fully aware he's lying and must improvise increasingly ridiculous cover stories. He knows the truth about Cecily and his double life but is desperately concealing both.

beat_states.defense_state: Rationalization — constructing absurd but confident-sounding logical arguments to explain away inconsistencies (the 'little Cecily' / aunt contradiction)
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He knows his explanation is nonsensical but must commit to it with total conviction; internally panicking while externally performing indignation

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Yes. But why does your aunt call you her uncle? ‘From little Cecily, with her fondest love to her dear Uncle Jack.’ There is no objection, I admit, to an aunt being a small aunt, but why an aunt, no matter what her size may be, should call her own nephew her uncle, I can’t quite make out. Besides, your name isn’t Jack at all; it is Ernest.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: It isn’t Ernest; it’s Jack.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: You have always told me it was Ernest. I have introduced you to every one as Ernest. You answer to the name of Ernest. You look as if your name was Ernest. You are the most earnest-looking person I ever saw in my life. It is perfectly absurd your saying that your name isn’t Ernest. It’s on your cards. Here is one of them. ‘Mr. Ernest Worthing, B. 4, The Albany.’ I’ll keep this as a proof that your name is Ernest if ever you attempt to deny it to me, or to Gwendolen, or to any one else.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Well, my name is Ernest in town and Jack in the country, and the cigarette case was given to me in the country.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Yes, but that does not account for the fact that your small Aunt Cecily, who lives at Tunbridge Wells, calls you her dear uncle. Come, old boy, you had much better have the thing out at once.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: My dear Algy, you talk exactly as if you were a dentist. It is very vulgar to talk like a dentist when one isn’t a dentist. It produces a false impression.

beat_states.desire_state: To corner Jack into confessing the full truth about his double identity
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain intellectual and social superiority by knowing everyone's secrets and using that leverage
beat_states.obstacle: Jack keeps deflecting with partial truths and evasive humor rather than giving a full confession

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is thoroughly enjoying himself. He holds all the cards — the cigarette case, the visiting card — and is relishing the logical trap he's constructed. He feels certain Jack is lying and is in complete control of the interrogation.

social_state.rationale: Algernon has clear status dominance here — he's the prosecutor with evidence. His warmth is moderate; the 'Come, old boy' shows affection, but the relentless pressing shows he values the game over Jack's comfort.

known_facts: The cigarette case is inscribed from 'little Cecily' to 'dear Uncle Jack'
Jack's visiting card reads 'Mr. Ernest Worthing, B. 4, The Albany'
Jack has always presented himself as Ernest in London
Jack now admits his name is Jack in the country and Ernest in town
Cecily lives at Tunbridge Wells
hidden_secrets: His own Bunburying practice, which mirrors Jack's double life
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon has assembled considerable evidence of Jack's deception and is methodically deploying it. He senses there's a deeper story but doesn't yet know about the ward/guardian relationship. Crucially, he hasn't yet revealed his own parallel double life.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectual superiority — treating the interrogation as an amusing logical puzzle keeps him emotionally safe
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Genuinely enjoys the game of exposing Jack while also using it as leverage to control who marries his cousin

beat_states.desire_state: To end the interrogation and reveal as little as possible about his double life
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his respectable identity and secure his romantic pursuit of Gwendolen without his fabrications being exposed
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon has overwhelming physical evidence (cigarette case, visiting card) and relentless logical persistence

affect_state.rationale: Jack is increasingly cornered. Each partial concession (admitting the double name) only invites more questions. His wit ('dentist' remark) signals rising discomfort disguised as breezy deflection. He's losing control of the conversation and his carefully maintained fiction.

social_state.rationale: Jack has lost significant status; he's the one being interrogated and caught in contradictions. The 'My dear Algy' maintains surface friendliness, but his position is defensive and subordinate in this exchange.

known_facts: His real name is Jack Worthing
He uses the name Ernest as a fictional brother and as his London identity
Cecily is his ward, not his aunt
He has already partially admitted the double name
hidden_secrets: Cecily is his ward, not his aunt — the uncle/niece relationship is guardian/ward
Ernest is a completely invented person he uses for escapism
The full mechanism and purpose of his double life
false_beliefs: That he can still keep Algernon from learning the full truth
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack knows the complete truth about his double life but is being forced to reveal it piece by piece. He earlier lied about Cecily being his aunt and is now being caught in the logical impossibilities of that lie.

beat_states.defense_state: Displacement through wit — comparing Algernon to a dentist to reframe the uncomfortable extraction of truth as something vulgar and inappropriate
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is being forced to reveal partial truths (Ernest in town, Jack in the country) while trying to maintain the dignity of someone who has done nothing wrong — yet the very admission confirms deception
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Jack may actually be somewhat relieved to be caught — the dentist metaphor ('have the thing out') suggests he's moving toward confession, and his resistance may be more performative than genuine.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Well, that is exactly what dentists always do. Now, go on! Tell me the whole thing. I may mention that I have always suspected you of being a confirmed and secret Bunburyist; and I am quite sure of it now.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Bunburyist? What on earth do you mean by a Bunburyist?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I’ll reveal to you the meaning of that incomparable expression as soon as you are kind enough to inform me why you are Ernest in town and Jack in the country.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Well, produce my cigarette case first.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Here it is. Now produce your explanation, and pray make it improbable.

beat_states.desire_state: to extract Jack's full confession about his double identity
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain intellectual superiority and control over social situations through wit and leverage
beat_states.obstacle: Jack is evasive and resistant, trying to stall by demanding the cigarette case back first

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is thoroughly enjoying himself. He holds all the cards — the cigarette case, the incriminating inscription, the knowledge of Jack's duplicity. He feels confident, amused, and in command of the exchange.

social_state.rationale: He maintains dominant status through possession of the evidence and superior composure. His warmth is moderate — he's friendly enough but clearly relishing Jack's discomfort, treating the interrogation as a delightful game.

known_facts: Jack goes by Ernest in town and Jack in the country
The cigarette case inscription proves a double identity
Cecily calls Jack 'uncle' despite not being his aunt in the conventional sense
He himself practices a parallel deception with the invented Bunbury
hidden_secrets: He maintains the fiction of an invalid friend named Bunbury to escape social obligations
He recognizes Jack's behavior because he does the same thing
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon has pieced together the essential truth of Jack's double life and is now pressing for the full narrative. He strategically withholds the meaning of 'Bunburyist' as a bargaining chip.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectual superiority — framing Jack's deception as a known category ('Bunburyism') to normalize his own similar behavior while keeping the upper hand
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He condemns Jack's double life while practicing the exact same deception himself with Bunbury

beat_states.desire_state: to recover his cigarette case and minimize the damage of his exposed double identity
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain respectability and control over the separate worlds he has constructed
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon holds both the physical evidence and the intellectual upper hand, and refuses to relent without a full explanation

affect_state.rationale: Jack is cornered and mildly irritated. He's uncertain how much to reveal and feels a loss of control as Algernon dictates the terms of the exchange. The mention of 'Bunburyist' — a term he doesn't know — increases his sense of being outmaneuvered.

social_state.rationale: Jack's status is diminished — he's the one being interrogated, caught out, and forced to negotiate for his own property. His warmth is minimal; he's guarded and transactional.

known_facts: He lives a double life as Ernest in town and Jack in the country
Algernon has discovered this through the cigarette case
He cannot fully deny the deception
hidden_secrets: The full details of his double life and the invented brother Ernest
His true feelings for Gwendolen and his plans regarding her
His origins as a foundling
false_beliefs: He may believe he can still limit how much Algernon learns
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack is aware his cover is blown but doesn't yet know what 'Bunburyist' means, leaving him epistemically disadvantaged. He's calculating how much to confess.

beat_states.defense_state: deflection — attempting to redirect focus to the cigarette case and introducing new demands to delay confession
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He knows he must eventually explain but resists, torn between wanting to recover his property (which requires cooperation) and wanting to reveal nothing
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Jack may also be slightly curious about what 'Bunburyist' means, and his stalling may partly be a tactic to extract Algernon's secret in fair exchange

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: My dear fellow, there is nothing improbable about my explanation at all. In fact it’s perfectly ordinary. Old Mr. Thomas Cardew, who adopted me when I was a little boy, made me in his will guardian to his grand-daughter, Miss Cecily Cardew. Cecily, who addresses me as her uncle from motives of respect that you could not possibly appreciate, lives at my place in the country under the charge of her admirable governess, Miss Prism.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Where is that place in the country, by the way?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: That is nothing to you, dear boy. You are not going to be invited . . . I may tell you candidly that the place is not in Shropshire.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I suspected that, my dear fellow! I have Bunburyed all over Shropshire on two separate occasions. Now, go on. Why are you Ernest in town and Jack in the country?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: My dear Algy, I don’t know whether you will be able to understand my real motives. You are hardly serious enough. When one is placed in the position of guardian, one has to adopt a very high moral tone on all subjects. It’s one’s duty to do so. And as a high moral tone can hardly be said to conduce very much to either one’s health or one’s happiness, in order to get up to town I have always pretended to have a younger brother of the name of Ernest, who lives in the Albany, and gets into the most dreadful scrapes. That, my dear Algy, is the whole truth pure and simple.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: The truth is rarely pure and never simple. Modern life would be very tedious if it were either, and modern literature a complete impossibility!

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: That wouldn’t be at all a bad thing.

beat_states.desire_state: To extract every detail of Jack's double life, both for ammunition and to validate his own philosophy of duplicity
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his position as the cleverest person in any room and to live entirely by pleasure and wit
beat_states.obstacle: Jack's attempts to withhold information (especially the country address) and to moralize about his deception

affect_state.rationale: Delighted and energized by the unfolding confession; feels intellectually superior and in command of the interrogation; the Bunburying revelation shows confident self-exposure without real vulnerability

social_state.rationale: Dominant — he's the questioner, the wit who gets the last clever word; warmth is genuine but deployed strategically, keeping Jack comfortable enough to keep talking

known_facts: Jack maintains a double identity as Ernest/Jack
Cecily Cardew exists and lives in Jack's country house
The country house is NOT in Shropshire
Jack invented Ernest to escape guardian duties
He himself has Bunburyed across Shropshire twice
hidden_secrets: His own Bunburying practice and its full extent
His likely emerging interest in Cecily as a potential romantic target
That he is mentally cataloguing this information for future use
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon is rapidly accumulating intelligence about Jack's double life; his pointed question about the country location and his aphoristic dismissal of Jack's 'truth' suggest he sees through Jack's self-justification entirely

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectual deflection — transforms every confession into an aphorism, refusing to engage with moral seriousness
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Genuinely fascinated by Jack's scheme while pretending it's merely entertaining; his pursuit of the country address hints at a practical scheming mind beneath the pose of pure aestheticism
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon's interest in the country address may be pure conversational reflex rather than scheming, though his later actions suggest otherwise

beat_states.desire_state: To confess his double life on his own terms, maintaining dignity and moral justification for the deception
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his respectable identity and secure his romantic prospects while preserving freedom
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon's relentless probing, wit, and refusal to take anything seriously undermines Jack's attempt to frame his deception as noble

affect_state.rationale: Relief at finally confessing the truth but anxious about Algernon's judgment and his probing about the country house; tries to control the narrative but keeps losing ground to Algernon's wit

social_state.rationale: Slightly subordinate — he's the one being interrogated and forced to confess, though he maintains affectionate familiarity ('my dear Algy'); his final dismissive quip about modern literature is a small attempt to reclaim status

known_facts: He was adopted by Thomas Cardew
He is guardian to Cecily Cardew
Cecily lives in the country with Miss Prism
He invented a brother named Ernest to escape to London
Algernon has been Bunburying in Shropshire
hidden_secrets: The exact location of his country estate
His deeper feelings for Gwendolen that motivate the Ernest persona
That his enjoyment of the double life goes beyond mere duty-avoidance
false_beliefs: That his explanation sounds 'perfectly ordinary' and morally justified
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack reveals the core mechanism of his double life but carefully withholds specifics (location) and frames the entire scheme as duty-driven rather than pleasure-seeking

beat_states.defense_state: Moralistic framing — wrapping his selfish desire for freedom in the language of duty and guardianship responsibility
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims to adopt a 'high moral tone' as guardian while simultaneously confessing to systematic deception; presents himself as burdened by duty while clearly enjoying his double life
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Jack may be testing Algernon's reaction to gauge whether his double life could be sustained now that it's known, rather than genuinely confessing

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Literary criticism is not your forte, my dear fellow. Don’t try it. You should leave that to people who haven’t been at a University. They do it so well in the daily papers. What you really are is a Bunburyist. I was quite right in saying you were a Bunburyist. You are one of the most advanced Bunburyists I know.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: What on earth do you mean?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: You have invented a very useful younger brother called Ernest, in order that you may be able to come up to town as often as you like. I have invented an invaluable permanent invalid called Bunbury, in order that I may be able to go down into the country whenever I choose. Bunbury is perfectly invaluable. If it wasn’t for Bunbury’s extraordinary bad health, for instance, I wouldn’t be able to dine with you at Willis’s to-night, for I have been really engaged to Aunt Augusta for more than a week.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I haven’t asked you to dine with me anywhere to-night.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I know. You are absurdly careless about sending out invitations. It is very foolish of you. Nothing annoys people so much as not receiving invitations.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: You had much better dine with your Aunt Augusta.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I haven’t the smallest intention of doing anything of the kind. To begin with, I dined there on Monday, and once a week is quite enough to dine with one’s own relations. In the second place, whenever I do dine there I am always treated as a member of the family, and sent down with either no woman at all, or two. In the third place, I know perfectly well whom she will place me next to, to-night. She will place me next Mary Farquhar, who always flirts with her own husband across the dinner-table. That is not very pleasant. Indeed, it is not even decent . . . and that sort of thing is enormously on the increase. The amount of women in London who flirt with their own husbands is perfectly scandalous. It looks so bad. It is simply washing one’s clean linen in public. Besides, now that I know you to be a confirmed Bunburyist I naturally want to talk to you about Bunburying. I want to tell you the rules.

beat_states.desire_state: To establish intellectual superiority over Jack and claim authority as the expert on their shared practice of double lives
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain a life of pleasure and freedom from obligation through wit and social manipulation
beat_states.obstacle: Jack's resistance to being categorized and his attempts to deflect or dismiss Algernon's framework

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is in his element — performing, lecturing, holding court. He's delighted to have caught Jack out and is savoring the power of the reveal. His certainty is near-total; he has the evidence and the framework.

social_state.rationale: Algernon claims the higher status position throughout — as critic, as namer ('Bunburyist'), as rule-maker. There's genuine warmth underneath the condescension; he wants Jack as a co-conspirator, not an enemy.

known_facts: Jack has invented a brother named Ernest to escape to London
Jack leads a double life in town and country
He himself has invented Bunbury to escape obligations
Aunt Augusta expects him to dine tonight
Mary Farquhar will be seated next to him at dinner
Jack's cigarette case inscription reveals the deception
hidden_secrets: His own dependence on Bunbury as escape mechanism reveals a deeper fear of entrapment by social/family obligation
false_beliefs: That he has fully mastered and systematized the art of deception into controllable 'rules'
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon has pieced together Jack's secret and now mirrors it with his own confession about Bunbury, positioning himself as the more experienced practitioner.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — transforms moral duplicity into a charming system with 'rules,' neutralizing any guilt
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims moral disgust at wives flirting with husbands (conventional values) while celebrating his own systematic deception of his aunt
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon's lengthy speech about Aunt Augusta's dinner could mask genuine anxiety about social entrapment — his bravado may be compensation for feeling more trapped than he lets on.

beat_states.desire_state: To regain footing and resist Algernon's categorization and control of the conversation
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain respectability and protect his double life so he can pursue Gwendolen
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon has fully decoded his deception and refuses to let him change the subject or escape the framework

affect_state.rationale: Jack is on the back foot throughout this beat. His brief, terse replies ('What on earth do you mean?', 'I haven't asked you to dine') betray discomfort and loss of control. He's been exposed and is scrambling to maintain dignity.

social_state.rationale: Jack has lost the status game in this beat. Algernon is lecturing him, naming him, and even self-inviting to dinner. Jack's coolness is defensive rather than commanding.

known_facts: His double life has been exposed to Algernon
Algernon practices the same kind of deception with Bunbury
He did not invite Algernon to dinner
hidden_secrets: His feelings for Gwendolen and his plan to propose
The full extent of his constructed identity as Ernest
false_beliefs: That he might still be able to keep some control over what Algernon knows
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack is receiving new information about Bunbury and processing that Algernon is a mirror of himself, but he's too busy defending to fully engage with the implications.

beat_states.defense_state: denial and dismissal — short, clipped responses attempting to shut down the topic or redirect
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Wants to maintain the fiction of respectability while his secret has been completely exposed by his closest friend
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Jack's terse resistance could be read as genuine annoyance rather than vulnerability — he may simply be irritated at Algernon's presumption rather than feeling exposed.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I’m not a Bunburyist at all. If Gwendolen accepts me, I am going to kill my brother, indeed I think I’ll kill him in any case. Cecily is a little too much interested in him. It is rather a bore. So I am going to get rid of Ernest. And I strongly advise you to do the same with Mr. . . . with your invalid friend who has the absurd name.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Nothing will induce me to part with Bunbury, and if you ever get married, which seems to me extremely problematic, you will be very glad to know Bunbury. A man who marries without knowing Bunbury has a very tedious time of it.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: That is nonsense. If I marry a charming girl like Gwendolen, and she is the only girl I ever saw in my life that I would marry, I certainly won’t want to know Bunbury.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Then your wife will. You don’t seem to realise, that in married life three is company and two is none.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: That, my dear young friend, is the theory that the corrupt French Drama has been propounding for the last fifty years.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Yes; and that the happy English home has proved in half the time.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: For heaven’s sake, don’t try to be cynical. It’s perfectly easy to be cynical.

beat_states.desire_state: To defend his philosophy of Bunburyism and expose Jack's naive idealism about marriage as self-deception
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To preserve his freedom and pleasurable lifestyle against all threats of conventional obligation
beat_states.obstacle: Jack's stubborn moral posturing and refusal to acknowledge the reality Algernon sees so clearly

affect_state.rationale: Thoroughly enjoying himself, intellectually dominant, relishing each escalating riposte; his certainty is high because he genuinely believes his cynical worldview is correct and demonstrable

social_state.rationale: Maintains clear rhetorical dominance throughout the beat, landing every punch; warmth is low but not hostile — he's teasing a friend, not attacking an enemy

known_facts: Jack leads a double life using the name Ernest
Jack is in love with Gwendolen
Cecily exists and is connected to Jack's country life
Marriage in English society frequently involves deception
hidden_secrets: His growing curiosity about Cecily, whom Jack has just mentioned
The depth of his own reliance on Bunbury as an escape mechanism
false_beliefs: That his clever observations about marriage exempt him from its complications
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon is genuinely perceptive about social hypocrisy but overestimates his own immunity to the romantic entanglements he mocks

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization through wit — transforming potentially threatening truths about relationships into polished epigrams
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: His worldly cynicism about marriage masks a deeper avoidance of genuine intimacy and commitment
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon's fierce defense of Bunbury could signal anxiety that Jack's impending marriage threatens their friendship and his own lifestyle — making his cynicism partly a plea for Jack not to change

beat_states.desire_state: To assert his moral superiority and his intention to abandon his double life, and to shut down Algernon's cynical philosophy about marriage
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure Gwendolen's hand and establish himself as a respectable, earnest man worthy of love
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon relentlessly undermines his romantic idealism with witty cynicism about marriage, refusing to take his sincerity seriously

affect_state.rationale: Increasingly irritated and defensive as Algernon's barbs about marriage land closer to uncomfortable truths; his rising frustration ('For heaven's sake') reveals he's losing the argument and knows it

social_state.rationale: Tries to adopt a lecturing, superior tone ('my dear young friend') but is consistently outmaneuvered by Algernon's wit, leaving him in a subordinate rhetorical position

known_facts: He has been leading a double life as Ernest
Gwendolen is the woman he wants to marry
Cecily is too interested in the fictional Ernest
Algernon maintains a fictional invalid named Bunbury
hidden_secrets: His true identity as Jack Worthing, a guardian in the country
His entire courtship depends on the Ernest persona he claims he'll abandon
false_beliefs: That he can simply 'kill off' Ernest without consequence
That his marriage to Gwendolen will be uncomplicated and require no deception
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack is sincere in his desire for respectability but blind to the structural dependence his courtship has on the very fiction he plans to eliminate

beat_states.defense_state: Moral indignation — retreating to conventional propriety to deflect Algernon's uncomfortable truths about married life
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims he will 'kill' Ernest and reject Bunburyism, yet his entire courtship of Gwendolen is built on the Ernest fiction he claims to want to end
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Jack's insistence on killing Ernest may partly be performative — a way to signal moral superiority to Algernon while privately uncertain about whether he can sustain honesty

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: My dear fellow, it isn’t easy to be anything nowadays. There’s such a lot of beastly competition about. Ah! that must be Aunt Augusta. Only relatives, or creditors, ever ring in that Wagnerian manner. Now, if I get her out of the way for ten minutes, so that you can have an opportunity for proposing to Gwendolen, may I dine with you to-night at Willis’s?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I suppose so, if you want to.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Yes, but you must be serious about it. I hate people who are not serious about meals. It is so shallow of them.

beat_states.desire_state: To extract a concrete reward (dinner at Willis's) in exchange for facilitating Jack's proposal to Gwendolen
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his lifestyle of pleasure and social manipulation while keeping others entertained and indebted to him
beat_states.obstacle: Jack's indifference and lack of enthusiasm about committing to the dinner arrangement

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is energized by the arrival of Lady Bracknell (opportunity to execute his scheme), confident in his ability to orchestrate the situation, and entirely comfortable making demands. The doorbell raises his arousal slightly.

social_state.rationale: He holds the power position — Jack needs his help to get time alone with Gwendolen, and Algernon knows it. His warmth is transactional rather than genuinely affectionate; he's leveraging the friendship.

known_facts: Lady Bracknell is arriving (recognized by the doorbell)
Jack wants to propose to Gwendolen
He has leverage over Jack because he controls access to Gwendolen
Jack leads a double life as Ernest/Jack
hidden_secrets: He is a committed Bunburyist himself and has no intention of giving up the practice
He has Jack's cigarette case with the incriminating inscription
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon is fully aware of the social dynamics at play and strategically positions himself to benefit from Jack's romantic desperation.

beat_states.defense_state: deflection through wit — pivoting from the philosophical to the transactional to avoid any deeper engagement with Jack's plans
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He mocks seriousness in all things except meals, revealing that his apparent frivolity masks genuine appetites and self-interest
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon's insistence on seriousness about meals could be read as genuine anxiety about being stood up or deprioritized, masking a deeper need to feel valued by Jack

beat_states.desire_state: To secure time alone with Gwendolen to propose, even at the cost of agreeing to Algernon's demands
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To win Gwendolen's hand and establish a respectable, settled identity
beat_states.obstacle: He is dependent on Algernon's cooperation and must submit to Algernon's terms

affect_state.rationale: Jack is anxious about the imminent arrival of Lady Bracknell and the pressure of proposing. He has low control because he's at Algernon's mercy. His tepid agreement reveals reluctance mixed with nervous anticipation.
