social_state.rationale: Jack is in the subordinate position — he's the one who needs something and must agree to Algernon's terms. His warmth is low because he's irritated at being leveraged.

known_facts: Lady Bracknell and Gwendolen are arriving
He needs Algernon to distract Lady Bracknell
Algernon knows about his double life
He has agreed to dine at Willis's as payment
hidden_secrets: His true identity as Jack Worthing, guardian of Cecily
The depth of his feelings for Gwendolen and how desperate he is
false_beliefs: That he can simply 'kill off' Ernest and be done with his double life without consequence
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack is focused on the immediate goal and hasn't thought through the complications. He underestimates how entangled his deceptions have become.

beat_states.defense_state: minimizing — his flat 'I suppose so' downplays how much he actually needs Algernon's help
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He desperately needs Algernon's assistance but resents being made to bargain for it, leading to performative indifference
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Jack's 'I suppose so' might be genuine indifference about dinner rather than strategic underplaying — he may simply not care about meals the way Algernon does

utterances.speaker: LANE
utterances.text: Lady Bracknell and Miss Fairfax.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Good afternoon, dear Algernon, I hope you are behaving very well.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I’m feeling very well, Aunt Augusta.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: That’s not quite the same thing. In fact the two things rarely go together.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Dear me, you are smart!

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: I am always smart! Am I not, Mr. Worthing?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: You’re quite perfect, Miss Fairfax.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Oh! I hope I am not that. It would leave no room for developments, and I intend to develop in many directions.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: I’m sorry if we are a little late, Algernon, but I was obliged to call on dear Lady Harbury. I hadn’t been there since her poor husband’s death. I never saw a woman so altered; she looks quite twenty years younger. And now I’ll have a cup of tea, and one of those nice cucumber sandwiches you promised me.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Certainly, Aunt Augusta.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Won’t you come and sit here, Gwendolen?

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Thanks, mamma, I’m quite comfortable where I am.

beat_states.desire_state: to appear the dutiful, charming nephew while concealing the fact that he has eaten all the cucumber sandwiches
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain his pleasure-seeking lifestyle while keeping up appearances with his aunt, his primary social and financial lifeline
beat_states.obstacle: he has consumed the cucumber sandwiches promised to Lady Bracknell, and the truth will soon be discovered

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is in a precarious position — he must perform charm while knowing the sandwich crisis is imminent. His arousal is elevated by the need to improvise; his control is low because the situation is about to expose him.

social_state.rationale: Subordinate to Lady Bracknell — he plays the agreeable nephew. His warmth is strategic, aimed at keeping her pleased.

known_facts: He ate all the cucumber sandwiches
Lady Bracknell expects them
Jack is interested in Gwendolen
Jack's double life as Ernest
hidden_secrets: The cucumber sandwiches are gone
He knows Jack's secret identity and Bunbury-like deceptions
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon has critical information that Lady Bracknell lacks — both about the sandwiches and about Jack's intentions toward Gwendolen.

beat_states.defense_state: concealment — his clipped 'Certainly, Aunt Augusta' is a deflection to buy time and avoid scrutiny
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: outward compliance masking inner panic or mischief; he agrees warmly while knowing he cannot deliver
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon may not be anxious at all but rather relishing the coming comic disaster, finding pleasure in the chaos

beat_states.desire_state: to position herself near Jack/Ernest rather than under her mother's direct supervision
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to pursue romantic independence and secure the man she desires, despite her mother's controlling nature
beat_states.obstacle: Lady Bracknell's attempt to manage her physically and socially in the room

affect_state.rationale: Gwendolen is pleased — she is in a room with the man she is interested in. Her certainty about what she wants is fairly high. Her control is moderate; she can resist her mother in small ways but not openly defy her.

social_state.rationale: Subordinate to her mother but asserting quiet independence. Her warmth toward Lady Bracknell is dutiful rather than genuinely affectionate; she is reserving her real warmth for Jack.

known_facts: Jack/Ernest is present or expected
Her mother wishes to control her movements
She is at Algernon's flat for tea
hidden_secrets: She likely already has romantic designs on Jack/Ernest
false_beliefs: She believes Jack's name is Ernest
epistemic_state.rationale: Gwendolen's knowledge is shaped by Jack's deception. Her positioning in the room is a tactical choice based on her awareness of romantic opportunity.

beat_states.defense_state: polite resistance — she uses decorous language ('Thanks, mamma, I'm quite comfortable') to refuse her mother's directive without overt confrontation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: performing filial obedience while enacting quiet rebellion; her comfort is strategic, not physical
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gwendolen's refusal may be purely about asserting independence from her mother rather than specifically positioning herself near Jack

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Good heavens! Lane! Why are there no cucumber sandwiches? I ordered them specially.

utterances.speaker: LANE
utterances.text: There were no cucumbers in the market this morning, sir. I went down twice.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: No cucumbers!

utterances.speaker: LANE
utterances.text: No, sir. Not even for ready money.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: That will do, Lane, thank you.

utterances.speaker: LANE
utterances.text: Thank you, sir.

beat_states.desire_state: To deflect blame for the missing cucumber sandwiches away from himself and onto Lane, preserving his image as a perfect host
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his position as a witty, effortless aristocrat who controls every social situation
beat_states.obstacle: He himself ate all the cucumber sandwiches earlier with Jack, and now Lady Bracknell and Gwendolen have arrived expecting them

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is performing shock and displeasure but is fully in control of the deception; slight anxiety that the ruse might be exposed, but mostly relishing the performative game

social_state.rationale: Exercising full master-servant authority, treating Lane as subordinate to be scolded, with cool dismissiveness in 'That will do, Lane'

known_facts: He and Jack ate all the cucumber sandwiches earlier
Lady Bracknell expects cucumber sandwiches
Lane will play along with any cover story
hidden_secrets: He consumed the sandwiches himself and is fabricating the entire exchange to cover his tracks
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon has complete knowledge of what happened and is deliberately constructing a false narrative for the benefit of anyone overhearing

beat_states.defense_state: Projection — shifting responsibility for his own gluttony onto Lane by performing outrage at an invented failure
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He knows perfectly well he ate the sandwiches himself, yet performs genuine-sounding indignation at their absence
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon may have genuinely forgotten he ate them and is experiencing momentary confusion, though this is far less likely given the comic structure

beat_states.desire_state: To seamlessly support Algernon's deception and fulfill his role as the ideal complicit servant
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his secure position by being the perfectly discreet, unflappable manservant who never exposes his master
beat_states.obstacle: He must perform a convincing lie without explicit instruction, relying on his long-practiced understanding of Algernon's needs — the risk is less improvisation than the moral weight of active complicity versus passive discretion
beat_states.tactic_state: perform — delivers the lie as a seamless piece of servant theater, using the same deadpan propriety that previously served deflection now repurposed for active deception

affect_state.rationale: Lane is almost entirely unruffled; he understands the game instantly, improvises with low emotional investment, and exits with characteristic detachment

social_state.rationale: Occupying the subordinate role fully and correctly, but with a subtle warmth of complicity — he and Algernon share an unspoken understanding

known_facts: Algernon and Jack ate the cucumber sandwiches
Algernon needs a cover story in front of guests
His role requires him to absorb blame without complaint
hidden_secrets: He witnessed Algernon eating the sandwiches and knows the entire charade is a lie
epistemic_state.rationale: Lane has perfect knowledge of the situation and reads Algernon's cue instantly, fabricating a plausible market-shortage excuse with impressive specificity ('not even for ready money')

beat_states.defense_state: Deadpan composure — maintaining absolute servant propriety to make the lie undetectable
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He knows the truth but performs dutiful regret with such conviction that the lie becomes a kind of art form; his 'Thank you, sir' at dismissal carries the same weight as his fabrication
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Lane may genuinely not care about the deception at all and is simply going through mechanical servant motions with no inner life engaged

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I am greatly distressed, Aunt Augusta, about there being no cucumbers, not even for ready money.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: It really makes no matter, Algernon. I had some crumpets with Lady Harbury, who seems to me to be living entirely for pleasure now.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I hear her hair has turned quite gold from grief.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: It certainly has changed its colour. From what cause I, of course, cannot say. Thank you. I’ve quite a treat for you to-night, Algernon. I am going to send you down with Mary Farquhar. She is such a nice woman, and so attentive to her husband. It’s delightful to watch them.

beat_states.desire_state: To deflect blame for the missing cucumber sandwiches and maintain his charming persona with Aunt Augusta
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To live beautifully and irresponsibly while keeping up appearances in society
beat_states.obstacle: He must conceal that he ate the sandwiches himself while performing genuine distress

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is thoroughly enjoying himself — the deception is a game, and his quip about Lady Harbury's hair turning gold 'from grief' shows him at peak wit, fully in command of the social moment

social_state.rationale: Nominally deferential to his aunt as host/nephew but asserting social equality through wit; his sardonic remark about Lady Harbury is a bid for conspiratorial intimacy

known_facts: He ate all the cucumber sandwiches himself
He blamed Lane for their absence
Lady Harbury has dyed her hair blonde after her husband's death
Society gossip about Lady Harbury's behavior
hidden_secrets: He consumed the cucumber sandwiches
He is leading a double life as 'Bunbury'
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon is fully aware of his deception and uses society gossip to redirect conversation away from the sandwiches

beat_states.defense_state: performative sincerity — feigning distress about cucumbers to cover his own gluttony
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is performing grief about the sandwiches while being the one who consumed them; his wit about Lady Harbury's hair reveals he cannot suppress his true nature of irreverent mockery even mid-performance

beat_states.desire_state: To assert her authority over the social occasion and manage Algernon's life, particularly his dinner pairing
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain absolute control over the social order and ensure propriety is upheld according to her standards
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon's irreverent wit subtly challenges her authority and the social codes she enforces

affect_state.rationale: Lady Bracknell is supremely confident, moving briskly from topic to topic with magisterial authority; she is pleased to be orchestrating social arrangements and feels no emotional disturbance

social_state.rationale: She commands the highest status in the room — dismissing topics, pronouncing judgments on Lady Harbury, and assigning Algernon his dinner companion with no consultation; warmth is minimal, replaced by imperious propriety

known_facts: Lady Harbury's hair has changed color
Lady Harbury is now 'living entirely for pleasure' since her husband's death
Mary Farquhar is attentive to her husband
She has authority to arrange dinner pairings
false_beliefs: That Mary Farquhar's attentiveness to her husband is genuinely 'delightful' rather than potentially performative
epistemic_state.rationale: Lady Bracknell operates on surfaces — she knows the facts of society but interprets them through her rigid moral framework, though her careful 'from what cause I cannot say' about the hair suggests more worldly knowledge than she lets on

beat_states.defense_state: strategic obtuseness — she acknowledges Lady Harbury's hair changed color but refuses to acknowledge the implication of dyeing, preserving decorum
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She praises Mary Farquhar for being 'attentive to her husband' as a virtue while her own worldview treats marriage as a social arrangement rather than a matter of feeling; she dismisses the cucumber issue as trivial while the social management of dinner seating is paramount
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Lady Bracknell may be fully aware that Lady Harbury dyed her hair and is performing deliberate ironic discretion rather than genuine ignorance, making her more self-aware and witty than she appears

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I am afraid, Aunt Augusta, I shall have to give up the pleasure of dining with you to-night after all.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: I hope not, Algernon. It would put my table completely out. Your uncle would have to dine upstairs. Fortunately he is accustomed to that.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: It is a great bore, and, I need hardly say, a terrible disappointment to me, but the fact is I have just had a telegram to say that my poor friend Bunbury is very ill again. They seem to think I should be with him.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: It is very strange. This Mr. Bunbury seems to suffer from curiously bad health.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Yes; poor Bunbury is a dreadful invalid.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Well, I must say, Algernon, that I think it is high time that Mr. Bunbury made up his mind whether he was going to live or to die. This shilly-shallying with the question is absurd. Nor do I in any way approve of the modern sympathy with invalids. I consider it morbid. Illness of any kind is hardly a thing to be encouraged in others. Health is the primary duty of life. I am always telling that to your poor uncle, but he never seems to take much notice . . . as far as any improvement in his ailment goes. I should be much obliged if you would ask Mr. Bunbury, from me, to be kind enough not to have a relapse on Saturday, for I rely on you to arrange my music for me. It is my last reception, and one wants something that will encourage conversation, particularly at the end of the season when every one has practically said whatever they had to say, which, in most cases, was probably not much.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I’ll speak to Bunbury, Aunt Augusta, if he is still conscious, and I think I can promise you he’ll be all right by Saturday. Of course the music is a great difficulty. You see, if one plays good music, people don’t listen, and if one plays bad music people don’t talk. But I’ll run over the programme I’ve drawn out, if you will kindly come into the next room for a moment.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Thank you, Algernon. It is very thoughtful of you. I’m sure the programme will be delightful, after a few expurgations. French songs I cannot possibly allow. People always seem to think that they are improper, and either look shocked, which is vulgar, or laugh, which is worse. But German sounds a thoroughly respectable language, and indeed, I believe is so. Gwendolen, you will accompany me.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Certainly, mamma.

beat_states.desire_state: To extract himself from Lady Bracknell's dinner so he can be free for the evening, while maintaining the Bunbury fiction
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To preserve maximum personal freedom while maintaining social respectability
beat_states.obstacle: Lady Bracknell's insistence on his attendance and her growing suspicion about Bunbury

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is thoroughly enjoying himself. He's confident in his Bunbury ruse, performing distress while inwardly delighted. He smoothly pivots to managing Lady Bracknell by offering to discuss the music programme — redirecting her attention from Bunbury to something she cares about more.

social_state.rationale: Nominally deferential to his aunt but subtly controlling the interaction. His warmth is performative — the 'poor friend Bunbury' language is practiced social lubrication. He manages to both escape and remain indispensable.

known_facts: Bunbury is entirely fictitious
Lady Bracknell is suspicious of Bunbury's recurring illnesses
He needs to get Lady Bracknell out of the room so Jack can be alone with Gwendolen
He has leverage because Lady Bracknell needs him to arrange her music
hidden_secrets: Bunbury does not exist
He is deliberately engineering Jack's time alone with Gwendolen
He has no telegram
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon is the most epistemically privileged character in this beat — he knows the full picture and is manipulating everyone. His offer to discuss the programme in the next room is a calculated move to leave Jack and Gwendolen alone.

beat_states.defense_state: Elaborate fabrication — using the invented invalid Bunbury as a shield against social obligation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Performs regret and disappointment while feeling relief and amusement at his own cleverness
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon may also genuinely enjoy the sparring with Lady Bracknell as entertainment rather than purely strategic maneuvering

beat_states.desire_state: To remain in the room with Jack/Ernest and resist being removed by her mother
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To pursue her romantic destiny with Ernest and assert her own will against her mother's control
beat_states.obstacle: Her mother's direct command to accompany her out of the room

affect_state.rationale: Gwendolen is frustrated but contained. She has been given no choice and must comply. Her two-word response masks what is likely keen disappointment at being separated from Ernest. Low control — she is entirely subject to her mother's will in this moment.

social_state.rationale: Subordinate to her mother in every way in this beat. Her warmth is dutiful but restrained. She barely registers as a social agent here — reduced to two words of obedience.

known_facts: Ernest/Jack is in the room
Her mother is taking her away from him
Algernon is arranging to discuss music in the next room
hidden_secrets: She is already determined to marry Ernest
Her compliance is strategic — she knows there will be other opportunities
false_beliefs: Jack's name is Ernest
epistemic_state.rationale: Gwendolen knows what she wants romantically but is unaware of Jack's true identity. Her brief reply conceals a rich inner life of romantic calculation.

beat_states.defense_state: Surface compliance masking internal resistance
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Says 'Certainly, mamma' with apparent obedience while almost certainly wanting to stay with Jack
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gwendolen may trust that Algernon is deliberately creating an opportunity for her to be with Ernest later, given their apparent alliance

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Charming day it has been, Miss Fairfax.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Pray don’t talk to me about the weather, Mr. Worthing. Whenever people talk to me about the weather, I always feel quite certain that they mean something else. And that makes me so nervous.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I _do_ mean something else.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: I thought so. In fact, I am never wrong.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: And I would like to be allowed to take advantage of Lady Bracknell’s temporary absence . . .

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: I would certainly advise you to do so. Mamma has a way of coming back suddenly into a room that I have often had to speak to her about.

beat_states.desire_state: To steer Jack toward an immediate proposal by removing every obstacle and excuse for delay
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To orchestrate and control her own romantic destiny, specifically to marry a man named Ernest
beat_states.obstacle: Social convention that requires the man to lead the courtship, and her mother's controlling presence

affect_state.rationale: Gwendolen is thoroughly enjoying herself and is in near-total control. She sees through Jack's opening gambit instantly, redirects him, and practically stage-manages the proposal. Her claimed nervousness is performative — she is deeply certain of what's happening and what she wants.

social_state.rationale: Gwendolen holds the higher status — she is the one granting permission, coaching Jack, and setting the terms. Her warmth is genuine but filtered through aristocratic wit and control.

known_facts: Jack (as Ernest) intends to propose or declare himself
Her mother will return soon and likely interrupt
She is attracted to this man she knows as Ernest Worthing
hidden_secrets: She has already decided she will accept him, largely because of his name
false_beliefs: She believes his name is Ernest
She believes the name Ernest is a reliable indicator of character
epistemic_state.rationale: Gwendolen reads the situation with forensic clarity — she knows exactly what Jack means to say. Her blind spot is the name: she has built an entire romantic ideology around 'Ernest' without knowing it's a fiction.

beat_states.defense_state: Performing supreme self-assurance ('I am never wrong') as armor against any vulnerability in the romantic encounter
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She claims nervousness about hidden meanings while simultaneously demonstrating total command of the conversation and its subtext
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: A slightly less controlling reading: Gwendolen is genuinely excited and her bravado masks real romantic nervousness, but the text strongly supports her as the orchestrator here.

beat_states.desire_state: To declare his romantic feelings to Gwendolen before Lady Bracknell returns
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure love and legitimacy by winning Gwendolen's hand
beat_states.obstacle: Social propriety, Lady Bracknell's imminent return, and his own nervousness about broaching the subject

affect_state.rationale: Jack is excited and hopeful but deeply nervous. He fumbles with weather talk, revealing his lack of control. Gwendolen's encouragement raises his valence but he remains exposed, racing against time.

social_state.rationale: Jack is in the supplicant position — asking to 'be allowed' to take advantage of the moment. He defers to Gwendolen's permission, positioning himself below her. His warmth is high as he pursues romantic connection.

known_facts: Lady Bracknell is temporarily out of the room
Gwendolen appears receptive to his attention
He must act quickly before Lady Bracknell returns
hidden_secrets: His real name is Jack, not Ernest
His origins are obscure — he was found in a handbag
He has invented a brother named Ernest as a cover for his London life
false_beliefs: He believes Gwendolen's interest is in him as a person rather than primarily in the name Ernest
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack is operating under the false identity of Ernest Worthing. He doesn't yet know that Gwendolen's fascination is fixated on the name itself.

beat_states.defense_state: Using weather small talk as a safe opening gambit to test the waters before committing
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He desperately wants to speak openly but instinctively reaches for the safest, most banal topic imaginable

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Miss Fairfax, ever since I met you I have admired you more than any girl . . . I have ever met since . . . I met you.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Yes, I am quite well aware of the fact. And I often wish that in public, at any rate, you had been more demonstrative. For me you have always had an irresistible fascination. Even before I met you I was far from indifferent to you. We live, as I hope you know, Mr. Worthing, in an age of ideals. The fact is constantly mentioned in the more expensive monthly magazines, and has reached the provincial pulpits, I am told; and my ideal has always been to love some one of the name of Ernest. There is something in that name that inspires absolute confidence. The moment Algernon first mentioned to me that he had a friend called Ernest, I knew I was destined to love you.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: You really love me, Gwendolen?

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Passionately!

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Darling! You don’t know how happy you’ve made me.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: My own Ernest!

beat_states.desire_state: to stage-manage a perfect romantic proposal that conforms to her preconceived ideal
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to live a life shaped entirely by aesthetic and social ideals, controlling her own romantic narrative
beat_states.obstacle: Jack's inarticulateness threatens to make the moment less than ideal; societal propriety could interrupt

affect_state.rationale: Gwendolen is supremely confident and delighted. She has been waiting for this moment and controls its every dimension. Her certainty is near-total — she knew she would love 'Ernest' before meeting him. Vulnerability is low because she has armored herself with ideology.

social_state.rationale: She holds the higher status throughout — she dictates the terms of the courtship, corrects Jack's approach, and delivers the definitive 'Passionately!' She is warm but in a sovereign, bestowing manner rather than an egalitarian one.

known_facts: The man before her is called Ernest (as far as she knows)
Algernon introduced him as Ernest
She has always wanted to love someone named Ernest
Jack/Ernest admires her
hidden_secrets: She may be aware that her mother would not approve of the match
false_beliefs: His name is actually Ernest
The name Ernest guarantees trustworthiness and character
epistemic_state.rationale: Gwendolen operates on a false premise — that the name Ernest is real and that it is a reliable index of character. Her entire romantic framework is built on a fabrication she doesn't suspect.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — she transforms genuine feeling into a theoretical framework about ideals and names, protecting herself from raw emotional exposure
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She claims passionate, spontaneous love but it is entirely predetermined by an abstract fixation on a name — the passion is real but the object is a signifier, not a person
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gwendolen could be performing knowingly — deploying absurd logic about names as a witty power play to test Jack's seriousness, rather than genuinely believing the name matters. But the play's subsequent acts confirm she is earnest about Ernest.

beat_states.desire_state: to declare his love for Gwendolen and secure her affection
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to establish a legitimate identity and win love through his Ernest persona
beat_states.obstacle: his nervousness makes him inarticulate, and his entire courtship is built on the false name Ernest

affect_state.rationale: He is elated by Gwendolen's reciprocation but stumbles over his words at the start, revealing nervous vulnerability. His certainty rises when she says 'Passionately!' but he remains aware at some level that the foundation is precarious.

social_state.rationale: He begins the beat as supplicant, fumbling his declaration. Gwendolen controls the terms of the proposal. He is emotionally open and warm, fully surrendering to the romantic moment.

known_facts: His real name is Jack, not Ernest
He uses Ernest as a fictional identity in town
Gwendolen appears to reciprocate his feelings
hidden_secrets: His name is not actually Ernest
He invented Ernest as a cover for his double life
false_beliefs: He may believe Gwendolen's love transcends the name and is truly for him
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack hears Gwendolen's fixation on the name Ernest but is too swept up in happiness to register the danger. He doesn't yet confront the implication that the name is the condition of her love.

beat_states.defense_state: conceeal — suppressing the truth about his real name to maintain the romantic moment
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: genuine joy at being loved collides with the knowledge that he is loved for a name that isn't his
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Jack may already sense the trap — his 'You really love me, Gwendolen?' could be a moment of testing whether the love is for him or the name, but the text plays it as sincere romantic disbelief.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: But you don’t really mean to say that you couldn’t love me if my name wasn’t Ernest?

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: But your name is Ernest.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Yes, I know it is. But supposing it was something else? Do you mean to say you couldn’t love me then?

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Ah! that is clearly a metaphysical speculation, and like most metaphysical speculations has very little reference at all to the actual facts of real life, as we know them.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Personally, darling, to speak quite candidly, I don’t much care about the name of Ernest . . . I don’t think the name suits me at all.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: It suits you perfectly. It is a divine name. It has a music of its own. It produces vibrations.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Well, really, Gwendolen, I must say that I think there are lots of other much nicer names. I think Jack, for instance, a charming name.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Jack? . . . No, there is very little music in the name Jack, if any at all, indeed. It does not thrill. It produces absolutely no vibrations . . . I have known several Jacks, and they all, without exception, were more than usually plain. Besides, Jack is a notorious domesticity for John! And I pity any woman who is married to a man called John. She would probably never be allowed to know the entrancing pleasure of a single moment’s solitude. The only really safe name is Ernest.

beat_states.desire_state: to affirm and celebrate her romantic ideal — being engaged to a man named Ernest — and to dismiss any challenge to that ideal
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to live out a life shaped by aesthetic ideals and romantic destiny, with style taking precedence over substance
beat_states.obstacle: Jack's oddly persistent hypothetical questions about other names, which threaten the perfection of her romantic narrative

affect_state.rationale: She is supremely confident, enjoying the role of newly engaged woman and pronouncing with absolute authority on names. She shows no vulnerability — the hypothetical doesn't genuinely trouble her because she refuses to take it seriously.

social_state.rationale: She holds the higher status position throughout — she is the authority on what is acceptable, delivering verdicts with magisterial certainty. Her warmth is moderate; she is affectionate toward 'Ernest' but ruthlessly dismissive of 'Jack' and its associations.

known_facts: She believes the man before her is named Ernest Worthing
She has long had an ideal of marrying someone named Ernest
She has just accepted his proposal
She knows several Jacks, all of whom she considers plain
false_beliefs: The man proposing to her is actually named Ernest
epistemic_state.rationale: Gwendolen operates on entirely false premises about Jack's identity. She has no hidden agenda — her beliefs are sincere, just wrong. She takes the surface presentation at face value.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — reframes Jack's sincere anxiety as abstract 'metaphysical speculation' unworthy of serious engagement
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She claims to love this man deeply yet her love is entirely contingent on a superficial attribute (his name), revealing that her romantic feeling is more aesthetic performance than genuine connection
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: A subtler reading: Gwendolen may be performing exaggerated fixation on the name as a kind of witty social game, half-aware it is absurd but committed to the bit as a display of personality — making her less naive and more deliberately theatrical.

beat_states.desire_state: to test whether Gwendolen could love him under his real name, Jack, and prepare the ground for revealing his true identity
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to win Gwendolen's love authentically and escape the double life he has constructed
beat_states.obstacle: Gwendolen's obsessive fixation on the name Ernest, which makes his real name a disqualifying liability

affect_state.rationale: Growing anxiety as each probe reveals how deeply Gwendolen is attached to the name Ernest. He's losing control of the situation — his attempts to float 'Jack' are being demolished, leaving him increasingly exposed and desperate.

social_state.rationale: He uses endearments ('darling') and maintains affectionate warmth, but Gwendolen's confident pronouncements place her in the dominant position; he is the supplicant trying to negotiate terms.

known_facts: His real name is Jack, not Ernest
He has been using Ernest as a fictional identity in London
Gwendolen has accepted his proposal believing him to be Ernest
Gwendolen has declared her fixation on the name Ernest
hidden_secrets: He is not Ernest — his real name is Jack
He invented a brother named Ernest as a pretext for his London visits
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack is fully aware of the deception and is now learning in real time just how catastrophic the truth could be. He possesses all the relevant information but cannot deploy it.

beat_states.defense_state: hedging — approaches the dangerous truth obliquely through hypotheticals rather than direct confession
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He wants honesty and authentic love but cannot bring himself to actually confess the lie; each attempt to nudge her toward accepting 'Jack' is met with categorical rejection, increasing his panic

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Gwendolen, I must get christened at once—I mean we must get married at once. There is no time to be lost.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Married, Mr. Worthing?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Well . . . surely. You know that I love you, and you led me to believe, Miss Fairfax, that you were not absolutely indifferent to me.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: I adore you. But you haven’t proposed to me yet. Nothing has been said at all about marriage. The subject has not even been touched on.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Well . . . may I propose to you now?

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: I think it would be an admirable opportunity. And to spare you any possible disappointment, Mr. Worthing, I think it only fair to tell you quite frankly before-hand that I am fully determined to accept you.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Gwendolen!

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Yes, Mr. Worthing, what have you got to say to me?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: You know what I have got to say to you.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Yes, but you don’t say it.

beat_states.desire_state: to orchestrate and savor the perfect formal proposal from the man she believes is named Ernest
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to live a life of romantic idealism on her own terms, controlling the narrative of her love story
beat_states.obstacle: Jack keeps trying to skip ahead or summarize rather than performing the ritual she demands

affect_state.rationale: She is thoroughly enjoying herself, completely in command. She knows the outcome, has announced it, and is now savoring the process. Almost no vulnerability — she has pre-empted any possibility of disappointment by declaring the result in advance.

social_state.rationale: She holds all the power in this exchange — she is the director, he the performer. Her warmth is genuine but measured; she dispenses it strategically through teasing encouragement.

known_facts: She believes his name is Ernest
He loves her
She has always wanted to marry someone named Ernest
She intends to accept his proposal
hidden_secrets: The depth to which the name Ernest is the foundation of her attraction — she may sense this is shallow but refuses to examine it
false_beliefs: His name is Ernest Worthing
The proposal is a genuine free romantic act rather than a performance she has fully scripted
epistemic_state.rationale: Gwendolen operates with supreme confidence built on a false premise. She believes she knows everything she needs to know but is completely wrong about his name — the one thing she cares most about.

beat_states.defense_state: ritualization — she converts genuine feeling into managed ceremony, maintaining power through formality
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She has already decided to accept him and tells him so, yet insists he go through the full performance — she wants both spontaneity and total control simultaneously
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: A more generous reading might see Gwendolen as genuinely trying to help Jack overcome his nervousness by removing the suspense, rather than purely exercising control

beat_states.desire_state: to propose marriage to Gwendolen and secure her acceptance immediately
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to win Gwendolen's love and establish a legitimate, respectable identity
beat_states.obstacle: Gwendolen insists on the formal ritual of proposal despite having already indicated acceptance — she controls the pace and form

affect_state.rationale: He is elated that Gwendolen adores him but deeply flustered — she keeps redirecting him, and he's lost control of the scene. His slip about christening betrays high anxiety. He knows he must perform the proposal correctly but keeps stumbling.

social_state.rationale: Gwendolen has taken the dominant position, directing how and when he may propose. He is supplicant, earnest, warm but subordinate in this exchange.

known_facts: Gwendolen has said she adores him
Gwendolen has told him she will accept his proposal
Gwendolen is fixated on the name Ernest
His real name is Jack, not Ernest
hidden_secrets: His real name is Jack Worthing, not Ernest
He has been living a double life using the name Ernest in town
false_beliefs: He seems to believe that getting christened could solve the name problem quickly
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack possesses the crucial secret that undermines the entire basis of Gwendolen's attraction. His slip about christening reveals he's already scheming to resolve the name deception, but he believes a quick fix is possible.

beat_states.defense_state: deflect — his Freudian slip about christening reveals his anxiety about the name issue, which he quickly covers
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is simultaneously relieved that she adores him and panicked that his real name isn't Ernest, driving urgency that reads as romantic eagerness but is actually fear of exposure

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Gwendolen, will you marry me?

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Of course I will, darling. How long you have been about it! I am afraid you have had very little experience in how to propose.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: My own one, I have never loved any one in the world but you.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Yes, but men often propose for practice. I know my brother Gerald does. All my girl-friends tell me so. What wonderfully blue eyes you have, Ernest! They are quite, quite, blue. I hope you will always look at me just like that, especially when there are other people present.

beat_states.desire_state: to orchestrate a perfect, stylish engagement on her own terms while enjoying the triumph of being proposed to
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to live a life of impeccable romantic style and social sophistication, shaped entirely by her own aesthetic ideals
beat_states.obstacle: Jack's clumsy inexperience in proposing threatens to undermine the elegance of the moment

affect_state.rationale: Gwendolen is delighted but never loses her poise. She accepts with the certainty of someone who already decided the outcome. Her arousal is moderated by her self-possession. She exposes almost no vulnerability, immediately pivoting to critique his technique and stage-manage his gaze for public consumption.

social_state.rationale: Gwendolen holds clear dominance — she evaluates, instructs, and conditions Jack even in the moment of acceptance. Her warmth is present ('darling,' admiring his eyes) but filtered through an imperious social manner.

known_facts: She believes his name is Ernest
She has always wanted to marry someone named Ernest
Her brother Gerald proposes to women for practice
She considers herself an expert on romantic matters
hidden_secrets: She may be aware that her mother will oppose the match
false_beliefs: That the man before her is actually named Ernest
That the name Ernest guarantees the qualities she idealizes
epistemic_state.rationale: Gwendolen operates with supreme confidence based on fundamentally false information. Her worldview is constructed from social axioms (names determine character) rather than empirical knowledge of this man.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualizing and aestheticizing the romantic moment to maintain composure and dominance
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: genuinely moved by the proposal while simultaneously treating it as a performance to be critiqued and managed
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gwendolen's instruction about looking at her 'especially when there are other people present' could suggest deeper insecurity about holding his attention, masked as social choreography.

beat_states.desire_state: to secure Gwendolen's acceptance of his proposal and cement the engagement
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to win Gwendolen's love and establish a legitimate, respectable identity
beat_states.obstacle: the looming deception about his name (Ernest vs. Jack) and his uncertain origins

affect_state.rationale: Jack is ecstatic at Gwendolen's acceptance but remains anxious underneath — he knows his name is not Ernest, the very foundation of her attraction. His declarations of singular devotion are sincere, but he has little control over Gwendolen's terms for the relationship.

social_state.rationale: Despite being the proposer, Jack is in a subordinate position — Gwendolen has dictated the terms of acceptability throughout the courtship. His warmth is genuine and unguarded.

known_facts: His real name is Jack, not Ernest
Gwendolen has accepted his proposal
Gwendolen is fixated on the name Ernest as essential to her love
He needs to get christened as Ernest to maintain the deception
hidden_secrets: His name is actually Jack Worthing
He invented the persona of Ernest for his London life
He was found in a handbag at Victoria station and has no known parentage
false_beliefs: That getting christened 'Ernest' will resolve the problem permanently
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack possesses crucial information about his double life that Gwendolen lacks. His false belief that a simple christening solves his identity crisis reveals his naïveté about the depth of the deception.

beat_states.defense_state: concealing his true identity behind the persona of 'Ernest' to maintain the romantic moment
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: experiencing genuine romantic elation while knowing his entire identity is a fabrication that could unravel at any moment

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Mr. Worthing! Rise, sir, from this semi-recumbent posture. It is most indecorous.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Mamma! I must beg you to retire. This is no place for you. Besides, Mr. Worthing has not quite finished yet.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Finished what, may I ask?

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: I am engaged to Mr. Worthing, mamma.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Pardon me, you are not engaged to any one. When you do become engaged to some one, I, or your father, should his health permit him, will inform you of the fact. An engagement should come on a young girl as a surprise, pleasant or unpleasant, as the case may be. It is hardly a matter that she could be allowed to arrange for herself . . . And now I have a few questions to put to you, Mr. Worthing. While I am making these inquiries, you, Gwendolen, will wait for me below in the carriage.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Mamma!

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: In the carriage, Gwendolen! Gwendolen, the carriage!

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Yes, mamma.

beat_states.desire_state: To defend her engagement to Jack and resist her mother's authority for as long as possible
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To achieve a romantic destiny on her own terms — specifically, to marry a man named Ernest
beat_states.obstacle: Lady Bracknell's overwhelming social and parental authority

affect_state.rationale: Buoyed by the thrill of having secured her engagement, but increasingly deflated as her mother reasserts dominance. Her certainty about the engagement remains but her control over the situation collapses rapidly.

social_state.rationale: Attempts to claim adult status and social parity with her mother but is decisively outranked. Warmth toward Jack lingers in her defense of the engagement, but she shows competitive coolness toward her mother.

known_facts: Jack has proposed and she has accepted
Her mother disapproves and will interrogate Jack
She must leave the room
hidden_secrets: She already knows she will find ways to circumvent her mother's authority — her compliance is tactical, not genuine
false_beliefs: That Jack's name is actually Ernest
epistemic_state.rationale: She is aware of the social dynamics but operates under the central false belief about Jack's identity. Her quick compliance suggests she knows strategic retreat is wiser than open war.

beat_states.defense_state: Mirroring her mother's imperious tone to claim equal standing, then retreating into compliance when overpowered
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She boldly declares her independence ('This is no place for you') but ultimately capitulates with 'Yes, mamma' — her rebellion is performative, not structural
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gwendolen's initial defiance could be read as entirely performative — a display for Jack's benefit to demonstrate her devotion, with no real expectation of overriding her mother

beat_states.desire_state: To seize total control of the situation, quash the unauthorized engagement, and interrogate Jack on her own terms
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain absolute authority over her daughter's marital prospects and uphold social order
beat_states.obstacle: Gwendolen's defiance and the fait accompli of a proposal already accepted

affect_state.rationale: Indignant but fully in command. She is offended by the impropriety but confident in her authority to override it. Minimal vulnerability — she holds all the social power.

social_state.rationale: Exercises supreme hierarchical dominance over both Jack and Gwendolen. Coldly authoritative, treating the engagement as an administrative irregularity rather than a human moment.

known_facts: Jack has proposed to Gwendolen
Gwendolen considers herself engaged
She has not yet vetted Jack as a suitor
Jack's surname is Worthing
false_beliefs: That Gwendolen will ultimately defer to her authority in all matters of marriage
epistemic_state.rationale: She has walked in on the proposal and grasps the situation immediately. She assumes her parental authority is absolute and unchallengeable.

beat_states.defense_state: Ritualized authority — retreating into the role of matriarchal gatekeeper to neutralize any emotional reality in the room
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She insists engagement should come as a 'surprise' to a young girl while simultaneously demanding total parental control over it — the absurdity she cannot see

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: You can take a seat, Mr. Worthing.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Thank you, Lady Bracknell, I prefer standing.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: I feel bound to tell you that you are not down on my list of eligible young men, although I have the same list as the dear Duchess of Bolton has. We work together, in fact. However, I am quite ready to enter your name, should your answers be what a really affectionate mother requires. Do you smoke?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Well, yes, I must admit I smoke.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: I am glad to hear it. A man should always have an occupation of some kind. There are far too many idle men in London as it is. How old are you?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Twenty-nine.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: A very good age to be married at. I have always been of opinion that a man who desires to get married should know either everything or nothing. Which do you know?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I know nothing, Lady Bracknell.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: I am pleased to hear it. I do not approve of anything that tampers with natural ignorance. Ignorance is like a delicate exotic fruit; touch it and the bloom is gone. The whole theory of modern education is radically unsound. Fortunately in England, at any rate, education produces no effect whatsoever. If it did, it would prove a serious danger to the upper classes, and probably lead to acts of violence in Grosvenor Square. What is your income?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Between seven and eight thousand a year.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: In land, or in investments?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: In investments, chiefly.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: That is satisfactory. What between the duties expected of one during one’s lifetime, and the duties exacted from one after one’s death, land has ceased to be either a profit or a pleasure. It gives one position, and prevents one from keeping it up. That’s all that can be said about land.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I have a country house with some land, of course, attached to it, about fifteen hundred acres, I believe; but I don’t depend on that for my real income. In fact, as far as I can make out, the poachers are the only people who make anything out of it.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: A country house! How many bedrooms? Well, that point can be cleared up afterwards. You have a town house, I hope? A girl with a simple, unspoiled nature, like Gwendolen, could hardly be expected to reside in the country.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Well, I own a house in Belgrave Square, but it is let by the year to Lady Bloxham. Of course, I can get it back whenever I like, at six months’ notice.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Lady Bloxham? I don’t know her.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Oh, she goes about very little. She is a lady considerably advanced in years.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Ah, nowadays that is no guarantee of respectability of character. What number in Belgrave Square?

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: The unfashionable side. I thought there was something. However, that could easily be altered.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Do you mean the fashion, or the side?

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Both, if necessary, I presume. What are your politics?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Well, I am afraid I really have none. I am a Liberal Unionist.

beat_states.desire_state: To pass Lady Bracknell's interrogation and win approval to marry Gwendolen
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure love and belonging through marriage to Gwendolen, overcoming his uncertain origins
beat_states.obstacle: Lady Bracknell's withering social scrutiny and his own awareness that some of his answers may not fully satisfy her standards

affect_state.rationale: Jack is anxious but holding composure. Each answer feels like a test he might fail. His initial refusal to sit signals defiance, but he quickly submits to the interrogation format. His wit is a nervous coping mechanism — he's genuinely at Lady Bracknell's mercy.

social_state.rationale: Despite his wealth and social standing, he is in the inferior position of supplicant to Lady Bracknell's role as gatekeeper. He tries to be agreeable and pleasant while maintaining some dignity.

known_facts: He earns seven to eight thousand a year, chiefly in investments
He owns a country house with 1500 acres
He owns 149 Belgrave Square, currently let to Lady Bloxham
He is twenty-nine years old
He smokes
He is a Liberal Unionist
Gwendolen has accepted his proposal
hidden_secrets: His true origins — he was found in a handbag at Victoria Station
His identity as 'Ernest' is fabricated
He leads a double life as Jack in the country and Ernest in town
false_beliefs: He believes that if he gives satisfactory material answers, he will win Lady Bracknell's approval
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack is presenting his best social credentials, unaware that the real disqualifying factor — his mysterious parentage — has not yet come up. He thinks wealth and property are the main hurdles.

beat_states.defense_state: Deflection through self-deprecating wit — making light of his deficiencies (poachers joke, 'fashion or the side?' quip) to charm his way through the examination
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He wants to appear dignified and independent ('I prefer standing') while simultaneously being forced into the submissive position of a candidate being vetted like a job applicant
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Jack's refusal to sit and occasional witticisms ('Do you mean the fashion, or the side?') could be read as passive-aggressive resistance to Lady Bracknell's authority rather than nervous deflection — a man who feels entitled to Gwendolen and resents being examined.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Oh, they count as Tories. They dine with us. Or come in the evening, at any rate. Now to minor matters. Are your parents living?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I have lost both my parents.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: To lose one parent, Mr. Worthing, may be regarded as a misfortune; to lose both looks like carelessness. Who was your father? He was evidently a man of some wealth. Was he born in what the Radical papers call the purple of commerce, or did he rise from the ranks of the aristocracy?

beat_states.desire_state: To survive the interrogation and provide answers that will satisfy Lady Bracknell and secure permission to marry Gwendolen
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To win Gwendolen and achieve the legitimate identity and belonging he has always lacked
beat_states.obstacle: Lady Bracknell's relentless questioning is steering toward the one subject — his origins — where he is most vulnerable and least able to provide satisfactory answers

affect_state.rationale: Growing anxiety as the questions approach his deepest wound — his unknown parentage. He is losing control of the conversation. The flippant dismissal of his orphanhood stings but he cannot protest. High vulnerability as exposure approaches.

social_state.rationale: He is in the subordinate position of a supplicant being examined. He maintains polite deference and warmth toward the woman who holds his future in her hands.

known_facts: He was found in a handbag at Victoria Station
He does not know who his biological parents were
Lady Bracknell expects clear answers about lineage
hidden_secrets: He is a foundling with no known parentage
His identity as 'Ernest' in town is a fabrication
He was adopted by Mr. Thomas Cardew
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack knows the devastating truth about his origins and can feel the interrogation closing in on it. He knows each question brings him closer to having to reveal what will almost certainly disqualify him.

beat_states.defense_state: Minimization — he states the bare fact of his parents' loss in the most understated way possible, trying to move past the topic quickly
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He must present himself as a respectable man of clear lineage while knowing his origins are foundling and unknowable — each question draws him closer to exposure
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Jack might be playing the scene with more comic detachment than genuine anxiety, delivering 'I have lost both my parents' as a deadpan setup for Lady Bracknell's famous line — in which case his vulnerability would be lower and his complicity in the comedy higher.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I am afraid I really don’t know. The fact is, Lady Bracknell, I said I had lost my parents. It would be nearer the truth to say that my parents seem to have lost me . . . I don’t actually know who I am by birth. I was . . . well, I was found.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Found!

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: The late Mr. Thomas Cardew, an old gentleman of a very charitable and kindly disposition, found me, and gave me the name of Worthing, because he happened to have a first-class ticket for Worthing in his pocket at the time. Worthing is a place in Sussex. It is a seaside resort.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Where did the charitable gentleman who had a first-class ticket for this seaside resort find you?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: In a hand-bag.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: A hand-bag?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Yes, Lady Bracknell. I was in a hand-bag—a somewhat large, black leather hand-bag, with handles to it—an ordinary hand-bag in fact.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: In what locality did this Mr. James, or Thomas, Cardew come across this ordinary hand-bag?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: In the cloak-room at Victoria Station. It was given to him in mistake for his own.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: The cloak-room at Victoria Station?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Yes. The Brighton line.

beat_states.desire_state: To confess his uncertain origins honestly while maintaining enough dignity to remain a viable suitor for Gwendolen
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To win Gwendolen's hand and secure social legitimacy despite his foundling status
beat_states.obstacle: His origins are absurd and socially disqualifying — he literally cannot explain himself without sounding ridiculous

affect_state.rationale: Jack is fully exposed here — his deepest secret and shame (his unknown parentage) is being dragged out detail by excruciating detail. He has no control over how this information lands. Each of Lady Bracknell's incredulous echoes increases his anxiety.

social_state.rationale: His status plummets with every revelation. He's a supplicant being interrogated, and each answer lowers him further. He retains a faint warmth through his earnest, almost naive honesty.

known_facts: He was found in a hand-bag at Victoria Station, Brighton line
Thomas Cardew found him and named him after a train ticket destination
He does not know his biological parents
These facts are socially catastrophic in this context
hidden_secrets: His double life as Ernest in town and Jack in the country
He has already proposed to Gwendolen and been accepted
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack knows his own history completely and understands its absurdity, but he cannot fabricate a better one under Lady Bracknell's direct questioning.

beat_states.defense_state: Over-specification of mundane details (the hand-bag's color, handles, the Brighton line) as a way to normalize the grotesque absurdity of his origins
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is deeply embarrassed yet compelled to be completely honest; each new detail he provides makes his case worse, yet he cannot stop supplying them
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Jack could be subtly enjoying the absurdity of the situation — Wilde gives him increasingly precise, deadpan details ('The Brighton line') that suggest a comic performer's instinct rather than pure mortification.

beat_states.desire_state: To determine whether Jack Worthing is an acceptable match for Gwendolen by establishing his social credentials
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain absolute control over her family's social standing and alliances through rigorous gatekeeping
beat_states.obstacle: Jack's answers are progressively more shocking and socially impossible, making her assessment increasingly definitive in the negative

affect_state.rationale: Each revelation increases her negative reaction and arousal. She is increasingly certain this match is impossible. She maintains near-total control of the interaction through her interrogation technique. She is invulnerable — this is his shame, not hers.

social_state.rationale: She holds supreme status as judge and arbiter. Each echo-question is a cold, withering blow. She extends no warmth or sympathy for his unfortunate origins.

known_facts: Jack does not know his parents
He was found in a hand-bag
He was found at Victoria Station cloak-room, Brighton line
He was named after a train ticket
Thomas Cardew adopted him
false_beliefs: She may believe Jack's name is Ernest Worthing
epistemic_state.rationale: She is rapidly accumulating damning information. Each new fact confirms the impossibility of this match in her worldview. She has no hidden agenda beyond her open one.

beat_states.defense_state: Echoing his words back with incredulous emphasis as a form of social shaming — repeating 'Found!', 'A hand-bag?', 'The cloak-room at Victoria Station?' to weaponize his own words against him
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She is genuinely horrified yet performing horror; her outrage is both authentic class revulsion and theatrical social ritual
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Lady Bracknell's escalating echoes could be read as fascination rather than pure horror — a woman who collects social grotesqueries with a connoisseur's relish, even as she condemns them.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: The line is immaterial. Mr. Worthing, I confess I feel somewhat bewildered by what you have just told me. To be born, or at any rate bred, in a hand-bag, whether it had handles or not, seems to me to display a contempt for the ordinary decencies of family life that reminds one of the worst excesses of the French Revolution. And I presume you know what that unfortunate movement led to? As for the particular locality in which the hand-bag was found, a cloak-room at a railway station might serve to conceal a social indiscretion—has probably, indeed, been used for that purpose before now—but it could hardly be regarded as an assured basis for a recognised position in good society.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: May I ask you then what you would advise me to do? I need hardly say I would do anything in the world to ensure Gwendolen’s happiness.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: I would strongly advise you, Mr. Worthing, to try and acquire some relations as soon as possible, and to make a definite effort to produce at any rate one parent, of either sex, before the season is quite over.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Well, I don’t see how I could possibly manage to do that. I can produce the hand-bag at any moment. It is in my dressing-room at home. I really think that should satisfy you, Lady Bracknell.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Me, sir! What has it to do with me? You can hardly imagine that I and Lord Bracknell would dream of allowing our only daughter—a girl brought up with the utmost care—to marry into a cloak-room, and form an alliance with a parcel? Good morning, Mr. Worthing!

beat_states.desire_state: To find any possible way to satisfy Lady Bracknell's requirements and win permission to marry Gwendolen
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure love and belonging through marriage to Gwendolen, overcoming the foundling identity that has always haunted him
beat_states.obstacle: Lady Bracknell's immovable class prejudice and his inability to produce parents or relations

affect_state.rationale: Jack is increasingly desperate and powerless. His offer of the hand-bag shows he's grasping at straws. The final dismissal ('Good morning') lands as a devastating blow. He has lost all control over the situation and his future happiness.

social_state.rationale: Jack has been systematically stripped of status throughout the interview, reduced from a wealthy gentleman to a foundling associated with luggage. His warmth—his genuine concern for Gwendolen's happiness—is his last card, and it fails entirely.

known_facts: He was found in a hand-bag at Victoria Station by Mr. Thomas Cardew
He has no knowledge of his biological parents
Lady Bracknell will not consent to the marriage
Gwendolen loves him (or at least his name)
hidden_secrets: His double life as Ernest in town and Jack in the country
Ernest is a fictional brother he invented
false_beliefs: That producing the hand-bag might somehow satisfy Lady Bracknell's requirements
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack knows his origins are an insurmountable problem but doesn't yet understand that Lady Bracknell's rejection is truly final. His secret double life remains hidden and irrelevant to this particular crisis.

beat_states.defense_state: Naive literalism—offering the actual hand-bag as though a physical object could substitute for lineage, deflecting the emotional devastation with practical absurdity
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is both genuinely desperate and earnest in his appeals, yet his suggestion to produce the hand-bag reveals either comic obliviousness or a subconscious recognition that her criteria are themselves absurd
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Jack's offer of the hand-bag could be read as a moment of defiant absurdist mockery—matching Lady Bracknell's absurd demands with an equally absurd solution—rather than genuine naivety

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Good morning! For goodness’ sake don’t play that ghastly tune, Algy. How idiotic you are!

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Didn’t it go off all right, old boy? You don’t mean to say Gwendolen refused you? I know it is a way she has. She is always refusing people. I think it is most ill-natured of her.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Oh, Gwendolen is as right as a trivet. As far as she is concerned, we are engaged. Her mother is perfectly unbearable. Never met such a Gorgon . . . I don’t really know what a Gorgon is like, but I am quite sure that Lady Bracknell is one. In any case, she is a monster, without being a myth, which is rather unfair . . . I beg your pardon, Algy, I suppose I shouldn’t talk about your own aunt in that way before you.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: My dear boy, I love hearing my relations abused. It is the only thing that makes me put up with them at all. Relations are simply a tedious pack of people, who haven’t got the remotest knowledge of how to live, nor the smallest instinct about when to die.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Oh, that is nonsense!

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: It isn’t!

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Well, I won’t argue about the matter. You always want to argue about things.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: That is exactly what things were originally made for.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Upon my word, if I thought that, I’d shoot myself . . . You don’t think there is any chance of Gwendolen becoming like her mother in about a hundred and fifty years, do you, Algy?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: All women become like their mothers. That is their tragedy. No man does. That’s his.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Is that clever?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: It is perfectly phrased! and quite as true as any observation in civilised life should be.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I am sick to death of cleverness. Everybody is clever nowadays. You can’t go anywhere without meeting clever people. The thing has become an absolute public nuisance. I wish to goodness we had a few fools left.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: We have.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I should extremely like to meet them. What do they talk about?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: The fools? Oh! about the clever people, of course.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: What fools!

beat_states.desire_state: to entertain himself at Jack's expense and extract information while demonstrating his own intellectual superiority
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain a life of aesthetic pleasure, wit, and freedom from moral earnestness
beat_states.obstacle: Jack's irritability and refusal to be a fully willing audience for his aphorisms

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is thoroughly enjoying himself. He's in his element — performing wit, needling Jack, and occupying the superior conversational position. He feels no threat in this exchange and treats Jack's distress as material for comedy. His arousal comes from the pleasure of verbal sparring.

social_state.rationale: Algernon holds the upper hand throughout this exchange — every time Jack tries to shut down the conversation, Algernon tops him with a wittier retort. His warmth is real but expressed through teasing; he genuinely enjoys Jack's company as a foil.

known_facts: Jack proposed to Gwendolen
Lady Bracknell likely interviewed Jack
Gwendolen has a habit of initially refusing proposals
Jack has a ward named Cecily in the country (from the cigarette case)
Jack leads a double life
hidden_secrets: He maintains his own double life through Bunburying
He is already scheming about Cecily based on the cigarette case discovery
He knows more about Jack's deceptions than he is currently revealing
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon is strategically withholding what he knows about Jack's double life and Cecily. He's fishing for more information while appearing merely to be bantering. His knowledge advantage gives him conversational control.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — transforms every emotional situation into an opportunity for epigram, keeping genuine feeling at bay
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He claims to love hearing relations abused yet his entire social existence depends on those same relations; his detachment masks a possible genuine curiosity about Jack's romantic fate
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon's aphorism about women becoming like their mothers could be a deliberate cruelty designed to increase Jack's anxiety, testing how far he can push before Jack pushes back

beat_states.desire_state: to vent his frustration about Lady Bracknell's rejection and seek reassurance about his engagement to Gwendolen
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to secure a respectable identity and marry Gwendolen — to belong fully to society
beat_states.obstacle: Lady Bracknell's absolute refusal to consent to the marriage; Algernon's infuriating wit that refuses to take anything seriously

affect_state.rationale: Jack enters agitated and frustrated after Lady Bracknell's devastating interview. He oscillates between relief that Gwendolen accepted him and genuine distress about the maternal obstacle. His irritation at Algernon's music and wit reveals high arousal; his question about Gwendolen becoming like her mother exposes deep underlying anxiety about the future.

social_state.rationale: Jack has just been humiliated by Lady Bracknell and feels diminished. He's comfortable enough with Algernon to insult his aunt freely, showing warmth through candor, but he keeps trying to assert dominance in the conversation and failing against Algernon's superior wit.

known_facts: Gwendolen has accepted his proposal
Lady Bracknell has categorically refused consent
He must find his parents or the engagement cannot proceed
He was found in a handbag at Victoria Station
Algernon is Lady Bracknell's nephew
hidden_secrets: He maintains a double identity as Ernest in town and Jack in the country
He has a ward named Cecily in the country
false_beliefs: That his frustration with cleverness is sincere rather than itself a performance of wit
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack knows the full situation with Lady Bracknell but is processing it emotionally. He's unaware that Algernon has already discovered the cigarette case inscription and is building his own scheme around Cecily.

beat_states.defense_state: dismissal — he repeatedly shuts down Algernon's cleverness as a way of managing his anxiety about the situation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is genuinely distressed about Lady Bracknell yet cannot help engaging in the same witty banter he claims to despise; his complaint about cleverness is itself a clever rhetorical performance
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Jack may be performing frustration partly to elicit sympathy and support from Algernon rather than genuinely being as distressed as he appears

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: By the way, did you tell Gwendolen the truth about your being Ernest in town, and Jack in the country?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: My dear fellow, the truth isn’t quite the sort of thing one tells to a nice, sweet, refined girl. What extraordinary ideas you have about the way to behave to a woman!

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: The only way to behave to a woman is to make love to her, if she is pretty, and to some one else, if she is plain.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Oh, that is nonsense.

beat_states.desire_state: to needle Jack about his double life and expose the absurdity of his deception with Gwendolen
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain intellectual and social superiority through wit and to keep Jack entangled in obligations that serve Algernon's amusement
beat_states.obstacle: Jack deflects and refuses to engage seriously with the moral implications of his deception

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is in his element — teasing, probing, performing. He's enjoying the post-Lady Bracknell wreckage and has the upper hand because he knows Jack's secrets. High control and low vulnerability.

social_state.rationale: Algernon holds the superior position — he's the one asking the pointed questions and delivering epigrams. Warmth is limited; his friendship with Jack is genuine but expressed through constant needling.

known_facts: Jack is Ernest in town and Jack in the country
Jack has not told Gwendolen the truth about his name
Gwendolen believes she is engaged to 'Ernest'
Lady Bracknell has just rejected Jack as a suitor
hidden_secrets: He has his own double life through Bunburying
He is interested in Jack's ward Cecily
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon knows exactly the bind Jack is in and asks the question knowing the answer — it's a rhetorical trap designed to amuse himself.

beat_states.defense_state: deflection through cynical epigram — turns the conversation away from any genuine moral terrain into performative wit
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims worldly expertise about women while being a bachelor who uses charm to avoid real intimacy
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon may genuinely be curious whether Jack has confessed, in case it affects his own plans regarding Cecily

beat_states.desire_state: to shut down Algernon's probing and avoid confronting the moral problem of his deception of Gwendolen
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to win Gwendolen and be accepted as a respectable man worthy of love and social standing
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon keeps poking at the gap between Jack's respectable self-image and his actual dishonesty

affect_state.rationale: Still rattled from the Lady Bracknell interview, Jack is defensive and irritable. He's lost control of his situation — rejected as a suitor, his secrets known to Algernon — and his dismissive tone masks real anxiety about his predicament.

social_state.rationale: Jack is in the weaker position — he's the one being questioned, the one whose plans have just collapsed. His warmth toward Algernon is minimal; he's annoyed and curt.

known_facts: He has not told Gwendolen the truth about his name
Gwendolen loves the name Ernest specifically
Lady Bracknell has forbidden the engagement
Algernon knows about the Ernest/Jack duality
hidden_secrets: He may be beginning to realize the name deception could unravel everything
false_beliefs: That his deception of Gwendolen is a form of courtesy rather than dishonesty
That the truth is something one withholds from 'refined' women as a matter of decorum
