epistemic_state.rationale: Jack's epistemic state is marked by self-serving distortion — he genuinely seems to believe that lying to Gwendolen is the decent thing to do, which reveals his deep capacity for self-deception.

beat_states.defense_state: rationalization — reframes his lie as gentlemanly propriety, suggesting truth-telling would be ungentlemanly toward a 'nice, sweet, refined girl'
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Presents himself as morally upright and chivalrous while actively deceiving the woman he claims to love; dismisses Algernon's cynicism while practicing a deeper cynicism himself
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Jack may be more self-aware than he lets on — his dismissal of Algernon's cynicism ('Oh, that is nonsense') could be a deliberate subject-change rather than genuine moral indignation

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: What about your brother? What about the profligate Ernest?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Oh, before the end of the week I shall have got rid of him. I’ll say he died in Paris of apoplexy. Lots of people die of apoplexy, quite suddenly, don’t they?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Yes, but it’s hereditary, my dear fellow. It’s a sort of thing that runs in families. You had much better say a severe chill.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: You are sure a severe chill isn’t hereditary, or anything of that kind?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Of course it isn’t!

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Very well, then. My poor brother Ernest is carried off suddenly, in Paris, by a severe chill. That gets rid of him.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: But I thought you said that . . . Miss Cardew was a little too much interested in your poor brother Ernest? Won’t she feel his loss a good deal?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Oh, that is all right. Cecily is not a silly romantic girl, I am glad to say. She has got a capital appetite, goes long walks, and pays no attention at all to her lessons.

beat_states.desire_state: To extract maximum information about Jack's country life, especially about Cecily Cardew, while appearing merely to offer helpful advice
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To pursue pleasure, novelty, and romantic adventure — Cecily represents a new source of intrigue
beat_states.obstacle: Jack is being dismissive and casual, not giving Algernon the detailed information he's fishing for

affect_state.rationale: Enjoying himself enormously — he's in his element, being clever, poking at Jack's plans, and secretly cataloguing useful details about Cecily. Low vulnerability because nothing is at stake for him yet

social_state.rationale: Maintains the upper hand through superior wit and by positioning himself as the expert advisor on plausible fictional deaths; warmth is surface-level — friendly but self-serving

known_facts: Jack has a ward named Cecily Cardew in the country
Cecily is interested in the fictional Ernest
Jack plans to kill off Ernest
Jack's double life as Bunburyist
Jack's cigarette case is inscribed from 'little Cecily'
hidden_secrets: He is already forming a plan to visit Cecily in the country by posing as Ernest
He has noted Jack's country address
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon is the most epistemically advantaged character — he knows Jack's secrets and is strategically accumulating more information while revealing nothing of his own intentions

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — channeling his real curiosity about Cecily into seemingly casual, witty advisory remarks about plausible causes of death
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Presents himself as helpfully advising Jack while actually gathering intelligence to potentially exploit the situation; his interest in Cecily is growing but he disguises it as idle conversation
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon might genuinely be trying to help Jack with practical advice, with the Cecily question being pure curiosity rather than scheming — but his later actions in the play confirm the predatory reading

beat_states.desire_state: To efficiently dispose of the fictional Ernest so he can pursue Gwendolen without complications
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure a respectable identity and win Gwendolen's hand in marriage
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon keeps poking holes in his plan, raising complications about the fictional brother's death

affect_state.rationale: Relieved to have a plan but slightly anxious as Algernon keeps finding flaws; adopts a breezy confidence that masks underlying uncertainty about whether his schemes will hold together

social_state.rationale: Has just been humiliated by Lady Bracknell's rejection; is trying to reassert competence and authority over his own narrative, but keeps deferring to Algernon's superior knowledge of plausible excuses

known_facts: He invented a brother named Ernest to escape to London
He needs to eliminate the Ernest fiction to marry Gwendolen
Cecily is his ward in the country
Lady Bracknell has rejected his proposal
hidden_secrets: He is a foundling with no known parentage
His entire double life as Ernest/Jack
false_beliefs: That Cecily has no real romantic interest in Ernest and will not be affected by his 'death'
That disposing of a fictional brother is a clean, simple matter
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack is deeply self-deceiving about how entangled his fictions have become, particularly underestimating Cecily's attachment to the idea of Ernest

beat_states.defense_state: Compartmentalization — treating the 'death' of a fictional person as a simple logistical problem, refusing to engage with emotional or moral dimensions
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims Cecily is sensible and unromantic, yet earlier admitted she was 'too much interested' in Ernest — he minimizes threats to maintain his sense of control

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I would rather like to see Cecily.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I will take very good care you never do. She is excessively pretty, and she is only just eighteen.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Have you told Gwendolen yet that you have an excessively pretty ward who is only just eighteen?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Oh! one doesn’t blurt these things out to people. Cecily and Gwendolen are perfectly certain to be extremely great friends. I’ll bet you anything you like that half an hour after they have met, they will be calling each other sister.

beat_states.desire_state: To gain access to Cecily and exploit Jack's vulnerability about his ward
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To amuse himself by puncturing others' pretensions and pursuing pleasure without constraint
beat_states.obstacle: Jack's protectiveness and refusal to allow the meeting

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Women only do that when they have called each other a lot of other things first. Now, my dear boy, if we want to get a good table at Willis’s, we really must go and dress. Do you know it is nearly seven?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Oh! It always is nearly seven.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Well, I’m hungry.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I never knew you when you weren’t . . .

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: What shall we do after dinner? Go to a theatre?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Oh no! I loathe listening.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Well, let us go to the Club?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Oh, no! I hate talking.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Well, we might trot round to the Empire at ten?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Oh, no! I can’t bear looking at things. It is so silly.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Well, what shall we do?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Nothing!

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: It is awfully hard work doing nothing. However, I don’t mind hard work where there is no definite object of any kind.

beat_states.desire_state: to move the evening forward and enjoy himself with Jack as companion
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to extract maximum pleasure and amusement from every social situation while avoiding anything earnest
beat_states.obstacle: Jack's petulant refusal of every suggestion, born of lovesick distraction

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is in good spirits, hungry and ready for the evening, mildly exasperated by Jack's rejections but never truly bothered — he turns every dead end into a bon mot. His final line about hard work with no definite object shows him landing on his feet with characteristic ease.

social_state.rationale: He plays the role of social director, proposing plan after plan with genial persistence. Despite Jack's contrariness, Algernon maintains affectionate camaraderie — he's hosting, leading, accommodating.

known_facts: Jack is engaged to Gwendolen
Jack leads a double life as Ernest
Jack has a ward named Cecily in the country
It is nearly seven and they need to dress for dinner at Willis's
Jack is in a contrary mood

epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon has gathered crucial intelligence this scene — Jack's country address, Cecily's existence — and is already scheming. His sociable exterior conceals active plotting.

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: genuinely wants company and entertainment but philosophically celebrates doing nothing
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon's persistence in suggesting evening plans could be a tactic to keep Jack close and extract more useful information rather than genuine social desire

beat_states.desire_state: to wallow in romantic agitation and resist being drawn into trivial social activity
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to secure love and respectability by becoming the 'earnest' man Gwendolen believes him to be
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon's relentless sociability and Jack's own inability to articulate or resolve his inner turmoil

His categorical rejections — loathing listening, hating talking, unable to bear looking — reveal a man consumed by internal preoccupation. The negativity is performative but rooted in genuine unease about his tangled double life and new engagement.

social_state.rationale: Jack is being the difficult guest, ceding social leadership to Algernon but asserting a kind of passive dominance through obstinate refusal. Warmth is low — he's being deliberately contrary and uncooperative, though without real malice.

beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He has just become engaged and should be elated, yet he is irritable and world-weary, suggesting anxiety about sustaining his deception
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Jack's blanket refusals could be read as pure comic exhaustion after a dramatically eventful afternoon rather than deeper psychological distress

utterances.speaker: LANE
utterances.text: Miss Fairfax.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Gwendolen, upon my word!

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Algy, kindly turn your back. I have something very particular to say to Mr. Worthing.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Really, Gwendolen, I don’t think I can allow this at all.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Algy, you always adopt a strictly immoral attitude towards life. You are not quite old enough to do that.

beat_states.desire_state: to maintain control of the social situation and prevent being excluded from a private conversation in his own home
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to remain at the center of all social intrigue and entertainment, never being sidelined

beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon's protest may be entirely performative — he may actually want to give them privacy to further his own schemes regarding Cecily

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: My own darling!

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Ernest, we may never be married. From the expression on mamma’s face I fear we never shall. Few parents nowadays pay any regard to what their children say to them. The old-fashioned respect for the young is fast dying out. Whatever influence I ever had over mamma, I lost at the age of three. But although she may prevent us from becoming man and wife, and I may marry some one else, and marry often, nothing that she can possibly do can alter my eternal devotion to you.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Dear Gwendolen!

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: The story of your romantic origin, as related to me by mamma, with unpleasing comments, has naturally stirred the deeper fibres of my nature. Your Christian name has an irresistible fascination. The simplicity of your character makes you exquisitely incomprehensible to me. Your town address at the Albany I have. What is your address in the country?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: The Manor House, Woolton, Hertfordshire.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: There is a good postal service, I suppose? It may be necessary to do something desperate. That of course will require serious consideration. I will communicate with you daily.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: My own one!

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: How long do you remain in town?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Till Monday.

beat_states.desire_state: To secure a permanent romantic connection with Ernest/Jack by obtaining practical details for continued contact despite her mother's opposition
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To live a life of romantic idealism on her own terms, with a man named Ernest
beat_states.obstacle: Lady Bracknell's disapproval and potential prohibition of the marriage

affect_state.rationale: Gwendolen is emotionally stirred by the romantic drama of the situation but maintains firm control. She transforms potential heartbreak into an opportunity for stylish determination. Her certainty is high because she has already decided on her course of action regardless of her mother's wishes.

social_state.rationale: Gwendolen entirely dominates this exchange, directing the conversation, gathering information, and dictating terms. Jack is reduced to exclamatory endearments while she orchestrates the logistics of their clandestine relationship. Her warmth is genuine but filtered through her performative style.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Good! Algy, you may turn round now.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Thanks, I’ve turned round already.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: You may also ring the bell.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: You will let me see you to your carriage, my own darling?

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Certainly.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I will see Miss Fairfax out.

utterances.speaker: LANE
utterances.text: Yes, sir.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: A glass of sherry, Lane.

utterances.speaker: LANE
utterances.text: Yes, sir.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: To-morrow, Lane, I’m going Bunburying.

utterances.speaker: LANE
utterances.text: Yes, sir.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I shall probably not be back till Monday. You can put up my dress clothes, my smoking jacket, and all the Bunbury suits . . .

utterances.speaker: LANE
utterances.text: Yes, sir.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I hope to-morrow will be a fine day, Lane.

utterances.speaker: LANE
utterances.text: It never is, sir.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Lane, you’re a perfect pessimist.

utterances.speaker: LANE
utterances.text: I do my best to give satisfaction, sir.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: There’s a sensible, intellectual girl! the only girl I ever cared for in my life. What on earth are you so amused at?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Oh, I’m a little anxious about poor Bunbury, that is all.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: If you don’t take care, your friend Bunbury will get you into a serious scrape some day.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I love scrapes. They are the only things that are never serious.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Oh, that’s nonsense, Algy. You never talk anything but nonsense.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Nobody ever does.

He maintains a double life in the country with a fictional brother named Ernest

epistemic_state.rationale: Jack is willfully blind to the superficiality of Gwendolen's attachment and does not yet grasp how Algernon might exploit knowledge of Bunburying

beat_states.defense_state: deflection through exasperation — labeling Algernon's conversation as nonsense to avoid engaging with the Bunbury threat
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He praises Gwendolen as 'sensible and intellectual' when she has just accepted him based entirely on a name — a fact he cannot acknowledge

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: Cecily, Cecily! Surely such a utilitarian occupation as the watering of flowers is rather Moulton’s duty than yours? Especially at a moment when intellectual pleasures await you. Your German grammar is on the table. Pray open it at page fifteen. We will repeat yesterday’s lesson.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: But I don’t like German. It isn’t at all a becoming language. I know perfectly well that I look quite plain after my German lesson.

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: Child, you know how anxious your guardian is that you should improve yourself in every way. He laid particular stress on your German, as he was leaving for town yesterday. Indeed, he always lays stress on your German when he is leaving for town.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Dear Uncle Jack is so very serious! Sometimes he is so serious that I think he cannot be quite well.

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: Your guardian enjoys the best of health, and his gravity of demeanour is especially to be commended in one so comparatively young as he is. I know no one who has a higher sense of duty and responsibility.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I suppose that is why he often looks a little bored when we three are together.

beat_states.desire_state: To avoid her German lesson and remain in the garden enjoying herself
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To live freely and romantically, resisting the dull constraints imposed by adults and propriety
beat_states.obstacle: Miss Prism's authority as governess and Jack's instructions

affect_state.rationale: She's in a pleasant, playful mood, enjoying the garden and the game of evading study. Low vulnerability because she feels secure in her position and her wit gives her agency.

social_state.rationale: Nominally subordinate as a ward/student but functionally equalizing or even dominating the exchange through wit. She's warm enough to keep things light but each remark subtly challenges Miss Prism's authority.

known_facts: Uncle Jack always stresses German when leaving for town
Uncle Jack seems bored when the three of them are together
Jack is very serious — perhaps excessively so
German makes her look plain (her own belief treated as fact)
hidden_secrets: She likely keeps a diary with romantic fantasies (revealed later)
She is already fascinated by the idea of 'Ernest,' Jack's wicked brother
false_beliefs: She may believe German genuinely makes her look plain
She does not know the full truth of Jack's double life
epistemic_state.rationale: Cecily perceives patterns — Jack's repeated emphasis on German when leaving, his boredom — but interprets them innocently. Her observations are sharper than she lets on, but she lacks the full picture of Jack's deceptions.

beat_states.defense_state: Using charm, humor, and seemingly innocent observations to undermine authority without direct confrontation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She performs naive innocence while delivering devastatingly perceptive observations — her comment about Jack looking bored reveals sharp social intelligence wrapped in childlike framing
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Cecily's remarks about Jack being unwell and bored could also be read as genuine concern rather than subversive wit, but the comic context and pattern of deflection strongly favor the reading of playful resistance.

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: Cecily! I am surprised at you. Mr. Worthing has many troubles in his life. Idle merriment and triviality would be out of place in his conversation. You must remember his constant anxiety about that unfortunate young man his brother.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I wish Uncle Jack would allow that unfortunate young man, his brother, to come down here sometimes. We might have a good influence over him, Miss Prism. I am sure you certainly would. You know German, and geology, and things of that kind influence a man very much.

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: I do not think that even I could produce any effect on a character that according to his own brother’s admission is irretrievably weak and vacillating. Indeed I am not sure that I would desire to reclaim him. I am not in favour of this modern mania for turning bad people into good people at a moment’s notice. As a man sows so let him reap. You must put away your diary, Cecily. I really don’t see why you should keep a diary at all.

beat_states.desire_state: to escape the tedium of lessons and invite excitement — specifically the romantic possibility of meeting Ernest
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to experience romance, adventure, and the forbidden — everything her sheltered country life denies her
beat_states.obstacle: Miss Prism's moral authority and her physical absence from Ernest/the outside world

affect_state.rationale: She is amused and mischievous (positive valence), moderately energized by the game of outwitting Prism, but lacks control over her situation as a ward under supervision.

social_state.rationale: Nominally subordinate as pupil but she wields warmth strategically — her compliments about German and geology are disarming weapons disguised as affection.

known_facts: Uncle Jack claims to have a troubled brother
Miss Prism knows German and geology
She is expected to study rather than daydream
hidden_secrets: She keeps a diary full of romantic fantasies about Ernest
Her interest in Ernest is romantic, not charitable
false_beliefs: Ernest is a real person she might actually meet
Her diary is simply a personal record rather than a fantasy life
epistemic_state.rationale: Cecily operates within Jack's fiction about Ernest, having built an elaborate romantic fantasy around a man who doesn't exist.

beat_states.defense_state: ironic innocence — deploying apparent naïveté to disguise subversive wit
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She presents her desire to meet Ernest as charitable concern for his moral improvement, while her real interest is romantic fascination with wickedness
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Her flattery of Miss Prism could be genuinely warm rather than purely tactical — she may have real affection for her governess even while teasing her

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I keep a diary in order to enter the wonderful secrets of my life. If I didn’t write them down, I should probably forget all about them.

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: Memory, my dear Cecily, is the diary that we all carry about with us.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Yes, but it usually chronicles the things that have never happened, and couldn’t possibly have happened. I believe that Memory is responsible for nearly all the three-volume novels that Mudie sends us.

beat_states.desire_state: to assert the superiority of imagination and romantic fantasy over practical duty
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to live a life of romance and excitement unconstrained by the dull propriety imposed on her
beat_states.obstacle: Miss Prism's insistence on moral seriousness and conventional wisdom

affect_state.rationale: Cecily is enjoying herself, playfully sparring with Miss Prism, confident in her wit. She feels in command of the exchange and delighted by her own cleverness.

social_state.rationale: Technically subordinate as a pupil but effectively claiming intellectual superiority through her paradoxical wit. She remains affectionate toward Miss Prism even while besting her.

known_facts: She keeps a diary filled with imaginative or embellished entries
She is well-read enough to reference Mudie's circulating library and three-volume novels
She understands that memory is unreliable and creative
hidden_secrets: Her diary likely contains romantic fantasies about Ernest, Jack's fictitious brother
She is far more interested in romance and imagination than she lets on to her guardians
epistemic_state.rationale: Cecily is surprisingly perceptive about the nature of memory and fiction, but she deploys this insight to justify her own romantic fabrications.

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — reframing her diary-keeping and fantasy life as philosophically superior to Miss Prism's platitudes
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She claims to keep a diary to remember secrets, yet admits she fabricates or embellishes — her 'wonderful secrets' may be inventions she half-believes
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Cecily may be more self-aware than she appears — her comments about memory and fiction could be a deliberate, knowing confession that her diary is largely fantasy, delivered as wit to avoid scrutiny.

beat_states.desire_state: to reassert her authority as educator and moral guide by offering a corrective aphorism
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain her position of intellectual and moral respectability, compensating for a secret past
beat_states.obstacle: Cecily is wittier than she is and keeps turning her platitudes against her

affect_state.rationale: Miss Prism attempts composure but is slightly flustered. Her aphorism falls flat as Cecily effortlessly dismantles it. She is losing the exchange and her authority is slipping.

social_state.rationale: Nominally the authority figure but functionally outmatched. She remains cordial but her pedagogical status is being gently undermined.

known_facts: She is Cecily's governess
She values moral instruction and propriety
She is familiar with Mudie's library and three-volume novels
hidden_secrets: She herself wrote a three-volume novel — Cecily's remark about memory and three-volume novels unknowingly touches her secret
She lost the baby Jack Worthing in a handbag at Victoria Station
false_beliefs: She believes her aphorisms are wise and persuasive
epistemic_state.rationale: Miss Prism carries significant secrets about her past that give dramatic irony to Cecily's casual reference to three-volume novels and unreliable memory.

beat_states.defense_state: deflection through aphorism — she retreats into a neat moral saying ('Memory is the diary we all carry') rather than engaging with Cecily's actual point
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She lectures on memory as reliable and moral, yet she herself is an author of a three-volume novel and has a secret past she may prefer to forget — Cecily's jab about memory and fiction lands closer to home than Miss Prism realizes
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Miss Prism may be momentarily stung by Cecily's reference to three-volume novels, experiencing a flash of self-consciousness about her own literary past, though the text doesn't make this explicit.

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: Do not speak slightingly of the three-volume novel, Cecily. I wrote one myself in earlier days.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Did you really, Miss Prism? How wonderfully clever you are! I hope it did not end happily? I don’t like novels that end happily. They depress me so much.

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: The good ended happily, and the bad unhappily. That is what Fiction means.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I suppose so. But it seems very unfair. And was your novel ever published?

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: Alas! no. The manuscript unfortunately was abandoned. I use the word in the sense of lost or mislaid. To your work, child, these speculations are profitless.

beat_states.desire_state: to be entertained and to avoid returning to her German lesson by keeping Miss Prism talking about her novel
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to live a life of romance and excitement rather than the dull propriety imposed on her
beat_states.obstacle: Miss Prism keeps trying to redirect her back to her studies

affect_state.rationale: Cecily is enjoying herself, playfully engaged in the conversation. Her comment that happy endings 'depress' her reveals a romantic sensibility that delights in paradox. She feels relatively in control of the dynamic through flattery.

social_state.rationale: Nominally subordinate as pupil, but her warmth and flattery ('wonderfully clever') are strategic tools to keep the conversation going and away from lessons. She wields charm as a soft equalizer.

known_facts: Miss Prism once wrote a three-volume novel
The novel was never published
Miss Prism abandoned/lost the manuscript
hidden_secrets: She is far more interested in romance and fantasy than she lets on to authority figures
false_beliefs: She may naively believe Miss Prism's novel is merely a quaint biographical detail, not sensing the deeper emotional weight behind the 'abandoned' manuscript
epistemic_state.rationale: Cecily absorbs the new information about Miss Prism's literary past with genuine curiosity but doesn't probe the suspicious language around 'abandoned.' Her question about publication is innocent.

beat_states.defense_state: deflection through charm and enthusiastic questioning
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She performs the role of admiring pupil while subtly subverting Miss Prism's authority and values at every turn
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Cecily's preference for unhappy endings could be read as genuine romantic melancholy rather than mere playful contrarianism

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: But I see dear Dr. Chasuble coming up through the garden.

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: Dr. Chasuble! This is indeed a pleasure.

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: And how are we this morning? Miss Prism, you are, I trust, well?

beat_states.desire_state: To deflect attention away from herself and the tedious lesson, and to create a diversion
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To escape the dull constraints of her education and experience romance and excitement
beat_states.obstacle: Miss Prism's authority and the expectation that she study German

affect_state.rationale: Cecily is relieved and mildly delighted — she knows Chasuble's arrival will distract Miss Prism and free her from lessons. She feels in control of the social situation.

social_state.rationale: As the ward she is nominally subordinate to Miss Prism, but she wields quiet social power by knowing exactly how to manipulate the situation. Warm surface manner.

known_facts: Dr. Chasuble is approaching
Miss Prism has a romantic interest in Chasuble
Chasuble's arrival will disrupt her lesson
hidden_secrets: She is deliberately using Chasuble's arrival to escape her studies
epistemic_state.rationale: Cecily is perceptive about Miss Prism's feelings for Chasuble and exploits this knowledge strategically.

beat_states.defense_state: Using external arrival as an escape hatch from an uncomfortable tutorial dynamic
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She announces Chasuble's arrival with apparent innocence while strategically engineering her own liberation from study
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: She may genuinely be pleased to see Chasuble for his own sake, not purely as a tactical move.

beat_states.desire_state: To perform his pastoral role while enjoying Miss Prism's company under the cover of social duty
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To reconcile his clerical celibacy with his unacknowledged romantic attraction to Miss Prism
beat_states.obstacle: His clerical position demands restraint and propriety; he cannot openly pursue romantic interest

affect_state.rationale: Pleasant, measured warmth. He is pleased to see Miss Prism but maintains clerical composure. Moderate arousal contained within formal greeting structure.

social_state.rationale: As a clergyman he holds respectable social status. His warmth is genuine but carefully modulated through professional courtesy.

known_facts: He is expected to visit the household
Miss Prism is Cecily's governess
He enjoys Miss Prism's intellectual company
hidden_secrets: His romantic feelings for Miss Prism
false_beliefs: He may believe his interest in Miss Prism reads as purely pastoral
epistemic_state.rationale: Chasuble operates under the self-deception that his visits are purely professional and spiritual, though his singling out of Miss Prism reveals otherwise.

beat_states.defense_state: Cloaking personal interest in the formulaic language of pastoral concern
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He addresses both women but singles out Miss Prism by name, betraying particular interest while performing general benevolence
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: He may be genuinely unaware of his romantic feelings, operating in complete self-deception rather than conscious concealment.

beat_states.desire_state: To express and receive romantic attention from Dr. Chasuble while maintaining propriety
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To find romantic fulfillment and validation despite her spinster status and past secrets
beat_states.obstacle: Social propriety, her role as governess, Cecily's watchful presence, and Chasuble's clerical reserve

affect_state.rationale: Genuine delight and romantic flutter at Chasuble's arrival. Her exclamatory tone betrays high arousal and positive feeling, but she is uncertain of his reciprocation and losing composure.

social_state.rationale: She positions herself warmly and somewhat deferentially toward Chasuble, elevating him. Her warmth is conspicuously high for a 'mere' social greeting.

known_facts: Chasuble visits regularly
She finds him attractive
Cecily is present and observing
hidden_secrets: She once wrote a three-volume novel that was lost under mysterious circumstances
The lost handbag/baby secret from her past
false_beliefs: She believes her romantic interest in Chasuble is not obvious to others
epistemic_state.rationale: Miss Prism thinks she is being discreet, but her excessive enthusiasm is transparent to Cecily and the audience.

beat_states.defense_state: Sublimating romantic excitement into the formal language of polite pleasure
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She is a strict moral authority figure who is transparently giddy at the sight of a man she desires

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Miss Prism has just been complaining of a slight headache. I think it would do her so much good to have a short stroll with you in the Park, Dr. Chasuble.

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: Cecily, I have not mentioned anything about a headache.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: No, dear Miss Prism, I know that, but I felt instinctively that you had a headache. Indeed I was thinking about that, and not about my German lesson, when the Rector came in.

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: I hope, Cecily, you are not inattentive.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Oh, I am afraid I am.

beat_states.desire_state: To get rid of Miss Prism so she can escape her German lesson and be free
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To live a life of romantic excitement and freedom from tedious obligation
beat_states.obstacle: Miss Prism's presence and insistence on lessons; Miss Prism publicly contradicts her fabrication

affect_state.rationale: Cecily is enjoying her own cleverness and feels largely in control of the situation. She's delighted by her own mischief. When caught in the lie by Miss Prism, she barely flinches, improvising an even more audacious excuse with total composure.

social_state.rationale: Nominally the subordinate student, but she wields social power through charm and manipulation. Her warmth is performed—sweetly solicitous toward Miss Prism and disarmingly honest with Chasuble about her inattention.

known_facts: Miss Prism has no headache
Miss Prism and Dr. Chasuble have a romantic interest in each other
A stroll together would please both of them
She has not been paying attention to her German lesson
hidden_secrets: She is deliberately fabricating the headache to engineer Miss Prism's departure
Her real motive is escaping the lesson, not concern for Miss Prism
epistemic_state.rationale: Cecily is the most knowing character in this beat—she sees through the adults' unspoken attraction and exploits it. She is fully aware she is lying and improvising.

beat_states.defense_state: Feigning innocent concern to disguise her scheming; doubling down with charming absurdity when caught
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Projects the image of a caring, attentive pupil while actively engineering her own liberation from study

beat_states.desire_state: To engage pleasantly with the household and maintain his dignified pastoral role
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To be valued and needed as a moral and spiritual guide while enjoying the quiet comforts of companionship
beat_states.obstacle: The social awkwardness of the situation—he may not fully grasp the manipulation unfolding

affect_state.rationale: Mildly pleased to be in company but somewhat oblivious or deliberately obtuse about the romantic maneuvering. His mild admonishment of Cecily is comfortable clerical territory. Low arousal—he is placid by nature.

social_state.rationale: As the Rector, he carries inherent social authority. His warmth is gentle and pastoral—he addresses Cecily with benign concern rather than severity.

known_facts: Cecily claims Miss Prism has a headache
Miss Prism denies having a headache
Cecily admits to being inattentive in her studies
hidden_secrets: He harbors romantic interest in Miss Prism
false_beliefs: He may believe Cecily's concern for Miss Prism is genuine
He may believe his interest in Miss Prism is purely intellectual or pastoral
epistemic_state.rationale: Chasuble appears to take the exchange more or less at face value, or at least chooses to. His comic function is partial obliviousness to the scheming around him.

beat_states.defense_state: Retreating into his clerical role by gently reproving Cecily's inattention, avoiding the romantic subtext entirely
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He deflects toward Cecily's studiousness rather than addressing the obvious invitation to walk with Miss Prism, suggesting he is both drawn to and anxious about the romantic implication
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: He may be more aware of the matchmaking than he lets on and is simply choosing not to acknowledge it, which would make his admonishment of Cecily a form of deflection rather than genuine concern.

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: That is strange. Were I fortunate enough to be Miss Prism’s pupil, I would hang upon her lips. I spoke metaphorically.—My metaphor was drawn from bees. Ahem! Mr. Worthing, I suppose, has not returned from town yet?

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: We do not expect him till Monday afternoon.

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: Ah yes, he usually likes to spend his Sunday in London. He is not one of those whose sole aim is enjoyment, as, by all accounts, that unfortunate young man his brother seems to be. But I must not disturb Egeria and her pupil any longer.

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: Egeria? My name is Lætitia, Doctor.

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: A classical allusion merely, drawn from the Pagan authors. I shall see you both no doubt at Evensong?

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: I think, dear Doctor, I will have a stroll with you. I find I have a headache after all, and a walk might do it good.

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: With pleasure, Miss Prism, with pleasure. We might go as far as the schools and back.

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: That would be delightful. Cecily, you will read your Political Economy in my absence. The chapter on the Fall of the Rupee you may omit. It is somewhat too sensational. Even these metallic problems have their melodramatic side.

beat_states.desire_state: to spend time with Miss Prism while maintaining clerical propriety
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to enjoy romantic companionship with Miss Prism without compromising his respectable position as a clergyman
beat_states.obstacle: his clerical role and social propriety prevent him from directly expressing romantic interest

affect_state.rationale: Pleased and flustered by the opportunity to be near Miss Prism; his 'Ahem!' and hasty correction about metaphor reveal arousal he cannot fully control; delighted when she agrees to walk with him ('With pleasure, Miss Prism, with pleasure') — the repetition betrays his eagerness

social_state.rationale: Holds status as the local clergyman but is slightly subordinate in this romantic dynamic — he's the one being pursued/manipulated into the walk; his warmth is high but expressed through elaborate indirection

known_facts: Jack usually spends Sundays in London
Jack has a reportedly dissolute brother named Ernest
Miss Prism is Cecily's governess
Miss Prism's first name is Lætitia
hidden_secrets: his romantic feelings for Miss Prism
false_beliefs: Ernest Worthing actually exists as Jack's wayward brother
he believes his compliments are sufficiently veiled
epistemic_state.rationale: Chasuble operates on the surface information provided by Jack's fiction about his brother; he is unaware his romantic interest is transparent to both Miss Prism and Cecily

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — retreats into classical allusions and pedantic corrections to mask his emotional investment
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: desires intimate connection with Miss Prism but compulsively distances himself through scholarly deflection the moment he gets too close ('I spoke metaphorically')
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Could be read as genuinely oblivious to the romantic subtext and simply being a sociable clergyman, but the repeated verbal stumbles and eagerness strongly suggest romantic interest

beat_states.desire_state: to engineer private time alone with Dr. Chasuble
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to achieve romantic fulfillment and companionship she has been denied as a governess
beat_states.obstacle: propriety and her role as Cecily's governess require her to maintain decorum and attend to her duties

affect_state.rationale: Delighted by Chasuble's veiled compliments and successfully executing her plan to walk with him; correcting him on her name ('My name is Lætitia') is a coy move to create intimacy; she's more in control of this exchange than he is, but still vulnerable to rejection

social_state.rationale: Slightly lower social status as a governess compared to a clergyman, but she commands the dynamic here; warmth is directed specifically at Chasuble while she's briskly dismissive toward Cecily's education

known_facts: Jack is not expected until Monday
Cecily set up this encounter deliberately
Chasuble is romantically interested in her
she does not actually have a headache
hidden_secrets: she wrote a three-volume novel that was lost
the true depth of her romantic feelings for Chasuble
her past involving the loss of a handbag (the play's central secret)
false_beliefs: Ernest Worthing exists as Jack's real brother
her departure instructions about the Fall of the Rupee constitute adequate pedagogy
epistemic_state.rationale: Miss Prism is shrewd about the interpersonal dynamics — she recognizes Cecily's matchmaking and plays along — but she shares the broader false belief about Ernest and conceals her deeper past

beat_states.defense_state: rationalization — invents a headache as pretext and frames abandoning her pupil as medically necessary
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: presents herself as a model of propriety and duty while actively scheming to abandon her pupil for a romantic stroll; just denied having a headache but now claims one
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Could be read as genuinely developing a headache and innocently accepting a walk, but the contradiction with her earlier denial and the transparent romantic maneuvering make this unlikely

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Horrid Political Economy! Horrid Geography! Horrid, horrid German!

beat_states.desire_state: to revel in her freedom from lessons now that Miss Prism has been dispatched with Chasuble
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to live a life of romantic excitement and imagination rather than dull propriety and education
beat_states.obstacle: the lingering expectation that she should be studying; the tedium of her prescribed curriculum

affect_state.rationale: Cecily is delighted — she has successfully maneuvered Miss Prism away and now experiences the exhilaration of freedom. High arousal from the release of pent-up boredom. She feels very much in control, having engineered the situation herself.

social_state.rationale: Alone now, she drops social performance and indulges in a private moment of rebellion. Her contempt for the subjects is playful but genuine — warmth is directed at herself and her own pleasure rather than outward.

known_facts: She has successfully sent Miss Prism off with Dr. Chasuble
She is now unsupervised and free from lessons
Her subjects include Political Economy, Geography, and German
Uncle Jack has a wicked brother named Ernest
hidden_secrets: She deliberately manufactured the headache excuse to get rid of Miss Prism
She maintains a secret diary full of romantic fantasies
She is already romantically fixated on the idea of Ernest
false_beliefs: Jack actually has a wicked brother named Ernest who may visit
epistemic_state.rationale: Cecily is fully aware of her own scheming and relishes it. Her false belief about Ernest's existence is the dramatic irony that drives much of Act 2.

beat_states.defense_state: externalizing frustration onto the subjects themselves rather than admitting she orchestrated her escape from them
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: she is gleefully triumphant at her own manipulation while performing innocent childish frustration, revealing a sophistication she masks with naïveté
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Could be read as genuine childish petulance rather than calculated triumph, but the context of her deliberate manipulation of Chasuble and Prism strongly supports the reading that this is a victory lap.

utterances.speaker: MERRIMAN
utterances.text: Mr. Ernest Worthing has just driven over from the station. He has brought his luggage with him.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: ‘Mr. Ernest Worthing, B. 4, The Albany, W.’ Uncle Jack’s brother! Did you tell him Mr. Worthing was in town?

utterances.speaker: MERRIMAN
utterances.text: Yes, Miss. He seemed very much disappointed. I mentioned that you and Miss Prism were in the garden. He said he was anxious to speak to you privately for a moment.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Ask Mr. Ernest Worthing to come here. I suppose you had better talk to the housekeeper about a room for him.

utterances.speaker: MERRIMAN
utterances.text: Yes, Miss.

beat_states.desire_state: to meet the romantic figure of 'Ernest' she has been fantasizing about
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to live out a romantic adventure that transcends her sheltered, predictable life
beat_states.obstacle: propriety and the uncertainty of who this stranger actually is; Uncle Jack is not present to mediate

affect_state.rationale: Cecily is delighted — the romantic figure from her diary fantasies has materialized. Her arousal is high with excitement but she channels it into decisiveness, issuing clear orders to Merriman. She assumes she knows who this is ('Uncle Jack's brother!') but there's underlying uncertainty about the encounter.

social_state.rationale: She steps into the authority of the household in Jack's absence, directing Merriman with confidence. Her warmth toward the servant is moderate — polite but businesslike — while her warmth toward the absent 'Ernest' is high, though she conceals it.

known_facts: A man calling himself Ernest Worthing has arrived from the station with luggage
His card reads 'B. 4, The Albany, W.'
Uncle Jack has a wayward brother named Ernest
Uncle Jack is currently in town
The visitor was disappointed to learn Jack was away
The visitor wishes to speak to her privately
hidden_secrets: She has already constructed an elaborate romantic fantasy about Ernest in her diary, including an imaginary engagement
false_beliefs: This visitor is genuinely Jack's brother Ernest
The name Ernest belongs to a real person related to Jack
epistemic_state.rationale: Cecily instantly accepts the visitor at face value because he confirms her pre-existing fantasy. She has no reason to suspect deception. The audience knows this is actually Algernon Bunburying.

beat_states.defense_state: performing composed authority to mask her excitement
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She is thrilled and romantically eager but must play the role of composed young mistress of the house giving sensible domestic instructions
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Cecily may be slightly performing her authority and composure for Merriman's benefit, using the domestic instructions as a way to delay and build anticipation for a meeting she desperately wants.

beat_states.desire_state: to fulfill his duty by properly announcing the visitor and receiving clear instructions
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain professional standing and the smooth running of the household
beat_states.obstacle: the master of the house is absent, requiring him to defer to Cecily for decisions about receiving a guest

affect_state.rationale: Merriman is emotionally neutral and highly controlled — the quintessential Wildean servant. He reports information cleanly and awaits instructions with high certainty about his role, if not about the situation's propriety.

social_state.rationale: Occupies the deferential servant position, addressing Cecily as 'Miss' and awaiting her commands. Warmth is minimal but correct — professional courtesy.

Mr. Worthing (Jack) is currently in town and not at the manor
The visitor was disappointed to learn Jack was away
The visitor asked to speak to Cecily privately
Cecily and Miss Prism were in the garden
false_beliefs: The visitor is genuinely who he claims to be
epistemic_state.rationale: Merriman reports what he has been told and observed. He has no reason to question the visitor's identity and no personal stake in the matter.

beat_states.defense_state: professional neutrality — reporting facts without personal opinion or emotional coloring

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I have never met any really wicked person before. I feel rather frightened. I am so afraid he will look just like every one else. He does!

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: You are my little cousin Cecily, I’m sure.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: You are under some strange mistake. I am not little. In fact, I believe I am more than usually tall for my age. But I am your cousin Cecily. You, I see from your card, are Uncle Jack’s brother, my cousin Ernest, my wicked cousin Ernest.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Oh! I am not really wicked at all, cousin Cecily. You mustn’t think that I am wicked.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: If you are not, then you have certainly been deceiving us all in a very inexcusable manner. I hope you have not been leading a double life, pretending to be wicked and being really good all the time. That would be hypocrisy.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Oh! Of course I have been rather reckless.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I am glad to hear it.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: In fact, now you mention the subject, I have been very bad in my own small way.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I don’t think you should be so proud of that, though I am sure it must have been very pleasant.

beat_states.desire_state: To charm Cecily and sustain the deception of being Ernest while enjoying the adventure of Bunburying
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To live freely, pursuing pleasure and escaping the constraints of duty and identity
beat_states.obstacle: Cecily's sharp wit keeps wrong-footing him, and she insists on him being wicked — a role he must calibrate without overplaying or underplaying

affect_state.rationale: Genuinely surprised and delighted by Cecily's beauty and wit, but thrown off balance by her paradoxical demands; he keeps adjusting his claims about his wickedness based on her reactions, revealing he's not in control of this exchange

social_state.rationale: Despite being the arriving guest and older party, Cecily commands the conversational dynamic; Algernon is reactive, adjusting to her lead, showing genuine warmth and growing fascination

Jack has a young ward named Cecily in the country
Jack has used a fictional brother 'Ernest' as a Bunbury excuse
He is here without Jack's knowledge or permission

epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon has full knowledge of the deception and is the only character in the scene who knows the truth, giving him epistemic advantage — yet Cecily's wit makes that advantage feel precarious

beat_states.defense_state: Self-deprecating modesty to appear attractively humble while carefully managing how much wickedness to claim
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is pretending to be someone who doesn't exist (Ernest) while also trying to be genuinely liked as himself — he's caught between performing a fiction and feeling real attraction
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon may already be falling genuinely in love rather than merely Bunburying for sport, which would explain his unusual willingness to follow Cecily's lead rather than dominating the exchange

beat_states.desire_state: To meet and romantically engage with the thrillingly 'wicked' Ernest she has fantasized about
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To escape the boredom of her sheltered life and experience romantic adventure
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon is disappointingly ordinary in appearance and keeps undermining his own wickedness

affect_state.rationale: Excited and delighted by the arrival of this romantic figure she's long imagined, but performing composure and wit; her playful corrections about her height and her paradoxical logic reveal both confidence and a fluttering nervousness underneath

social_state.rationale: As the younger cousin and hostess she holds a slightly lower social position, but she seizes conversational dominance through her wit, creating warmth through flirtation disguised as moral instruction

beat_states.defense_state: Performing sophisticated wit and worldliness to mask her naïveté and excitement
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She wants him to be wicked for the thrill, but is also genuinely drawn to him as a person — she simultaneously demands wickedness and gently scolds him for it

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: It is much pleasanter being here with you.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I can’t understand how you are here at all. Uncle Jack won’t be back till Monday afternoon.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: That is a great disappointment. I am obliged to go up by the first train on Monday morning. I have a business appointment that I am anxious . . . to miss?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Couldn’t you miss it anywhere but in London?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: No: the appointment is in London.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Well, I know, of course, how important it is not to keep a business engagement, if one wants to retain any sense of the beauty of life, but still I think you had better wait till Uncle Jack arrives. I know he wants to speak to you about your emigrating.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: About my what?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Your emigrating. He has gone up to buy your outfit.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I certainly wouldn’t let Jack buy my outfit. He has no taste in neckties at all.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I don’t think you will require neckties. Uncle Jack is sending you to Australia.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Australia! I’d sooner die.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Well, he said at dinner on Wednesday night, that you would have to choose between this world, the next world, and Australia.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Oh, well! The accounts I have received of Australia and the next world, are not particularly encouraging. This world is good enough for me, cousin Cecily.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Yes, but are you good enough for it?

beat_states.desire_state: To charm Cecily and prolong his stay in the country under his false identity as Ernest
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To pursue pleasure and novelty without consequence, escaping the constraints of social obligation
beat_states.obstacle: Jack's plan to get rid of 'Ernest' (emigration/Australia) and Cecily's awareness of this plan, plus the ticking clock of Jack's return

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is enjoying the flirtation and the game, but each revelation about Jack's plans (emigration, Australia) creates moments of genuine alarm beneath the witty surface. He's improvising rapidly, not fully in control.

social_state.rationale: He positions himself as the charming, worldly visitor, but Cecily keeps wrong-footing him with information he doesn't have, subtly undermining his status. His warmth is high — he's actively courting her.

beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Cecily's final line ('are you good enough for it?') could be read less as moral testing and more as overt flirtation disguised as moralizing — a provocation rather than a genuine question.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I’m afraid I’m not that. That is why I want you to reform me. You might make that your mission, if you don’t mind, cousin Cecily.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I’m afraid I’ve no time, this afternoon.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Well, would you mind my reforming myself this afternoon?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: It is rather Quixotic of you. But I think you should try.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I will. I feel better already.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: You are looking a little worse.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: That is because I am hungry.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: How thoughtless of me. I should have remembered that when one is going to lead an entirely new life, one requires regular and wholesome meals. Won’t you come in?

beat_states.desire_state: To charm Cecily into spending time with him and deepening their connection
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To pursue pleasure and novelty wherever it presents itself, conquering through wit and charm
beat_states.obstacle: Cecily's playful resistance and his own fraudulent identity as 'Ernest' which limits how genuine he can be

social_state.rationale: He deliberately positions himself as the supplicant needing reform, lowering his status to flatter Cecily, while radiating warmth and charm to draw her in

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Thank you. Might I have a buttonhole first? I never have any appetite unless I have a buttonhole first.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: A Marechal Niel?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: No, I’d sooner have a pink rose.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Why?

beat_states.desire_state: To create an intimate, flirtatious moment with Cecily through the ritual of the buttonhole flower
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To pursue pleasure and romantic conquest while maintaining his performative identity as Ernest
beat_states.obstacle: He must sustain the fiction of being Ernest while deepening the connection without appearing too forward

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is in his element — flirting, performing, controlling the social tempo. His request for a pink rose over a Marechal Niel is a deliberate romantic signal, confident and playful. He feels pleasure and mastery in the exchange.

social_state.rationale: He positions himself as the charming guest making delicate requests, slightly elevated by his worldliness, but warmly attentive to Cecily, drawing her into a shared aesthetic game.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Because you are like a pink rose, Cousin Cecily.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I don’t think it can be right for you to talk to me like that. Miss Prism never says such things to me.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Then Miss Prism is a short-sighted old lady. You are the prettiest girl I ever saw.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Miss Prism says that all good looks are a snare.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: They are a snare that every sensible man would like to be caught in.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Oh, I don’t think I would care to catch a sensible man. I shouldn’t know what to talk to him about.

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: You are too much alone, dear Dr. Chasuble. You should get married. A misanthrope I can understand—a womanthrope, never!

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: Believe me, I do not deserve so neologistic a phrase. The precept as well as the practice of the Primitive Church was distinctly against matrimony.

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: That is obviously the reason why the Primitive Church has not lasted up to the present day. And you do not seem to realise, dear Doctor, that by persistently remaining single, a man converts himself into a permanent public temptation. Men should be more careful; this very celibacy leads weaker vessels astray.

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: But is a man not equally attractive when married?

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: No married man is ever attractive except to his wife.

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: And often, I’ve been told, not even to her.

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: That depends on the intellectual sympathies of the woman. Maturity can always be depended on. Ripeness can be trusted. Young women are green. I spoke horticulturally. My metaphor was drawn from fruits. But where is Cecily?

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: Perhaps she followed us to the schools.

beat_states.desire_state: To engage with Miss Prism's attention while maintaining his clerical identity and intellectual dignity
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To preserve his respectable position in the community while cautiously enjoying the pleasures of Miss Prism's companionship
beat_states.obstacle: His clerical role demands celibacy, but he is drawn into Miss Prism's flirtatious argumentation

beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Chasuble may be genuinely committed to celibacy and merely engaging in academic debate rather than flirtation, but his witty provocations suggest active enjoyment of the romantic subtext

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: Mr. Worthing!

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: Mr. Worthing?

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: This is indeed a surprise. We did not look for you till Monday afternoon.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I have returned sooner than I expected. Dr. Chasuble, I hope you are well?

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: Dear Mr. Worthing, I trust this garb of woe does not betoken some terrible calamity?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: My brother.

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: More shameful debts and extravagance?

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: Still leading his life of pleasure?

beat_states.desire_state: To offer pastoral concern to Jack while demonstrating his clerical usefulness and moral authority
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To fulfill his role as spiritual shepherd of the community and be valued for his learning and pastoral care
