epistemic_state.rationale: Jack is confident in his scheme but completely unaware that Algernon has arrived as 'Ernest,' which will catastrophically undermine his story.

beat_states.defense_state: Performing grief through mourning clothes to preemptively establish credibility for his lie
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is pretending to grieve for a brother who never existed while Algernon is at this very moment in the garden pretending to BE that brother

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Dead!

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: Your brother Ernest dead?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Quite dead.

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: What a lesson for him! I trust he will profit by it.

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: Mr. Worthing, I offer you my sincere condolence. You have at least the consolation of knowing that you were always the most generous and forgiving of brothers.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Poor Ernest! He had many faults, but it is a sad, sad blow.

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: Very sad indeed. Were you with him at the end?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: No. He died abroad; in Paris, in fact. I had a telegram last night from the manager of the Grand Hotel.

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: Was the cause of death mentioned?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: A severe chill, it seems.

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: As a man sows, so shall he reap.

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: Charity, dear Miss Prism, charity! None of us are perfect. I myself am peculiarly susceptible to draughts. Will the interment take place here?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: No. He seems to have expressed a desire to be buried in Paris.

beat_states.desire_state: To fulfill his pastoral duty by offering appropriate spiritual comfort and conducting himself with clerical dignity
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To be recognized as a worthy, learned, and compassionate clergyman
beat_states.obstacle: The awkwardness of the situation and Miss Prism's harsh moralizing, which he must gently correct while remaining tactful

affect_state.rationale: Mildly somber in keeping with the occasion, low arousal as he defaults to practiced clerical demeanor. Moderate control as he navigates between consoling Jack and correcting Miss Prism.

social_state.rationale: Occupying his expected clerical authority, showing genuine warmth and charity toward the supposedly deceased, and gentle warmth toward Miss Prism even in correction

known_facts: Jack claims his brother Ernest has died in Paris
Ernest reportedly died of a severe chill
Ernest wished to be buried in Paris
false_beliefs: Ernest was a real person
Jack is genuinely grieving
epistemic_state.rationale: Chasuble takes everything at face value with no reason to doubt Jack's account. He is entirely credulous.

beat_states.defense_state: Retreating into clerical formality and ritual phrases to navigate an emotionally complex situation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He gently rebukes Miss Prism's judgmental comments but also reveals personal vulnerability ('I myself am peculiarly susceptible to draughts'), undermining his pastoral authority with comic self-exposure

beat_states.desire_state: To convince everyone that his fictional brother Ernest has died, establishing the narrative convincingly so he can eventually drop the Ernest persona
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his respectable identity and win acceptance while managing his double life
beat_states.obstacle: He must perform grief convincingly while improvising details about a death that never happened

affect_state.rationale: Internally pleased that his plan is working, but must maintain a convincingly sad exterior. High control as he steers the conversation, moderate arousal from the performance demands.

social_state.rationale: Elevated status as the bereaved brother receiving sympathy and condolence; projecting a muted, sorrowful warmth appropriate to mourning

known_facts: Ernest never existed
He invented this death to free himself from the Ernest fiction
He is lying to Chasuble and Miss Prism
He needs to maintain this story consistently
hidden_secrets: There is no brother Ernest
He himself has been posing as Ernest in London
Algernon is at this moment in the house pretending to be Ernest
false_beliefs: He believes his plan to kill off Ernest will succeed cleanly
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack possesses complete knowledge that this is a fabrication, while everyone else takes it at face value. He is unaware Algernon has arrived as 'Ernest,' which will destroy his scheme.

beat_states.defense_state: Performative mourning — using exaggerated grief displays to deflect scrutiny and forestall difficult questions
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He must appear devastated about the death of someone who never existed, requiring him to simulate emotion he cannot feel while also secretly feeling relief at shedding a burdensome fiction

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: In Paris! I fear that hardly points to any very serious state of mind at the last. You would no doubt wish me to make some slight allusion to this tragic domestic affliction next Sunday. My sermon on the meaning of the manna in the wilderness can be adapted to almost any occasion, joyful, or, as in the present case, distressing. I have preached it at harvest celebrations, christenings, confirmations, on days of humiliation and festal days. The last time I delivered it was in the Cathedral, as a charity sermon on behalf of the Society for the Prevention of Discontent among the Upper Orders. The Bishop, who was present, was much struck by some of the analogies I drew.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Ah! that reminds me, you mentioned christenings I think, Dr. Chasuble? I suppose you know how to christen all right? I mean, of course, you are continually christening, aren’t you?

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: It is, I regret to say, one of the Rector’s most constant duties in this parish. I have often spoken to the poorer classes on the subject. But they don’t seem to know what thrift is.

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: But is there any particular infant in whom you are interested, Mr. Worthing? Your brother was, I believe, unmarried, was he not?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Oh yes.

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: People who live entirely for pleasure usually are.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: But it is not for any child, dear Doctor. I am very fond of children. No! the fact is, I would like to be christened myself, this afternoon, if you have nothing better to do.

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: But surely, Mr. Worthing, you have been christened already?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I don’t remember anything about it.

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: But have you any grave doubts on the subject?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I certainly intend to have. Of course I don’t know if the thing would bother you in any way, or if you think I am a little too old now.

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: Not at all. The sprinkling, and, indeed, the immersion of adults is a perfectly canonical practice.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Immersion!

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: You need have no apprehensions. Sprinkling is all that is necessary, or indeed I think advisable. Our weather is so changeable. At what hour would you wish the ceremony performed?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Oh, I might trot round about five if that would suit you.

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: Perfectly, perfectly! In fact I have two similar ceremonies to perform at that time. A case of twins that occurred recently in one of the outlying cottages on your own estate. Poor Jenkins the carter, a most hard-working man.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Oh! I don’t see much fun in being christened along with other babies. It would be childish. Would half-past five do?

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: Admirably! Admirably! And now, dear Mr. Worthing, I will not intrude any longer into a house of sorrow. I would merely beg you not to be too much bowed down by grief. What seem to us bitter trials are often blessings in disguise.

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: This seems to me a blessing of an extremely obvious kind.

beat_states.desire_state: to demonstrate his pastoral usefulness and intellectual versatility while accommodating Jack's unusual request
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to be recognized as a learned, indispensable figure in the community and worthy of admiration (particularly from Miss Prism)
beat_states.obstacle: Jack's absurd request for adult christening strains the boundaries of pastoral propriety, and he must maintain dignity while being accommodating

affect_state.rationale: Comfortably in his element — dispensing pastoral advice, showing off his sermon's versatility, and feeling needed. Slightly elevated by the opportunity to perform a christening for a gentleman. The weather comment reveals total ease and lack of existential concern.

social_state.rationale: Occupies a respected but subordinate position to Jack as landowner; performs warmth through pastoral solicitude ('dear Mr. Worthing'), but maintains professional authority on ecclesiastical matters.

known_facts: Jack claims his brother Ernest has died in Paris
Jack wants to be christened as an adult
He has twin christenings already scheduled at five
The Bishop appreciated his manna sermon
hidden_secrets: His romantic feelings for Miss Prism
false_beliefs: That Jack's brother Ernest actually existed and has died
That his one sermon is genuinely adaptable to all occasions
That Jack's desire for christening stems from genuine spiritual need
epistemic_state.rationale: Chasuble takes everything at face value with clerical credulity; he never questions the oddity of an adult requesting christening or the suspicious circumstances of the 'death.'

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — wraps every situation in ecclesiastical procedure and scholarly references to avoid confronting the absurdity
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Genuinely believes in his pastoral importance while being completely oblivious to how his one-sermon-fits-all approach reveals his intellectual shallowness

beat_states.desire_state: to arrange a christening under the name 'Ernest' so he can legitimately adopt that identity for Gwendolen
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to secure Gwendolen's love by becoming 'Ernest' in fact, maintaining his double life's resolution
beat_states.obstacle: He must manipulate the conversation toward christening without revealing his true motive, while maintaining the fiction of mourning his 'brother'

affect_state.rationale: Beneath the mourning costume, Jack is energized and goal-directed. He's pleased his plan is working but anxious about the details (immersion startles him). His petulance about sharing with twins shows his real emotional state — entitled, scheming, not grieving at all.

social_state.rationale: As the local landowner he holds social authority over Chasuble; his warmth is strategic ('dear Doctor') rather than genuine, deployed to get what he wants.

known_facts: Ernest Worthing never existed — he invented the brother
He needs to be christened 'Ernest' to satisfy Gwendolen
He has just 'killed off' Ernest to simplify his life
Algernon may be causing complications
hidden_secrets: Ernest is a complete fabrication
His real motive for christening is to change his name to Ernest
He is not grieving at all
false_beliefs: That this christening scheme will resolve his identity problem cleanly
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack holds all the cards epistemically — he knows the entire deception while everyone else believes his fiction. His false belief is that this plan will work smoothly.

beat_states.defense_state: performance — sustaining an elaborate grief charade while carefully steering the conversation to his real goal
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Performing deep mourning for a fictional death while internally scheming and feeling no grief whatsoever; his impatience about being christened 'with other babies' reveals his vanity breaking through the mourning mask

beat_states.desire_state: to assert her moral authority and intellectual superiority while enjoying proximity to Dr. Chasuble
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain her identity as a respectable, morally unimpeachable woman of learning, burying her past transgression
beat_states.obstacle: She is a subordinate figure who can only interject judgmentally; her moralizing masks deeper anxieties about her own past

affect_state.rationale: She's enjoying herself — getting to moralize, display her wit, and share the scene with Chasuble. Her certainty is high because she's on comfortable territory (judging others). The vulnerability traces to the buried secret about the handbag and baby that 'Ernest's death' might unconsciously stir.

social_state.rationale: Subordinate to both Jack (employer) and Chasuble (clergyman), but claims moral authority through sharp judgments. Her warmth is low — her comments are barbed and self-satisfied rather than compassionate.

known_facts: Jack claims his brother died in Paris
She disapproves of Ernest's alleged lifestyle
She has strong opinions about thrift and morality
Chasuble is unmarried
hidden_secrets: She lost the baby Jack in a handbag at Victoria Station years ago
She wrote a three-volume novel
Her connection to Jack's true origins
false_beliefs: That Ernest Worthing was a real person who actually died
That Jack's grief is genuine
epistemic_state.rationale: Miss Prism is the character with the most consequential hidden knowledge in the play — the secret of Jack's origins — but she has compartmentalized it so thoroughly she doesn't connect present events to her past.

beat_states.defense_state: projection — condemns pleasure-seekers and the 'poorer classes' to distance herself from her own hidden shame
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Presents herself as an arbiter of moral rectitude while harboring the secret of the lost baby in a handbag; her final line's cutting wit ('blessing of an extremely obvious kind') reveals she actually relishes Ernest's death, contradicting Christian charity
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Her final cutting remark could be read as genuine relief that a corrupting influence on the household is gone, rather than veiled cruelty

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Uncle Jack! Oh, I am pleased to see you back. But what horrid clothes you have got on! Do go and change them.

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: Cecily!

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: My child! my child!

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: What is the matter, Uncle Jack? Do look happy! You look as if you had toothache, and I have got such a surprise for you. Who do you think is in the dining-room? Your brother!

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Who?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Your brother Ernest. He arrived about half an hour ago.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: What nonsense! I haven’t got a brother.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Oh, don’t say that. However badly he may have behaved to you in the past he is still your brother. You couldn’t be so heartless as to disown him. I’ll tell him to come out. And you will shake hands with him, won’t you, Uncle Jack?

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: These are very joyful tidings.

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: After we had all been resigned to his loss, his sudden return seems to me peculiarly distressing.

beat_states.desire_state: To cheer Uncle Jack up and reunite him with his 'brother' Ernest
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To live in a world of romantic excitement and narrative drama, making life conform to her fantasy
beat_states.obstacle: Jack's mourning clothes and denial that he has a brother

affect_state.rationale: Cecily is bubbling with excitement and certainty — she believes she has wonderful news. She feels no threat, only the pleasure of delivering a surprise.

social_state.rationale: As the young ward she is socially subordinate, but she exercises surprising influence through her warmth, enthusiasm, and moral appeal to Jack's better nature.

known_facts: A man calling himself Ernest Worthing has arrived
Uncle Jack appears to be in mourning clothes
She believes this man is Jack's wayward brother
false_beliefs: Jack actually has a brother named Ernest
The man in the dining room is genuinely Ernest Worthing
epistemic_state.rationale: Cecily has been told about Ernest for years and takes Algernon's impersonation at face value. She has no idea that Ernest is Jack's fiction or that the visitor is Algernon.

beat_states.defense_state: Innocent obliviousness — she deflects all dark or contradictory signals by reframing them positively
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She is genuinely kind and warm but is also imposing her romantic fantasy onto reality, refusing to hear Jack's actual words

beat_states.desire_state: To respond to the situation with appropriate pastoral sentiment
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To be seen as a wise, relevant moral authority in the community
beat_states.obstacle: The rapid reversals (death then resurrection of Ernest) leave him scrambling to find the right register

affect_state.rationale: Mildly pleased, mildly confused, defaulting to a generically positive clerical response. Not deeply invested either way.

social_state.rationale: Maintains his clerical authority through formulaic pronouncements; warm but generic.

known_facts: Jack announced Ernest died in Paris
Cecily says Ernest is alive and in the dining room
false_beliefs: Ernest is a real person
This is a genuine family reunion
epistemic_state.rationale: Chasuble takes everything at face value and simply pivots his pastoral response to match the latest information.

beat_states.defense_state: Platitude — he retreats to stock phrases ('joyful tidings') to avoid engaging with the complexity of the situation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He just offered condolences for Ernest's death and now celebrates his return with equal solemnity, revealing the mechanical emptiness of his responses

beat_states.desire_state: To kill off the Ernest fiction permanently and be done with his double life
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain respectability and win Gwendolen while keeping his carefully constructed life intact
beat_states.obstacle: Someone claiming to be his fictional brother Ernest has actually appeared, completely undermining his plan to announce Ernest's death

affect_state.rationale: Jack is blindsided and horrified. His elaborate scheme has backfired spectacularly. He is losing control of his own narrative in front of witnesses.

social_state.rationale: His authority as head of the household is being undermined — he arrived in mourning expecting sympathy and control, and instead his ward is managing the situation and correcting him.

known_facts: He invented Ernest as a fictional brother
He has just announced Ernest's death
Someone is in the dining room claiming to be Ernest
hidden_secrets: Ernest never existed — he is Jack's alter ego for trips to London
He uses the Ernest persona to court Gwendolen
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack knows the truth about Ernest but doesn't yet know who the impostor is (Algernon). He is in a state of epistemic panic — someone has hijacked his fiction.

beat_states.defense_state: Flat contradiction — he bluntly states 'I haven't got a brother' as if sheer assertion can undo reality
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He has just elaborately staged Ernest's death and now must confront that 'Ernest' has materialized in the flesh; his fiction has escaped his control

beat_states.desire_state: To express her disapproval of Ernest's return and reassert moral order
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain propriety and moral rigor in the household, suppressing anything unruly or disruptive
beat_states.obstacle: Everyone else (especially Cecily) is treating Ernest's return as good news

affect_state.rationale: Annoyed and disapproving. She was comfortable with the moral clarity of Ernest's death and is unsettled by his resurrection, which disrupts her narrative of righteous consequence.

social_state.rationale: She positions herself as the voice of sober moral judgment against the general enthusiasm, which gives her authority but distances her from warmth.

known_facts: Jack announced Ernest died in Paris
Cecily claims Ernest is in the dining room
Ernest was reputed to be dissolute
hidden_secrets: Her own past secret involving a novel/handbag (not yet revealed)
false_beliefs: Ernest is a real person who has actually returned
epistemic_state.rationale: Like Chasuble, she accepts the surface narrative but filters it through her moralistic lens, interpreting Ernest's return as an affront to propriety.

beat_states.defense_state: Moral displacement — she channels her personal irritation into a posture of principled disapproval
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She says the return is 'distressing' after they were 'resigned to his loss,' revealing she actually preferred Ernest dead — her moralism masks a punitive streak

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: My brother is in the dining-room? I don’t know what it all means. I think it is perfectly absurd.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Good heavens!

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Brother John, I have come down from town to tell you that I am very sorry for all the trouble I have given you, and that I intend to lead a better life in the future.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Uncle Jack, you are not going to refuse your own brother’s hand?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Nothing will induce me to take his hand. I think his coming down here disgraceful. He knows perfectly well why.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Uncle Jack, do be nice. There is some good in every one. Ernest has just been telling me about his poor invalid friend Mr. Bunbury whom he goes to visit so often. And surely there must be much good in one who is kind to an invalid, and leaves the pleasures of London to sit by a bed of pain.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Oh! he has been talking about Bunbury, has he?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Yes, he has told me all about poor Mr. Bunbury, and his terrible state of health.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Bunbury! Well, I won’t have him talk to you about Bunbury or about anything else. It is enough to drive one perfectly frantic.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Of course I admit that the faults were all on my side. But I must say that I think that Brother John’s coldness to me is peculiarly painful. I expected a more enthusiastic welcome, especially considering it is the first time I have come here.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Uncle Jack, if you don’t shake hands with Ernest I will never forgive you.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Never forgive me?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Never, never, never!

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Well, this is the last time I shall ever do it.

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: It’s pleasant, is it not, to see so perfect a reconciliation? I think we might leave the two brothers together.

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: Cecily, you will come with us.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Certainly, Miss Prism. My little task of reconciliation is over.

utterances.speaker: CHASUBLE
utterances.text: You have done a beautiful action to-day, dear child.

utterances.speaker: MISS PRISM
utterances.text: We must not be premature in our judgments.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I feel very happy.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: You young scoundrel, Algy, you must get out of this place as soon as possible. I don’t allow any Bunburying here.

utterances.speaker: MERRIMAN
utterances.text: I have put Mr. Ernest’s things in the room next to yours, sir. I suppose that is all right?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: What?

utterances.speaker: MERRIMAN
utterances.text: Mr. Ernest’s luggage, sir. I have unpacked it and put it in the room next to your own.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: His luggage?

utterances.speaker: MERRIMAN
utterances.text: Yes, sir. Three portmanteaus, a dressing-case, two hat-boxes, and a large luncheon-basket.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I am afraid I can’t stay more than a week this time.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Merriman, order the dog-cart at once. Mr. Ernest has been suddenly called back to town.

utterances.speaker: MERRIMAN
utterances.text: Yes, sir.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: What a fearful liar you are, Jack. I have not been called back to town at all.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Yes, you have.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I haven’t heard any one call me.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Your duty as a gentleman calls you back.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: My duty as a gentleman has never interfered with my pleasures in the smallest degree.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I can quite understand that.

beat_states.desire_state: To remain at the manor as long as possible, presumably to pursue Cecily
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To live for pleasure and avoid the tedium of obligation, experiencing life's delights freely
beat_states.obstacle: Jack is the master of the house and is actively trying to have him removed

affect_state.rationale: Thoroughly enjoying himself. The luggage detail (three portmanteaus, luncheon-basket) shows he planned a comfortable invasion. His quip about not staying more than a week is pure gleeful provocation. He feels secure because Jack can't expose him without exposing himself.

social_state.rationale: Though technically a guest who should defer to his host, Algernon holds the upper hand through blackmail-adjacent knowledge of Jack's double life. His warmth toward Jack is superficial — playful antagonism rather than genuine hostility or affection.

known_facts: Jack's real name is Jack, not Ernest
Jack has been Bunburying in London under the name Ernest
Cecily is Jack's ward and lives at the manor
Jack cannot expose him without revealing his own deception
hidden_secrets: He is likely already attracted to Cecily and this is his real motivation for staying
He discovered Jack's country address from a cigarette case
false_beliefs: He believes his charm and wit will protect him from any real consequences
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon has a strategic advantage: mutual assured destruction of reputations. He knows Jack's secret gives him leverage to remain.

beat_states.defense_state: Weaponized insouciance — treating Jack's moral arguments as absurd and deflecting every attempt at ejection with wit
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He accuses Jack of being a 'fearful liar' while himself perpetrating an elaborate impersonation

beat_states.desire_state: To expel Algernon from his country estate immediately before the deception deepens
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain control of his double life and protect his respectable identity
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon has already unpacked and settled in, Merriman treats him as a legitimate guest, and Algernon refuses to cooperate

affect_state.rationale: Increasingly frustrated and panicked as each revelation (luggage, three portmanteaus, Algernon's brazen refusal) shows him losing grip on the situation. His rapid-fire attempts to eject Algernon betray desperation beneath the commanding tone.

social_state.rationale: Nominally the master of the house giving orders to servants, but Algernon's cheek and Merriman's oblivious compliance undermine his authority. Coldly hostile toward Algernon, delivering insults ('young scoundrel') and a cutting final barb.

known_facts: Algernon is impersonating his fictional brother Ernest
Algernon has brought extensive luggage suggesting a long stay
Cecily and the household have accepted Algernon as Ernest
Algernon knows about his Bunbury double life
hidden_secrets: He himself invented Ernest as a cover for his double life
He intended to kill off Ernest this very visit
He is in love with Gwendolen and proposed as Ernest
false_beliefs: He believes he can simply order Algernon away and restore the status quo
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack understands the full scope of the deception but underestimates Algernon's determination to stay and the complications already set in motion.

beat_states.defense_state: Asserting authority as master of the house to compensate for his loss of control over the situation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is furious at Algernon's Bunburying while being himself the original Bunburyist whose invented brother created this opening

beat_states.desire_state: To fulfill his duties correctly and receive clear instructions from his master
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his position through impeccable, unquestioning service
beat_states.obstacle: Jack's increasingly agitated and contradictory responses make it difficult to know how to proceed

affect_state.rationale: Mildly confused by Jack's shocked reactions to routine announcements about the guest's luggage. Maintains professional composure but senses something is off.

social_state.rationale: Occupies the subordinate servant role, deferential and formal. Politely neutral warmth — neither cold nor familiar.

known_facts: A gentleman named Mr. Ernest has arrived and is staying
Mr. Ernest brought extensive luggage
Jack wants the dog-cart ordered immediately
false_beliefs: The visitor is genuinely Jack's brother Ernest
epistemic_state.rationale: Merriman takes the situation entirely at face value. He has no reason to doubt the identity of the guest and simply follows orders.

beat_states.defense_state: Professional neutrality — retreating behind the formality of his role to avoid the tension between Jack and Algernon
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Merriman may be subtly amused by the chaos but his professionalism masks it entirely

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Well, Cecily is a darling.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: You are not to talk of Miss Cardew like that. I don’t like it.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Well, I don’t like your clothes. You look perfectly ridiculous in them. Why on earth don’t you go up and change? It is perfectly childish to be in deep mourning for a man who is actually staying for a whole week with you in your house as a guest. I call it grotesque.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: You are certainly not staying with me for a whole week as a guest or anything else. You have got to leave . . . by the four-five train.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I certainly won’t leave you so long as you are in mourning. It would be most unfriendly. If I were in mourning you would stay with me, I suppose. I should think it very unkind if you didn’t.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Well, will you go if I change my clothes?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Yes, if you are not too long. I never saw anybody take so long to dress, and with such little result.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Well, at any rate, that is better than being always over-dressed as you are.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: If I am occasionally a little over-dressed, I make up for it by being always immensely over-educated.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Your vanity is ridiculous, your conduct an outrage, and your presence in my garden utterly absurd. However, you have got to catch the four-five, and I hope you will have a pleasant journey back to town. This Bunburying, as you call it, has not been a great success for you.

beat_states.desire_state: To stay at the Manor House as long as possible, specifically to remain near Cecily
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To escape boredom and pursue pleasure wherever it presents itself, regardless of social rules
beat_states.obstacle: Jack is ordering him to leave and is furious at his deception

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is thoroughly enjoying himself. He's charmed by Cecily and delighted by the absurdity of Jack's mourning clothes. He feels in control because Jack cannot physically force him to leave and his wit gives him the upper hand in every exchange.

social_state.rationale: Algernon holds the higher status position throughout — he's the one being witty, composed, and dictating terms (he'll leave IF Jack changes). His warmth toward Jack is superficially friendly but fundamentally dismissive and self-serving.

known_facts: Cecily is Jack's ward and lives at the Manor House
Jack has invented a brother named Ernest as a Bunbury device
Jack is wearing mourning clothes for a fictional death
He himself is impersonating Ernest
He is attracted to Cecily
hidden_secrets: His real motive for staying is Cecily, not concern for Jack
He has no intention of catching the four-five train
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon understands the full mechanics of both his and Jack's deceptions. He knows exactly what he's doing and why. His agreement to leave if Jack changes clothes is almost certainly a stalling tactic.

beat_states.defense_state: Wit as armor — he redirects every attack Jack makes into a trivial matter of fashion or manners, refusing to engage with the substance of Jack's anger
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He claims to stay out of friendly concern for Jack's mourning, but his real motivation is Cecily — a desire he only half-reveals
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon might genuinely believe he can talk his way into staying indefinitely, rather than having a specific plan — his confidence may be spontaneous rather than strategic.

beat_states.desire_state: To get Algernon out of his house immediately before the deception spirals further out of control
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his respectable double life and ultimately win Gwendolen's hand in marriage
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon refuses to leave and keeps deflecting every demand with wit, making Jack's authority impotent

affect_state.rationale: Jack is irritated and increasingly desperate. He keeps issuing ultimatums but each one gets deflected or negotiated down. His agreeing to change clothes is a concession — evidence he's losing the battle. He's anxious about the threat Algernon poses to his carefully constructed world.

social_state.rationale: Despite being the host and the elder authority figure, Jack is losing status with every exchange — Algernon outmaneuvers him verbally at every turn. His warmth is actively hostile; he wants Algernon gone.

known_facts: Algernon has arrived pretending to be his fictional brother Ernest
Cecily and Miss Prism have welcomed Algernon as Ernest
His mourning clothes are now absurd since 'Ernest' is alive and present
Algernon knows about his double life
hidden_secrets: He has been living a double life as Ernest in town
He is in love with Gwendolen and wants to marry her
He planned to kill off Ernest to simplify his life
false_beliefs: He believes Algernon will actually leave on the four-five train if he changes his clothes
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack understands the danger of the situation but underestimates Algernon's determination to stay. His false belief that the bargain about clothes will work shows he doesn't fully grasp how outmatched he is.

beat_states.defense_state: Rigid insistence on propriety and schedule (the four-five train) as a way to assert control over a situation that is slipping away from him
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is furious at Algernon's Bunburying while being himself the original Bunburyist — he is outraged by the very behavior he invented
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Jack may on some level enjoy the sparring — his final speech ('Your vanity is ridiculous...') has the cadence of someone performing exasperation rather than truly feeling it, suggesting the relationship dynamic is more affectionately combative than purely hostile.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I think it has been a great success. I’m in love with Cecily, and that is everything. But I must see her before I go, and make arrangements for another Bunbury. Ah, there she is.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Oh, I merely came back to water the roses. I thought you were with Uncle Jack.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: He’s gone to order the dog-cart for me.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Oh, is he going to take you for a nice drive?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: He’s going to send me away.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Then have we got to part?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I am afraid so. It’s a very painful parting.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: It is always painful to part from people whom one has known for a very brief space of time. The absence of old friends one can endure with equanimity. But even a momentary separation from anyone to whom one has just been introduced is almost unbearable.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: MERRIMAN
utterances.text: The dog-cart is at the door, sir.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: It can wait, Merriman for . . . five minutes.

utterances.speaker: MERRIMAN
utterances.text: Yes, Miss.

beat_states.desire_state: To secure a private moment with Cecily before being sent away and establish grounds for returning
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To escape the tedium of obligation through romantic adventure and self-invention
beat_states.obstacle: Jack is actively trying to expel him; time is running out with the dog-cart being ordered

affect_state.rationale: Buoyant from declaring himself in love, excited by the romantic game, but aware he's on borrowed time and at Jack's mercy regarding his removal

social_state.rationale: Playing the romantic underdog being cast out — lower status than usual due to Jack's authority here — but projecting maximum warmth and charm toward Cecily

known_facts: Jack wants him gone immediately
He is posing as Jack's brother Ernest
Cecily is receptive to him
The dog-cart has been ordered
hidden_secrets: His real name is Algernon, not Ernest
He came to the country under false pretenses to meet Cecily
He plans to 'Bunbury' again to return
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon is fully aware of his own deception and is strategically planning his next move even as he performs sincerity

beat_states.defense_state: Framing his expulsion as romantic tragedy to elicit Cecily's sympathy and attachment
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims to be in love with Cecily yet simultaneously frames this as another 'Bunbury' — genuine feeling wrapped in habitual deception
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon may genuinely be falling in love for the first time and the Bunbury language is simply habit masking real feeling he doesn't yet know how to express authentically

beat_states.desire_state: To prolong the encounter with Algernon/Ernest and deepen the romantic connection
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To live out the romantic fantasy she has constructed in her diary and imagination
beat_states.obstacle: Uncle Jack is sending Algernon away; social propriety limits how openly she can pursue him

affect_state.rationale: Excited by the romantic drama of parting, genuinely moved but also relishing the performance of it; exercises surprising control over the situation by delaying the dog-cart

social_state.rationale: Despite being younger and theoretically subordinate, she commands Merriman and controls the tempo of the scene; warmth toward Algernon is high but stylized through Wildean wit

known_facts: This man is supposedly Ernest, Jack's wicked brother
Uncle Jack wants to send him away
She finds him attractive and interesting
hidden_secrets: She has likely already fantasized about Ernest extensively in her diary before meeting him
false_beliefs: She believes this man is Ernest Worthing
She believes he is genuinely Jack's brother
epistemic_state.rationale: Cecily operates entirely within the false identity Algernon has constructed; her romantic feelings are genuine but directed at a fiction

beat_states.defense_state: Performing naive innocence ('I merely came back to water the roses') to conceal her deliberate pursuit of him
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Performs guileless innocence while exercising decisive authority (dismissing the dog-cart, controlling Merriman) — she is both ingenue and commander
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Cecily's epigrams about parting may reveal she is more self-aware about performing romance than genuinely feeling it — she may be testing whether Algernon can match her wit

beat_states.desire_state: To fulfill his duty by announcing the dog-cart and receive clear instructions
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain the smooth functioning of the household and his position within it
beat_states.obstacle: Cecily countermands the implicit instruction from Jack by delaying the departure

affect_state.rationale: Emotionally neutral, low arousal, simply performing his role; slight uncertainty about whose authority takes precedence but trained not to show it

social_state.rationale: Occupies the lowest social status in the scene as a servant; warmth is minimal and purely professional

known_facts: Jack ordered the dog-cart
The dog-cart is ready
Cecily has told him to wait five minutes
epistemic_state.rationale: Merriman has straightforward functional knowledge; he likely perceives the romantic dynamic but it is not his place to acknowledge it

beat_states.defense_state: Professional neutrality — retreating behind servant protocol to avoid taking sides between Jack's wishes and Cecily's command
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Knows the dog-cart was ordered by Jack (the master) but accepts Cecily's delay without protest — caught between two authorities

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I hope, Cecily, I shall not offend you if I state quite frankly and openly that you seem to me to be in every way the visible personification of absolute perfection.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I think your frankness does you great credit, Ernest. If you will allow me, I will copy your remarks into my diary.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Do you really keep a diary? I’d give anything to look at it. May I?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Oh no. You see, it is simply a very young girl’s record of her own thoughts and impressions, and consequently meant for publication. When it appears in volume form I hope you will order a copy. But pray, Ernest, don’t stop. I delight in taking down from dictation. I have reached ‘absolute perfection’. You can go on. I am quite ready for more.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Ahem! Ahem!

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Oh, don’t cough, Ernest. When one is dictating one should speak fluently and not cough. Besides, I don’t know how to spell a cough.

beat_states.desire_state: To charm and win Cecily's affection through lavish compliments
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To live freely and pleasurably, pursuing whatever delights him without consequence
beat_states.obstacle: Cecily's wit keeps deflecting and reframing his romantic overtures into something transactional and comic, leaving him off-balance

affect_state.rationale: He begins confidently with his grand compliment but is progressively destabilized as Cecily treats his romantic speech as dictation. His coughing reveals he's run out of material and lost control of the scene.

social_state.rationale: Despite attempting to hold the high-status position of the romantic suitor, Cecily has subtly inverted the dynamic—she is directing him, and he is performing for her. His warmth toward her is genuine and growing.

known_facts: Cecily is charming and witty
He is pretending to be Ernest
Jack wants him to leave
hidden_secrets: His real name is Algernon
He invented Bunbury to escape obligations
He came here specifically because Jack tried to keep Cecily from him
false_beliefs: He believes he is leading this courtship
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon thinks he's in command of the seduction, but Cecily is clearly steering the interaction. He doesn't yet realize how deeply she has already constructed a fantasy around 'Ernest.'

beat_states.defense_state: Retreats into nervous stalling ('Ahem! Ahem!') when his prepared romantic script runs out and Cecily demands more
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He fancies himself the sophisticated seducer but finds himself being managed and directed by a young country girl
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon may be deliberately playing the flustered suitor as part of his charm, using apparent vulnerability as a deeper seduction tactic

beat_states.desire_state: To enjoy and control the romantic encounter she has long fantasized about with 'Ernest'
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To experience the romantic adventure and excitement that her sheltered country life denies her
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon's compliments, while welcome, are generic and insufficient—she wants a grander, more sustained romantic performance

affect_state.rationale: She is thoroughly enjoying herself, confident in her command of the situation. Her delight is genuine but filtered through ironic self-possession. She shows low vulnerability because her diary conceit gives her a protective frame.

social_state.rationale: Despite being younger and ostensibly the one being courted, she has seized the higher-status position by becoming the director/editor of his romantic speech. Her warmth is real but playfully withholding.

known_facts: She believes this man is Ernest, Jack's wicked brother
She has been keeping a diary
She has already constructed a fantasy about Ernest
hidden_secrets: She may have already invented an entire engagement with Ernest in her diary before meeting him
false_beliefs: This man is actually named Ernest
His compliments are spontaneous rather than rehearsed seduction
epistemic_state.rationale: Cecily operates under the key false belief about his identity, but her instinct for managing the encounter suggests she understands romantic performance on a meta-level even if she doesn't see through his specific deception.

beat_states.defense_state: Transforms genuine romantic vulnerability into literary performance by framing the encounter as 'dictation,' maintaining emotional distance through wit
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She is both a naive young girl genuinely thrilled by romantic attention and a shrewdly self-aware comic intelligence who controls every beat of the conversation
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Cecily's diary business could be read as genuine naivety rather than control—she literally wants to preserve his words because she's smitten—but her commanding tone ('don't stop,' 'don't cough') argues for the more knowing reading

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Cecily, ever since I first looked upon your wonderful and incomparable beauty, I have dared to love you wildly, passionately, devotedly, hopelessly.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I don’t think that you should tell me that you love me wildly, passionately, devotedly, hopelessly. Hopelessly doesn’t seem to make much sense, does it?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Cecily!

utterances.speaker: MERRIMAN
utterances.text: The dog-cart is waiting, sir.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Tell it to come round next week, at the same hour.

utterances.speaker: MERRIMAN
utterances.text: Yes, sir.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Uncle Jack would be very much annoyed if he knew you were staying on till next week, at the same hour.

beat_states.desire_state: To win Cecily's love through an extravagant romantic declaration
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To live fully in the moment, pursuing pleasure and romance without consequence under his false identity
beat_states.obstacle: Cecily's playful deflection undermines his rhetorical momentum; the dog-cart's arrival threatens to end his visit

affect_state.rationale: He is exhilarated by the romantic game and genuinely attracted to Cecily, but her logical deflection of 'hopelessly' catches him off guard, slightly destabilizing his control. His dismissal of the dog-cart shows he's committing fully.

social_state.rationale: He positions himself as the ardent suitor, temporarily lowering status through romantic supplication, but his command over Merriman reasserts his social authority. High warmth toward Cecily throughout.

known_facts: He is posing as Ernest, Jack's brother
Cecily is Jack's ward
Jack wants him gone
The dog-cart was ordered to take him away
hidden_secrets: His real name is Algernon Moncrieff
He invented this visit specifically to meet Cecily
He is Jack's friend, not his brother
false_beliefs: He believes his extravagant rhetoric is irresistibly charming
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon maintains the Ernest deception while being unaware that Cecily has already constructed an elaborate fantasy engagement with 'Ernest' in her diary.

beat_states.defense_state: Treats practical interruptions (the dog-cart, Uncle Jack's disapproval) as trivial inconveniences to be dismissed with aristocratic ease
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: His declaration of 'hopeless' love is entirely performative — he is actually supremely confident and enjoying himself
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon may be genuinely falling in love rather than merely playing a game, which would make his dismissal of the dog-cart an impulsive emotional decision rather than a calculated move

beat_states.desire_state: To engage with Algernon's romantic declarations on her own terms, maintaining intellectual control over the courtship
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To experience the romance she has already scripted in her diary, but to manage and shape it according to her own imaginative authority
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon's rhetorical excess threatens to make the scene less satisfying than her pre-written version; she must correct and edit him in real time

affect_state.rationale: She is enjoying the courtship enormously but from a position of surprising control. Her critique of 'hopelessly' shows she is not swept away — she is curating the experience. Her warning about Uncle Jack subtly tests whether Algernon will stay.

social_state.rationale: Despite being younger and ostensibly the pursued party, she holds higher effective status by critiquing and directing Algernon's declarations. Warm but composed.

known_facts: She believes this man is Ernest, Jack's wicked brother
Uncle Jack wants Ernest to leave
She has already imagined and written about this romance in her diary
The dog-cart was meant to take him away
hidden_secrets: She has already written their engagement into her diary
She has been in love with the idea of Ernest before meeting him
false_beliefs: She believes Algernon's name is Ernest
She believes he is Jack's brother
epistemic_state.rationale: Cecily is operating from a richly imagined interior narrative that she has not yet revealed, giving her a sense of foreknowledge about how this romance 'should' go.

beat_states.defense_state: Uses logical analysis of his language to maintain emotional distance and power in the exchange
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She wants passionate romance but cannot resist intellectually dismantling it — she is simultaneously the romantic heroine and the literary critic of her own love scene
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Cecily's critique of 'hopelessly' could be genuine naïveté rather than wit — though the overall pattern of her behavior strongly suggests deliberate intellectual play

beat_states.desire_state: To fulfill his duty by announcing the dog-cart and receiving instructions
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain the smooth operation of the household and uphold professional decorum regardless of circumstances
beat_states.obstacle: He is interrupting an intimate romantic exchange, creating social awkwardness, and receives an absurd instruction he must accept without comment

affect_state.rationale: Neutral affect maintained through professional training. Low arousal. Some uncertainty about the household chaos but no personal emotional investment visible.

social_state.rationale: Occupies the lowest status position as servant. Maintains formal, minimal warmth — purely professional interaction.

known_facts: The dog-cart was ordered by Jack for Ernest's departure
The guest is not leaving as planned
Jack will likely be displeased
false_beliefs: He likely believes the guest is Ernest, Jack's brother
epistemic_state.rationale: Merriman has practical household knowledge and can likely sense the developing romantic situation, but his role requires him to process only explicit instructions.

beat_states.defense_state: Professional impassivity — he processes Algernon's nonsensical order without visible reaction
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He must treat the patently ridiculous instruction ('tell it to come round next week at the same hour') as perfectly reasonable

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Oh, I don’t care about Jack. I don’t care for anybody in the whole world but you. I love you, Cecily. You will marry me, won’t you?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: You silly boy! Of course. Why, we have been engaged for the last three months.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: For the last three months?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Yes, it will be exactly three months on Thursday.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: But how did we become engaged?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Well, ever since dear Uncle Jack first confessed to us that he had a younger brother who was very wicked and bad, you of course have formed the chief topic of conversation between myself and Miss Prism. And of course a man who is much talked about is always very attractive. One feels there must be something in him, after all. I daresay it was foolish of me, but I fell in love with you, Ernest.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Darling! And when was the engagement actually settled?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: On the 14th of February last. Worn out by your entire ignorance of my existence, I determined to end the matter one way or the other, and after a long struggle with myself I accepted you under this dear old tree here. The next day I bought this little ring in your name, and this is the little bangle with the true lover’s knot I promised you always to wear.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Did I give you this? It’s very pretty, isn’t it?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Yes, you’ve wonderfully good taste, Ernest. It’s the excuse I’ve always given for your leading such a bad life. And this is the box in which I keep all your dear letters.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: My letters! But, my own sweet Cecily, I have never written you any letters.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: You need hardly remind me of that, Ernest. I remember only too well that I was forced to write your letters for you. I wrote always three times a week, and sometimes oftener.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Oh, do let me read them, Cecily?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Oh, I couldn’t possibly. They would make you far too conceited. The three you wrote me after I had broken off the engagement are so beautiful, and so badly spelled, that even now I can hardly read them without crying a little.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: But was our engagement ever broken off?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Of course it was. On the 22nd of last March. You can see the entry if you like. ‘To-day I broke off my engagement with Ernest. I feel it is better to do so. The weather still continues charming.’

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: But why on earth did you break it off? What had I done? I had done nothing at all. Cecily, I am very much hurt indeed to hear you broke it off. Particularly when the weather was so charming.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: It would hardly have been a really serious engagement if it hadn’t been broken off at least once. But I forgave you before the week was out.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: What a perfect angel you are, Cecily.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: You dear romantic boy. I hope your hair curls naturally, does it?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Yes, darling, with a little help from others.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I am so glad.

beat_states.desire_state: To win Cecily's hand in marriage and enjoy the delightful absurdity of discovering he's already engaged
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To live aesthetically and pleasurably, turning life into an entertaining fiction free of boring responsibility
beat_states.obstacle: The entire courtship has been pre-scripted by Cecily without his participation, leaving him perpetually catching up to a narrative he didn't author

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is genuinely charmed and amused but constantly disoriented — each revelation (the engagement, the ring, the letters, the breakup) catches him off-guard. He's lost control of the courtship narrative but finds the loss pleasurable rather than threatening. His vulnerability is real: he is emotionally invested and keeps asking sincere questions.

social_state.rationale: Cecily holds the dominant position throughout — she has authored the entire relationship and dispenses its history to him like a teacher. Algernon willingly cedes status, calling her 'a perfect angel,' expressing genuine warmth and admiration.

known_facts: Cecily has been 'engaged' to him for three months
She bought herself a ring and bangle in his name
She wrote all the love letters herself
The engagement was broken off on March 22nd and then restored
His hair curls with assistance
hidden_secrets: His real name is Algernon, not Ernest
He is not Jack's brother but Jack's friend
He invented the identity of 'Bunbury' to escape obligations
He came to the country specifically to meet Cecily after seeing her photograph
false_beliefs: That maintaining the 'Ernest' fiction will be sufficient to sustain this engagement
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon is learning Cecily's elaborate fantasy history in real time while concealing his entire true identity. He's too enchanted to worry about the inevitable unraveling.

beat_states.defense_state: Embracing the absurdity completely rather than questioning it — deflecting any discomfort by treating the whole invented history as charming rather than alarming
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He came to seduce and conquer, but discovers he has already been conquered; his vanity as a pursuer conflicts with his delight at having been pursued
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon could be performing delight strategically to maintain the fiction and secure Cecily, but his repeated genuine surprise suggests authentic enchantment.

beat_states.desire_state: To have her meticulously constructed romantic fantasy validated and made real by the living Ernest standing before her
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To transform the monotony of her sheltered country life into the romantic narrative she craves, authored entirely on her own terms
beat_states.obstacle: The real person in front of her may not match the fictional Ernest she has constructed — she must manage the gap between her diary fantasy and reality

affect_state.rationale: Cecily is in her element — she wrote this script and is now directing its performance. Her certainty is high because she has documentary 'evidence' for every claim. Her vulnerability is low because she holds all the cards, though her final question about his hair curling reveals a flicker of genuine anxiety that the real man might not match the fantasy.

social_state.rationale: Despite being younger and ostensibly the ingenue, Cecily holds clear social dominance in this exchange — she educates Algernon about his own love life. Her warmth is genuine but expressed through playful condescension ('You silly boy,' 'You dear romantic boy').

known_facts: Uncle Jack has a wicked younger brother named Ernest
She has maintained a detailed diary chronicling an imaginary relationship
She bought physical tokens (ring, bangle) to materialize the fantasy
She wrote all correspondence on both sides
hidden_secrets: The entire relationship was a solitary fantasy before today
Her attraction is fundamentally to the name 'Ernest' and the idea of wickedness, not to the actual person
false_beliefs: That this man is truly named Ernest
That he is genuinely Jack's younger brother
That his wickedness is real rather than a fiction Jack invented
epistemic_state.rationale: Cecily is deeply invested in a fiction layered on top of Jack's fiction. She doesn't know that 'Ernest' is really Algernon, that he's not Jack's brother, or that the entire wicked-brother story was Jack's cover for his double life.

beat_states.defense_state: Treating her invented narrative as objective historical fact, presenting diary entries as evidence, forestalling any challenge to the fiction's legitimacy
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She presents herself as innocent and passive ('worn out by your entire ignorance of my existence') while being the sole architect and controller of the entire relationship — her demureness masks total authorial dominance
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Cecily may be more self-aware about the absurdity of her invented engagement than she lets on — her precise diary citations could be a deliberate comedic performance to test how far Algernon will play along, rather than genuine naivety.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: You’ll never break off our engagement again, Cecily?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I don’t think I could break it off now that I have actually met you. Besides, of course, there is the question of your name.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Yes, of course.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: You must not laugh at me, darling, but it had always been a girlish dream of mine to love some one whose name was Ernest. There is something in that name that seems to inspire absolute confidence. I pity any poor married woman whose husband is not called Ernest.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: But, my dear child, do you mean to say you could not love me if I had some other name?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: But what name?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Oh, any name you like—Algernon—for instance . . .

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: But I don’t like the name of Algernon.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Well, my own dear, sweet, loving little darling, I really can’t see why you should object to the name of Algernon. It is not at all a bad name. In fact, it is rather an aristocratic name. Half of the chaps who get into the Bankruptcy Court are called Algernon. But seriously, Cecily . . . . . . if my name was Algy, couldn’t you love me?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I might respect you, Ernest, I might admire your character, but I fear that I should not be able to give you my undivided attention.

beat_states.desire_state: To secure Cecily's love and commitment despite the looming problem of his real name
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To live pleasurably and without consequence, pursuing whatever delights him in the moment
beat_states.obstacle: Cecily's romantic fixation on the name 'Ernest' — his real name is Algernon, which she explicitly rejects

affect_state.rationale: Algernon begins the beat confident and affectionate but grows increasingly anxious as he probes the name issue. His attempt to casually introduce 'Algernon' as a hypothetical is a high-stakes gamble. Cecily's flat rejection leaves him exposed and scrambling, using charm and wit to mask real worry.

social_state.rationale: Despite being the suitor in a nominally dominant courtship position, Cecily holds all the power here — she sets the terms. His escalating endearments ('my own dear, sweet, loving little darling') reveal his supplicant position. His warmth is genuine and increasingly desperate.

He is posing as Jack's fictional brother Ernest
Cecily is deeply attached to the name Ernest
Cecily has definitively rejected the name Algernon
