There is no brother Ernest — he is Jack's friend perpetrating Bunbury-style deception
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon is fully aware of his deception and is now confronting its consequences in real time. He tests the waters to see if his real identity could be accepted, and the answer is decisively no.

beat_states.defense_state: Deflection through humor — joking about bankruptcy courts to soften the blow of floating his real name
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is genuinely falling for Cecily while simultaneously maintaining a deception that, if revealed, would destroy the connection he's building
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon may be less genuinely worried and more playfully testing boundaries, treating the name issue as another absurd social convention he can eventually charm his way around

beat_states.desire_state: To confirm and preserve the romantic fantasy she has constructed around 'Ernest'
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To experience the grand, storybook romance she has imagined and scripted in her diary
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon's unsettling hypothetical suggestion that his name might not be Ernest

affect_state.rationale: Cecily is serene and authoritative in her convictions. She shows no anxiety about the hypothetical — she simply states her terms with charming, absolute certainty. Her vulnerability is low because she doesn't perceive any real threat to her fantasy; she assumes she is with Ernest.

social_state.rationale: Cecily holds clear power in this exchange. She is the one setting conditions, and Algernon must meet them. Her warmth is genuine but conditional — affectionate as long as the terms of her fantasy are met. Her final line is a devastating, sweetly delivered verdict.

known_facts: She believes she is engaged to Ernest Worthing, Jack's wicked brother
She has maintained a fictional diary engagement for months
She has now met the man she believes to be Ernest
hidden_secrets: Her entire engagement history was fabricated in her diary before meeting him
false_beliefs: The man before her is named Ernest
Ernest Worthing actually exists as Jack's brother
epistemic_state.rationale: Cecily is operating entirely within her constructed romantic narrative. She has no suspicion that 'Ernest' is actually Algernon or that the name is a fiction. Her epistemic world is a self-authored fairy tale.

beat_states.defense_state: Romantic idealization used as a rigid boundary — the name 'Ernest' is non-negotiable, insulating her fantasy from reality
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She claims to love the person before her, yet her love is entirely conditional on a name — she loves an idea more than the man
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Cecily's insistence on the name may be a form of flirtatious play rather than genuine rigidity — she enjoys watching him squirm and perform devotion

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Ahem! Cecily! Your Rector here is, I suppose, thoroughly experienced in the practice of all the rites and ceremonials of the Church?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Oh, yes. Dr. Chasuble is a most learned man. He has never written a single book, so you can imagine how much he knows.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I must see him at once on a most important christening—I mean on most important business.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Oh!

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I shan’t be away more than half an hour.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Considering that we have been engaged since February the 14th, and that I only met you to-day for the first time, I think it is rather hard that you should leave me for so long a period as half an hour. Couldn’t you make it twenty minutes?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I’ll be back in no time.

beat_states.desire_state: To arrange his christening as 'Ernest' immediately so he can sustain the deception with Cecily
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To live pleasurably and without consequence by maintaining his Bunbury-like double life
beat_states.obstacle: He must leave Cecily to see Dr. Chasuble without revealing his true reason, and Cecily resists his departure

affect_state.rationale: Buoyant from the engagement but slightly anxious about the slip ('christening') and the urgency of getting rechristened before Jack returns or the truth emerges

social_state.rationale: He is performing the role of devoted fiancé, warmly promising a quick return, while also taking a somewhat commanding lead in arranging practical matters

known_facts: He is not actually Ernest — his real name is Algernon
He needs to be christened Ernest to maintain the deception
Dr. Chasuble is the local rector
Cecily believes they have been engaged since February 14th
hidden_secrets: His real name is Algernon Moncrieff
He is Jack's friend impersonating Jack's fictional brother Ernest
The 'important business' is arranging his own christening as Ernest
false_beliefs: He believes he can sustain this impersonation indefinitely
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon is fully aware of his deception and is racing to solidify it before it unravels, nearly betraying himself with the word 'christening'

beat_states.defense_state: Deflection — catching himself mid-sentence ('christening—I mean on most important business') to conceal his real purpose
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He is genuinely charmed by Cecily and wants to stay, yet must leave to solidify the very lie that makes their engagement possible
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon may also be testing whether Cecily's attachment is strong enough that a brief absence will cause her genuine distress, feeding his vanity

beat_states.desire_state: To keep Algernon ('Ernest') close and to enjoy the romantic reality of an engagement she has long fantasized about
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To bring her romantic fantasy life into reality and experience genuine love and adventure
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon insists on leaving, even briefly, which threatens the fragile magic of their newly materialized romance

affect_state.rationale: Happy and playful in the glow of engagement, but her insistence that he shorten his absence reveals genuine neediness beneath the wit

social_state.rationale: She holds slight relational power as the one being courted and left behind, and she wields it with affectionate teasing warmth

known_facts: She believes this man is Ernest, Jack's wicked brother
She has kept a diary documenting their fictional engagement since February 14th
Dr. Chasuble is a learned local rector
They have only just met in person today
hidden_secrets: The entire engagement history exists only in her diary and imagination
false_beliefs: This man is named Ernest Worthing
He is Jack's brother
Their diary-constructed engagement has genuine historical weight
epistemic_state.rationale: Cecily is innocent of Algernon's true identity and purpose; her epistemic world blends fantasy and reality seamlessly, which Wilde exploits for comedy

beat_states.defense_state: Playful guilt-tripping — using the absurd logic of their 'long engagement' to make him feel he owes her his presence
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She simultaneously inhabits the fiction of a months-long engagement and acknowledges they just met today, holding both as equally real
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Cecily's playful protest about half an hour may also be a shrewd test of his devotion — seeing how eagerly he promises to return

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: What an impetuous boy he is! I like his hair so much. I must enter his proposal in my diary.

utterances.speaker: MERRIMAN
utterances.text: A Miss Fairfax has just called to see Mr. Worthing. On very important business, Miss Fairfax states.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Isn’t Mr. Worthing in his library?

utterances.speaker: MERRIMAN
utterances.text: Mr. Worthing went over in the direction of the Rectory some time ago.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Pray ask the lady to come out here; Mr. Worthing is sure to be back soon. And you can bring tea.

utterances.speaker: MERRIMAN
utterances.text: Yes, Miss.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Miss Fairfax! I suppose one of the many good elderly women who are associated with Uncle Jack in some of his philanthropic work in London. I don’t quite like women who are interested in philanthropic work. I think it is so forward of them.

utterances.speaker: MERRIMAN
utterances.text: Miss Fairfax.

beat_states.desire_state: To savor and record her romantic triumph with 'Ernest' while maintaining composure as mistress of the house
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To live inside the romantic narrative she has constructed for herself
beat_states.obstacle: An unknown visitor interrupts her reverie and may complicate her fantasy world

affect_state.rationale: Basking in post-proposal glow, she feels in command of her domain and her diary narrative. Slight irritation at the interruption but confident in her authority as hostess.

social_state.rationale: Exercises clear authority over Merriman and the household, issuing orders naturally. Warmth is low toward the unseen Miss Fairfax, whom she preemptively categorizes and subtly disdains.

known_facts: Algernon (as Ernest) has just proposed and they are 'engaged'
Uncle Jack is not in his library
A Miss Fairfax has arrived on important business for Mr. Worthing
Jack went toward the Rectory
hidden_secrets: She has fabricated an entire prior engagement history with Ernest in her diary
false_beliefs: Algernon is actually Ernest Worthing, Jack's brother
Miss Fairfax must be an elderly philanthropic associate of Uncle Jack's
Her diary entries constitute real romantic history
epistemic_state.rationale: Cecily has no idea that Miss Fairfax is Gwendolen, Jack's young fiancée. Her assumption that the visitor is elderly and philanthropic is a comic false belief that sets up the coming confrontation.

beat_states.defense_state: Preemptively diminishing the unknown Miss Fairfax by imagining her as an elderly philanthropist, neutralizing any potential romantic threat
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She is deeply romantic and impetuous herself yet judges Miss Fairfax as 'forward' for being active in the world
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Cecily's dismissal of Miss Fairfax could also be read as unconscious territorial jealousy — she instinctively diminishes any woman connected to her household's men.

beat_states.desire_state: To fulfill his duties correctly by announcing the visitor and relaying information
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain the smooth operation of the household and his professional standing
beat_states.obstacle: Mr. Worthing is absent, requiring him to seek instructions from Cecily

affect_state.rationale: Completely composed and emotionally neutral; he is performing a routine function with professional precision.

social_state.rationale: Occupies the subordinate servant role, deferring to Cecily's instructions. Maintains respectful but minimal warmth appropriate to his station.

known_facts: Miss Fairfax has arrived and states she has important business
Mr. Worthing left in the direction of the Rectory
Mr. Worthing is not in his library
epistemic_state.rationale: Merriman knows the practical facts of household movements and relays them accurately. He has no stake in the personal dramas unfolding.

beat_states.defense_state: Professional neutrality — he conveys facts without editorial commentary

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Pray let me introduce myself to you. My name is Cecily Cardew.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Cecily Cardew? What a very sweet name! Something tells me that we are going to be great friends. I like you already more than I can say. My first impressions of people are never wrong.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: How nice of you to like me so much after we have known each other such a comparatively short time. Pray sit down.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: I may call you Cecily, may I not?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: With pleasure!

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: And you will always call me Gwendolen, won’t you?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: If you wish.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Then that is all quite settled, is it not?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I hope so.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Perhaps this might be a favourable opportunity for my mentioning who I am. My father is Lord Bracknell. You have never heard of papa, I suppose?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I don’t think so.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Outside the family circle, papa, I am glad to say, is entirely unknown. I think that is quite as it should be. The home seems to me to be the proper sphere for the man. And certainly once a man begins to neglect his domestic duties he becomes painfully effeminate, does he not? And I don’t like that. It makes men so very attractive. Cecily, mamma, whose views on education are remarkably strict, has brought me up to be extremely short-sighted; it is part of her system; so do you mind my looking at you through my glasses?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Oh! not at all, Gwendolen. I am very fond of being looked at.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: You are here on a short visit, I suppose.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Oh no! I live here.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Really? Your mother, no doubt, or some female relative of advanced years, resides here also?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Oh no! I have no mother, nor, in fact, any relations.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Indeed?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: My dear guardian, with the assistance of Miss Prism, has the arduous task of looking after me.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Your guardian?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Yes, I am Mr. Worthing’s ward.

beat_states.desire_state: To present herself as charming and hospitable while sizing up this unexpected visitor
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To live out a romantic fantasy life of her own crafting, where she controls the narrative
beat_states.obstacle: She doesn't know Gwendolen's connection to Ernest/Jack or her potential rivalry

affect_state.rationale: Pleasant and socially at ease as hostess, but increasingly uncertain as Gwendolen's questions narrow toward her guardian — the mention of Mr. Worthing as her guardian shifts the dynamic and she doesn't yet know why Gwendolen cares

social_state.rationale: As ward of a country house she has territorial home advantage, but Gwendolen's aristocratic lineage and London sophistication place Cecily in a slightly subordinate social position; she compensates with genuine warmth and hospitality

She is engaged (in her own mind) to Ernest Worthing (actually Algernon)
This visitor is named Gwendolen and is daughter of Lord Bracknell
She has no living relatives

beat_states.defense_state: Performing naïve sweetness as a shield — her innocence is both genuine and strategic
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She is genuinely open and warm while simultaneously being a young woman who writes her own diary entries as fiction — her candor always has a performative edge
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Cecily may already sense something slightly predatory in Gwendolen's line of questioning and is being more guarded than she appears

beat_states.desire_state: To investigate who Cecily is, determine her relationship to Ernest Worthing, and assess whether she is a romantic threat
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure her engagement to Ernest Worthing and maintain absolute control over her romantic destiny
beat_states.obstacle: She must gather intelligence while maintaining the performance of effortless aristocratic friendliness — she cannot reveal jealousy before she has cause

affect_state.rationale: Outwardly charming but internally on high alert — each answer Cecily gives ('I live here,' 'no relations,' 'Mr. Worthing's ward') escalates her suspicion; the beat ends with the word 'ward' landing like a blow, shifting her from social performance to genuine alarm

social_state.rationale: She leads the entire conversational rhythm — she initiates first names, announces her parentage, examines Cecily through her glasses like an object; her warmth is lavish but fundamentally patronizing, a London sophisticate condescending to a country girl

known_facts: She is engaged to Ernest Worthing (actually Jack)
Ernest has a country house
Cecily Cardew is Ernest Worthing's ward
Cecily lives here permanently with no family
Her own father is Lord Bracknell
hidden_secrets: She is engaged to Ernest Worthing and has come to the country specifically to find him
She is already suspicious about why a young, pretty girl lives in Ernest's house
false_beliefs: Her 'Ernest' (Jack) is actually named Ernest
She assumes Cecily is merely a ward with no romantic designs on Ernest
epistemic_state.rationale: Gwendolen's questions systematically extract information — does she live here, is anyone else here, who is her guardian — building a picture that increasingly threatens her; by the end of the beat she has discovered a beautiful young woman with no chaperone living in her fiancé's home

beat_states.defense_state: Performing extravagant warmth and condescension to maintain dominance while probing for threatening information
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Her effusive declarations of instant friendship are instruments of social control — she claims openness while conducting a covert investigation; her warmth tightens into alarm at 'Mr. Worthing's ward'
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gwendolen's warmth could be read as entirely genuine early in the beat, with suspicion only crystallizing at the final revelation of 'Mr. Worthing's ward'

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Oh! It is strange he never mentioned to me that he had a ward. How secretive of him! He grows more interesting hourly. I am not sure, however, that the news inspires me with feelings of unmixed delight. I am very fond of you, Cecily; I have liked you ever since I met you! But I am bound to state that now that I know that you are Mr. Worthing’s ward, I cannot help expressing a wish you were—well, just a little older than you seem to be—and not quite so very alluring in appearance. In fact, if I may speak candidly—

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Pray do! I think that whenever one has anything unpleasant to say, one should always be quite candid.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Well, to speak with perfect candour, Cecily, I wish that you were fully forty-two, and more than usually plain for your age. Ernest has a strong upright nature. He is the very soul of truth and honour. Disloyalty would be as impossible to him as deception. But even men of the noblest possible moral character are extremely susceptible to the influence of the physical charms of others. Modern, no less than Ancient History, supplies us with many most painful examples of what I refer to. If it were not so, indeed, History would be quite unreadable.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I beg your pardon, Gwendolen, did you say Ernest?

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Oh, but it is not Mr. Ernest Worthing who is my guardian. It is his brother—his elder brother.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Ernest never mentioned to me that he had a brother.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I am sorry to say they have not been on good terms for a long time.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Ah! that accounts for it. And now that I think of it I have never heard any man mention his brother. The subject seems distasteful to most men. Cecily, you have lifted a load from my mind. I was growing almost anxious. It would have been terrible if any cloud had come across a friendship like ours, would it not? Of course you are quite, quite sure that it is not Mr. Ernest Worthing who is your guardian?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Quite sure. In fact, I am going to be his.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: I beg your pardon?

beat_states.desire_state: To announce her engagement to Ernest (Algernon) and claim her own romantic territory
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To live out the romantic fantasy she has constructed in her diary and secure her 'Ernest'
beat_states.obstacle: Gwendolen's possessive, interrogative manner and social dominance threaten to overshadow Cecily's claim

affect_state.rationale: Cecily is buoyant with her new engagement and confident in her claim. She feels relatively secure because she believes she has a different Ernest than Gwendolen. Her vulnerability is low because she's on home turf and holds the informational advantage.

social_state.rationale: Cecily occupies the lower social status as the country girl and ward, but she compensates with genuine hospitality and pointed candor that subtly challenges Gwendolen's authority

known_facts: Her guardian is Jack Worthing
She believes Algernon is Ernest, Jack's younger brother
She is engaged to 'Ernest' (Algernon)
Gwendolen appears connected to her guardian Mr. Worthing
hidden_secrets: Her entire engagement, including the proposal, was essentially self-authored in her diary
false_beliefs: That Algernon is really named Ernest
That Algernon is truly Jack's brother
That her Ernest and Gwendolen's Ernest are two different people
epistemic_state.rationale: Cecily operates under Algernon's deception. She genuinely believes she and Gwendolen each have a separate Ernest, which gives her false confidence that there is no rivalry—until the next beat.

beat_states.defense_state: Naïve directness used as a weapon — her seeming innocence conceals strategic information-dropping
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Presents herself as sweet and guileless while deliberately withholding and then deploying the bombshell of her engagement with precise comic timing
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Cecily's 'I am going to be his' could be read as deliberately provocative rather than innocently declarative—she may enjoy the power of the revelation

beat_states.desire_state: To establish that Cecily is not a romantic rival for Ernest and to secure her exclusive claim on him
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To possess Ernest completely and control her romantic destiny with absolute authority
beat_states.obstacle: Cecily is young, beautiful, and lives in close proximity to 'Ernest' as his ward—a potential threat

affect_state.rationale: Relief floods in when she learns the guardian is the 'brother' not 'Ernest,' but the beat begins with sharp jealousy barely masked by politeness. Her final 'I beg your pardon?' shows her control cracking again as Cecily drops a new bombshell. She oscillates between territorial anxiety and performative warmth.

social_state.rationale: Gwendolen maintains her position as the sophisticated London woman dispensing judgments, but her warmth is strategically deployed—rising when Cecily seems non-threatening, dropping when new information unsettles her

known_facts: Ernest (Jack) is her fiancé
Cecily is Mr. Worthing's ward
Cecily claims the guardian is Ernest's elder brother, not Ernest himself
Cecily has just said 'I am going to be his'
hidden_secrets: Her engagement to Ernest is not yet fully sanctioned by her mother Lady Bracknell
false_beliefs: That Jack and 'Ernest' are two different people (Jack and his fictional brother)
That the man she knows as Ernest is truthful and honorable
epistemic_state.rationale: Gwendolen operates under the double fiction—she believes her Ernest is a separate person from Cecily's guardian. She has just received Cecily's cryptic 'I am going to be his,' which she hasn't yet decoded and which threatens to upend her relief.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — wrapping jealousy and territorial aggression in elaborate, pseudo-scholarly rhetoric about History and moral character
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims to be fond of Cecily and to trust Ernest completely, while her every word reveals deep suspicion and competitive anxiety
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gwendolen may already suspect what 'I am going to be his' means and her 'I beg your pardon?' is not confusion but a steely demand for clarification before she attacks

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Dearest Gwendolen, there is no reason why I should make a secret of it to you. Our little county newspaper is sure to chronicle the fact next week. Mr. Ernest Worthing and I are engaged to be married.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: My darling Cecily, I think there must be some slight error. Mr. Ernest Worthing is engaged to me. The announcement will appear in the _Morning Post_ on Saturday at the latest.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I am afraid you must be under some misconception. Ernest proposed to me exactly ten minutes ago.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: It is certainly very curious, for he asked me to be his wife yesterday afternoon at 5.30. If you would care to verify the incident, pray do so. I never travel without my diary. One should always have something sensational to read in the train. I am so sorry, dear Cecily, if it is any disappointment to you, but I am afraid I have the prior claim.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: It would distress me more than I can tell you, dear Gwendolen, if it caused you any mental or physical anguish, but I feel bound to point out that since Ernest proposed to you he clearly has changed his mind.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: If the poor fellow has been entrapped into any foolish promise I shall consider it my duty to rescue him at once, and with a firm hand.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Whatever unfortunate entanglement my dear boy may have got into, I will never reproach him with it after we are married.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Do you allude to me, Miss Cardew, as an entanglement? You are presumptuous. On an occasion of this kind it becomes more than a moral duty to speak one’s mind. It becomes a pleasure.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Do you suggest, Miss Fairfax, that I entrapped Ernest into an engagement? How dare you? This is no time for wearing the shallow mask of manners. When I see a spade I call it a spade.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: I am glad to say that I have never seen a spade. It is obvious that our social spheres have been widely different.

beat_states.desire_state: To assert and defend her claim to Ernest as her fiancé against Gwendolen's rival claim
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To live out the romantic fantasy she has constructed for herself and secure the love story she has authored in her diary
beat_states.obstacle: Gwendolen claims prior engagement to the same man and asserts social superiority

affect_state.rationale: Cecily escalates from polite announcement to open hostility. She feels threatened by Gwendolen's social pedigree and prior claim, which erodes her certainty. Her arousal rises sharply as the veneer of politeness drops and she explicitly rejects 'the shallow mask of manners.' Vulnerability is moderate—her claim rests on a fabricated diary entry.

social_state.rationale: Cecily begins with performative warmth ('Dearest Gwendolen') but by the end has dropped all pretense. Gwendolen's 'spade' remark lands as a devastating class put-down, leaving Cecily at a status disadvantage. Warmth plummets as the women move from endearments to open combat.

Algernon (as Ernest) proposed to her minutes ago
Gwendolen also claims to be engaged to Ernest Worthing

She has never actually met the real Ernest Worthing

beat_states.defense_state: Weaponizing directness and moral frankness as class-coded aggression disguised as sincerity
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Her entire engagement is a fiction she wrote in her diary, yet she defends it with genuine emotional ferocity as though it were real
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Cecily may be partly enjoying the theatrical drama of the confrontation, performing outrage as much as feeling it, given her documented love of romantic sensation

beat_states.desire_state: To assert her prior and superior claim to Ernest and demolish Cecily's pretensions
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure a husband named Ernest and maintain her position as a sophisticated woman of society who controls her own romantic destiny
beat_states.obstacle: Cecily claims a more recent (and therefore potentially superseding) engagement to the same Ernest

affect_state.rationale: Gwendolen is agitated but channels it into controlled aggression. She feels more certain of her claim (she has dates, a diary, the Morning Post announcement) but is genuinely threatened by Cecily's assertion that Ernest changed his mind. Her final 'spade' line is a calculated class kill-shot, indicating she feels she's regaining control.

social_state.rationale: Gwendolen consistently pulls rank—citing the Morning Post, her diary, and finally delivering the devastating class snub about never having seen a spade. She has abandoned all warmth and is now openly hostile beneath a thin gloss of pity ('I am so sorry, dear Cecily').
