She recorded the engagement in her diary
Cecily claims to also be engaged to Ernest Worthing
Cecily is Jack/Ernest's ward
hidden_secrets: She came to the country partly against her mother's wishes to pursue Ernest
false_beliefs: Jack's real name is Ernest
She and Cecily are rivals for the same man
Cecily may have 'entrapped' Ernest into a foolish promise
epistemic_state.rationale: Gwendolen believes Jack is Ernest and that Cecily's Ernest is the same person. She has no idea there are two imposters. Her assumption that Cecily might have entrapped Ernest reveals both her class prejudice and her need to narrativize the situation in her favor.

beat_states.defense_state: Deploying social superiority and class distinction as weapons to invalidate Cecily's claim
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She maintains exquisite manners while delivering increasingly savage attacks, taking visible pleasure in cruelty she frames as moral duty
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gwendolen may be less certain than she appears—her escalation to open class warfare ('never seen a spade') could be compensation for genuine anxiety that Ernest has indeed changed his mind

utterances.speaker: MERRIMAN
utterances.text: Shall I lay tea here as usual, Miss?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Yes, as usual.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Are there many interesting walks in the vicinity, Miss Cardew?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Oh! yes! a great many. From the top of one of the hills quite close one can see five counties.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Five counties! I don’t think I should like that; I hate crowds.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I suppose that is why you live in town?

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Quite a well-kept garden this is, Miss Cardew.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: So glad you like it, Miss Fairfax.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: I had no idea there were any flowers in the country.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Oh, flowers are as common here, Miss Fairfax, as people are in London.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Personally I cannot understand how anybody manages to exist in the country, if anybody who is anybody does. The country always bores me to death.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Ah! This is what the newspapers call agricultural depression, is it not? I believe the aristocracy are suffering very much from it just at present. It is almost an epidemic amongst them, I have been told. May I offer you some tea, Miss Fairfax?

beat_states.desire_state: To hold her ground against Gwendolen's condescension and prove herself an equal or superior wit
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure her romantic identity and autonomy against all who would diminish her
beat_states.obstacle: Gwendolen's social superiority and persistent belittling of country life

affect_state.rationale: Cecily is enjoying this verbal sparring match. She's alert and quick, feeling increasingly confident that she can match Gwendolen blow for blow. Her retorts about people in London and agricultural depression show growing pleasure in her own wit.

social_state.rationale: As hostess on her own territory she commands a certain status, but Gwendolen's London sophistication threatens it. Warmth has cooled significantly beneath the polite veneer — both women are now openly sparring through coded language.

known_facts: Gwendolen claims to be engaged to Ernest/Jack
Cecily believes she is engaged to Ernest/Algernon
Gwendolen is deliberately insulting her country life
She is the hostess and has the advantage of home ground
hidden_secrets: Her engagement to 'Ernest Worthing' (Algernon) is based on a fiction
Her diary contains invented romantic history
false_beliefs: Algernon is truly named Ernest
epistemic_state.rationale: Cecily perceives the rivalry clearly but doesn't yet know both 'Ernests' are fabrications. She reads Gwendolen's condescension accurately and responds strategically.

beat_states.defense_state: Deflects each insult with a clever counter-thrust disguised as innocent politeness
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Maintains the surface of a gracious hostess while delivering pointed barbs
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Cecily may be slightly more anxious beneath the surface than she lets on, using her wit as armor against genuine social insecurity about her country upbringing

beat_states.desire_state: To establish dominance over Cecily by asserting the superiority of London society and undermining the value of country life
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain absolute control over her social world and secure her claim on Ernest
beat_states.obstacle: Cecily refuses to be intimidated and keeps returning sharp, witty replies

affect_state.rationale: Gwendolen is becoming frustrated. Each attempt to condescend is met with a counter-blow. Her rapid topic changes (walks, garden, flowers, country life) reveal she's losing control of the exchange. Her arousal is rising as she escalates her attacks.

social_state.rationale: She carries the status of London society and Lady Bracknell's daughter, but she's on Cecily's territory. Her warmth is thoroughly performative — she's being openly hostile through the thinnest veil of politeness.

known_facts: Cecily claims to be engaged to Ernest Worthing
Jack/Ernest has a ward he never mentioned
She herself claims to be engaged to Ernest
hidden_secrets: She is deeply threatened by Cecily as a romantic rival
false_beliefs: Jack is truly named Ernest
Country people are genuinely inferior
epistemic_state.rationale: Gwendolen's knowledge is limited by her London-centric worldview. She doesn't yet realize the full scope of the name deception.

beat_states.defense_state: Maintains an air of cultivated superiority to mask her growing irritation at Cecily's resilience
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Presents herself as above this rivalry while clearly investing enormous energy in winning it
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gwendolen may genuinely believe in London's superiority rather than using it purely as a weapon — her snobbery may be sincere rather than strategic

beat_states.desire_state: To perform his duties correctly and receive instruction from his mistress
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain the smooth operation of the household and his professional standing
beat_states.obstacle: None significant — he is a functional presence

affect_state.rationale: Merriman is entirely neutral and professional. His single line is routine domestic business. He likely perceives the tension but it is not his place to engage with it.

social_state.rationale: As a servant he occupies a clearly subordinate position. His warmth is the minimal professional courtesy of good service.

known_facts: Tea is customarily served in the garden
Cecily has a guest
The household routines
epistemic_state.rationale: Merriman knows domestic facts and likely senses the undercurrent of hostility, but his role limits his engagement with the dramatic situation.

beat_states.defense_state: Professional detachment shields him from the social tension in the scene
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Merriman's entrance may serve as a deliberate dramatic device — breaking tension momentarily and providing Cecily the opportunity to reassert her role as hostess

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Thank you. Detestable girl! But I require tea!

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Sugar?

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: No, thank you. Sugar is not fashionable any more.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Cake or bread and butter?

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Bread and butter, please. Cake is rarely seen at the best houses nowadays.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Hand that to Miss Fairfax.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: You have filled my tea with lumps of sugar, and though I asked most distinctly for bread and butter, you have given me cake. I am known for the gentleness of my disposition, and the extraordinary sweetness of my nature, but I warn you, Miss Cardew, you may go too far.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: To save my poor, innocent, trusting boy from the machinations of any other girl there are no lengths to which I would not go.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: From the moment I saw you I distrusted you. I felt that you were false and deceitful. I am never deceived in such matters. My first impressions of people are invariably right.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: It seems to me, Miss Fairfax, that I am trespassing on your valuable time. No doubt you have many other calls of a similar character to make in the neighbourhood.

beat_states.desire_state: To drive Gwendolen away and defend her claim to Ernest/Algernon
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To live out the romantic narrative she has constructed for herself with 'Ernest' (Algernon)
beat_states.obstacle: Gwendolen's social polish, London authority, and competing claim to Ernest

affect_state.rationale: Cecily is energized and relatively in control — she's on her home turf, serving tea, wielding the power of hostess. Her sabotage is deliberate and successful. Less vulnerable than Gwendolen because she holds the tactical advantage in this moment.

social_state.rationale: As hostess she holds structural power over the tea service, which she weaponizes. Her final line — suggesting Gwendolen has 'many other calls of a similar character' — is a devastating dismissal, implying Gwendolen is common. Warmth is entirely hostile beneath a veneer of politeness.

known_facts: Gwendolen claims to be engaged to Ernest
She herself believes she is engaged to Ernest (Algernon)
Gwendolen prefers no sugar and bread and butter
She deliberately gave Gwendolen the opposite
hidden_secrets: Her diary-based engagement to 'Ernest' is largely a fiction she constructed
false_beliefs: That Algernon is truly named Ernest
That there is only one Ernest and Gwendolen is trying to steal him
epistemic_state.rationale: Like Gwendolen, Cecily doesn't know there are two men using the name Ernest. She genuinely believes Gwendolen is a rival for the same man.

beat_states.defense_state: Weaponizing hospitality — using the role of hostess as cover for deliberate cruelty, then feigning innocence behind moral righteousness about protecting 'her boy'
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She presents herself as the innocent, trusting girl protecting her fiancé, while executing a calculated, malicious act of sabotage through the tea service

beat_states.desire_state: To assert dominance over Cecily and establish her superior claim to Ernest/Jack
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure her romantic destiny with 'Ernest' and maintain her position as a woman of impeccable taste and authority
beat_states.obstacle: Cecily's passive-aggressive sabotage through the tea service and her refusal to yield the claim to Ernest

affect_state.rationale: Gwendolen is furious at being publicly humiliated through the tea service — sugar and cake instead of what she ordered. Her control is slipping as her polished surface cracks. She's certain of her moral position but losing control of the social situation.

social_state.rationale: She clings to her London sophistication and social knowledge ('not fashionable,' 'best houses') to maintain status, but Cecily's sabotage has undermined her authority as guest. Warmth is entirely gone — open hostility now.

known_facts: Cecily claims to be engaged to Ernest
Cecily deliberately gave her sugar and cake against her wishes
Cecily is Uncle Jack's ward
She herself believes she is engaged to Ernest Worthing
hidden_secrets: She is deeply threatened by Cecily's claim and youth
false_beliefs: That 'Ernest' is one person — Jack, her fiancé
That her first impressions are invariably right
epistemic_state.rationale: Gwendolen operates under the false belief that there is only one Ernest, so she sees Cecily as a direct rival. Her claim about her infallible first impressions is comic self-deception.

beat_states.defense_state: Performing aristocratic composure and moral superiority to mask her fury — claiming 'gentleness of disposition' while issuing threats
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She insists on her gentleness and sweetness while being openly hostile and threatening; she demands tea from someone she calls 'detestable'

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Ernest! My own Ernest!

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Gwendolen! Darling!

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: A moment! May I ask if you are engaged to be married to this young lady?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: To dear little Cecily! Of course not! What could have put such an idea into your pretty little head?

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Thank you. You may!

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I knew there must be some misunderstanding, Miss Fairfax. The gentleman whose arm is at present round your waist is my guardian, Mr. John Worthing.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: I beg your pardon?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: This is Uncle Jack.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Jack! Oh!

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Here is Ernest.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: My own love!

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: A moment, Ernest! May I ask you—are you engaged to be married to this young lady?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: To what young lady? Good heavens! Gwendolen!

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Yes! to good heavens, Gwendolen, I mean to Gwendolen.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Of course not! What could have put such an idea into your pretty little head?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Thank you. You may.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: I felt there was some slight error, Miss Cardew. The gentleman who is now embracing you is my cousin, Mr. Algernon Moncrieff.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Algernon Moncrieff! Oh!

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Are you called Algernon?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I cannot deny it.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Oh!

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Is your name really John?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I could deny it if I liked. I could deny anything if I liked. But my name certainly is John. It has been John for years.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: A gross deception has been practised on both of us.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: My poor wounded Cecily!

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: My sweet wronged Gwendolen!

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: You will call me sister, will you not?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: There is just one question I would like to be allowed to ask my guardian.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: An admirable idea! Mr. Worthing, there is just one question I would like to be permitted to put to you. Where is your brother Ernest? We are both engaged to be married to your brother Ernest, so it is a matter of some importance to us to know where your brother Ernest is at present.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Gwendolen—Cecily—it is very painful for me to be forced to speak the truth. It is the first time in my life that I have ever been reduced to such a painful position, and I am really quite inexperienced in doing anything of the kind. However, I will tell you quite frankly that I have no brother Ernest. I have no brother at all. I never had a brother in my life, and I certainly have not the smallest intention of ever having one in the future.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: No brother at all?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: None!

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Had you never a brother of any kind?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Never. Not even of any kind.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: I am afraid it is quite clear, Cecily, that neither of us is engaged to be married to any one.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: It is not a very pleasant position for a young girl suddenly to find herself in. Is it?

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Let us go into the house. They will hardly venture to come after us there.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: No, men are so cowardly, aren’t they?

beat_states.desire_state: to form an alliance with Gwendolen against the men who deceived them and to preserve her dignity
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to find romantic adventure and excitement while maintaining her sense of self-worth
beat_states.obstacle: the discovery that Ernest doesn't exist means her engagement and romantic fantasy are shattered

affect_state.rationale: Hurt by the deception but quickly regaining composure by allying with Gwendolen; the certainty of betrayal paradoxically gives her control over the emotional situation

social_state.rationale: Elevated from rival to sister-in-arms with Gwendolen; warm toward her new ally, cold and dismissive toward the men ('men are so cowardly')

known_facts: Jack has no brother Ernest
Jack has no brother at all
Both she and Gwendolen were deceived
Neither of them is actually engaged
hidden_secrets: She was the one who initially provoked Gwendolen with sugar and cake
epistemic_state.rationale: The truth is now fully out — she knows the Ernest fiction was a lie and processes this rapidly, shifting from shock to righteous indignation

beat_states.defense_state: deflection through sisterly solidarity — redirecting humiliation outward onto the men rather than internalizing it
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: she is genuinely wounded but performs the wound with theatrical relish, enjoying the drama of being wronged
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Cecily may be less hurt than she performs — the theatrical quality of her responses suggests she's somewhat thrilled by the dramatic situation

beat_states.desire_state: to assert moral superiority over the men and solidify her alliance with Cecily in dignified retreat
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to marry a man named Ernest and to always maintain perfect social composure and control
beat_states.obstacle: the complete collapse of her engagement — there is no Ernest, so her deepest romantic requirement cannot be met

affect_state.rationale: Wounded but maintaining impeccable composure; her methodical questioning of Jack shows she processes pain through control and protocol

social_state.rationale: Takes the leadership role — she frames the questions, makes the pronouncements, and directs the retreat into the house; warm toward Cecily as fellow victim, dominant over Jack

known_facts: Jack has no brother named Ernest
Jack has no brother at all and never did
Neither she nor Cecily is engaged
Jack has been lying about having a brother
hidden_secrets: The depth of her fixation on the name Ernest as a marriage requirement
epistemic_state.rationale: She has extracted the full truth through direct interrogation and now possesses complete clarity about the deception

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization and formality — she processes devastating news through procedural questioning and composed pronouncements
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: she is deeply invested in the name Ernest yet must now act as though the deception is the primary injury, not the loss of the name
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gwendolen's rapid pivot to sisterhood with Cecily may be as much about winning the social battle as genuine sympathy — she wants an ally more than a sister

beat_states.desire_state: to confess the truth while minimizing the damage to his relationships with both women
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to win Gwendolen's hand and maintain his respectable social standing
beat_states.obstacle: the truth itself — admitting there is no Ernest destroys both women's engagements and his own romantic prospects

affect_state.rationale: Cornered and exposed; the women's united front strips him of all social power; his repeated 'None! Never!' shows increasing desperation as each answer deepens his predicament

social_state.rationale: Utterly diminished — he is being interrogated by two women who have formed an alliance against him, and their retreat into the house is a final humiliation

known_facts: He has no brother Ernest or any brother
Both women believed they were engaged to Ernest
Algernon was impersonating Ernest
His Bunbury-style double life is fully exposed
hidden_secrets: He does not yet know his true parentage or whether he actually has a brother
He invented Ernest as a cover for his London life
false_beliefs: He believes he truly has no brother — this will be overturned in the final act
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack is being honest for once but ironically his 'truth' about having no brother will prove to be false — he is unknowingly wrong about his own family

beat_states.defense_state: self-deprecating charm — framing truth-telling as a novel and painful experience to soften the blow with humor
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he claims the truth is painful yet has been comfortable lying for years; his performative discomfort with honesty is itself a form of dishonesty

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: This ghastly state of things is what you call Bunburying, I suppose?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Yes, and a perfectly wonderful Bunbury it is. The most wonderful Bunbury I have ever had in my life.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Well, you’ve no right whatsoever to Bunbury here.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: That is absurd. One has a right to Bunbury anywhere one chooses. Every serious Bunburyist knows that.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Serious Bunburyist! Good heavens!

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Well, one must be serious about something, if one wants to have any amusement in life. I happen to be serious about Bunburying. What on earth you are serious about I haven’t got the remotest idea. About everything, I should fancy. You have such an absolutely trivial nature.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Well, the only small satisfaction I have in the whole of this wretched business is that your friend Bunbury is quite exploded. You won’t be able to run down to the country quite so often as you used to do, dear Algy. And a very good thing too.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Your brother is a little off colour, isn’t he, dear Jack? You won’t be able to disappear to London quite so frequently as your wicked custom was. And not a bad thing either.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: As for your conduct towards Miss Cardew, I must say that your taking in a sweet, simple, innocent girl like that is quite inexcusable. To say nothing of the fact that she is my ward.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I can see no possible defence at all for your deceiving a brilliant, clever, thoroughly experienced young lady like Miss Fairfax. To say nothing of the fact that she is my cousin.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I wanted to be engaged to Gwendolen, that is all. I love her.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Well, I simply wanted to be engaged to Cecily. I adore her.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: There is certainly no chance of your marrying Miss Cardew.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I don’t think there is much likelihood, Jack, of you and Miss Fairfax being united.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Well, that is no business of yours.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: If it was my business, I wouldn’t talk about it. It is very vulgar to talk about one’s business. Only people like stock-brokers do that, and then merely at dinner parties.

beat_states.desire_state: To deflect all blame, maintain his claim on Cecily, and enjoy the absurdity of the situation
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To live freely, pursuing pleasure and romance without constraint or accountability
beat_states.obstacle: Jack's authority as Cecily's guardian and his attempts to block the engagement

affect_state.rationale: More buoyant than Jack — genuinely amused by the symmetry and relishing the verbal sparring. Still confident in his charm. Some underlying vulnerability about actually wanting Cecily, which is a new experience for him.

social_state.rationale: Slightly wins the status contest through superior wit and composure. Low warmth toward Jack in this moment — competitive and needling — but there's an undercurrent of fraternal familiarity.

known_facts: Jack has been Bunburying to London under the name Ernest
Both deceptions are now exposed to Gwendolen and Cecily
Cecily was engaged to 'Ernest' through her own fantasy before he arrived
Jack is Cecily's guardian and could block a marriage
His own fictional Bunbury is now useless as an excuse
hidden_secrets: The depth of his genuine feeling for Cecily, which exceeds his usual frivolous attachments
false_beliefs: He believes his deception of Cecily is somehow less serious because he frames it as charming
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon is fully aware of the parallel deceptions but uses wit to obscure any moral equivalence, maintaining his self-image as the cleverer and more self-aware of the two.

beat_states.defense_state: Inversion — reframing every accusation as a compliment to himself and turning Jack's logic against him
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims to be 'serious about Bunburying' — genuinely fell for Cecily, meaning his escapist philosophy is now entangled with real feeling
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon's bravado may mask genuine anxiety that he has for the first time encountered a situation his wit alone cannot resolve — he actually wants something he might not get.

beat_states.desire_state: To shame Algernon for his deception and assert moral superiority while defending his own position
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his respectable identity and secure his relationship with Gwendolen
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon refuses to be shamed and mirrors every accusation back at Jack with equal force

affect_state.rationale: Frustrated and exposed; every attack he makes is deflected by Algernon's perfect counter-punch. His irritation grows as he realizes his moral leverage is nil. Beneath the bluster, he is genuinely worried about losing Gwendolen.

social_state.rationale: Tries to assert authority as host and guardian of Cecily, but Algernon consistently matches him move for move, neutralizing any status advantage. Warmth is low — brotherly antagonism laced with genuine annoyance.

known_facts: Algernon came to the country pretending to be Ernest
Both women have discovered the deception
His own Ernest fiction is now exposed
Gwendolen and Cecily know neither man is named Ernest
Algernon is courting Cecily under false pretenses
hidden_secrets: He invented the Ernest persona specifically to escape his responsibilities in the country
false_beliefs: He believes his deception of Gwendolen is somehow more justified than Algernon's deception of Cecily
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack has full knowledge of the mutual exposure but cannot see that his own conduct is a perfect mirror of Algernon's.

beat_states.defense_state: Projection — attacking Algernon for the exact same deceptions he himself has committed
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Genuinely outraged at Algernon's Bunburying while being equally guilty of the same practice; claims moral high ground he cannot hold
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Jack may be performing outrage partly as a ritual of male bonding — the quarrel has a comedic symmetry suggesting neither man takes it as seriously as they pretend.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: How can you sit there, calmly eating muffins when we are in this horrible trouble, I can’t make out. You seem to me to be perfectly heartless.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Well, I can’t eat muffins in an agitated manner. The butter would probably get on my cuffs. One should always eat muffins quite calmly. It is the only way to eat them.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I say it’s perfectly heartless your eating muffins at all, under the circumstances.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: When I am in trouble, eating is the only thing that consoles me. Indeed, when I am in really great trouble, as any one who knows me intimately will tell you, I refuse everything except food and drink. At the present moment I am eating muffins because I am unhappy. Besides, I am particularly fond of muffins.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Well, that is no reason why you should eat them all in that greedy way.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I wish you would have tea-cake instead. I don’t like tea-cake.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Good heavens! I suppose a man may eat his own muffins in his own garden.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: But you have just said it was perfectly heartless to eat muffins.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I said it was perfectly heartless of you, under the circumstances. That is a very different thing.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: That may be. But the muffins are the same.

beat_states.desire_state: to enjoy his muffins in peace and avoid confronting the emotional fallout of the deception
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to live aesthetically and freely, extracting pleasure from life while evading its consequences
beat_states.obstacle: Jack's persistent moral indignation and attempts to shame him into seriousness

affect_state.rationale: Perfectly composed and even enjoying the argument; his low arousal and high control are deliberate provocations — he is genuinely unruffled or performing unruffledness so convincingly it amounts to the same thing

social_state.rationale: Dominates the exchange through superior wit and logical trap-setting; nominally friendly but actually needling Jack at every turn by refusing to share his distress

known_facts: Both women are angry about the Ernest deception
Jack is genuinely distressed
He posed as Ernest to court Cecily
Muffins are delicious
hidden_secrets: He may genuinely care about Cecily but refuses to show vulnerability
false_beliefs: That the situation will resolve itself without serious effort on his part
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon understands the crisis intellectually but refuses to engage with it emotionally, converting all gravity into trivial aesthetic discourse

beat_states.defense_state: intellectualization — transforms emotional crisis into an aesthetic philosophy about the proper consumption of muffins
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Claims to be eating because he is unhappy, yet exhibits no unhappiness whatsoever — his self-diagnosed suffering is a rhetorical convenience
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon's obsessive muffin-eating may itself be a genuine anxiety response masked by his performance of calm — the eating is compulsive comfort-seeking dressed up as philosophy

beat_states.desire_state: to make Algernon feel guilty and stop his cavalier indifference to the crisis they've caused
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to secure his identity and win Gwendolen's love through respectability
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon's complete refusal to take the situation seriously, deflecting every accusation with witty logic about muffins

affect_state.rationale: Genuinely agitated about the women's departure and the exposure of his deceptions; his inability to control Algernon's nonchalance compounds his frustration and helplessness

social_state.rationale: Asserts property rights ('my own muffins in my own garden') to claim territorial dominance, but is being consistently outmaneuvered in the verbal sparring; irritable and cold toward Algernon

known_facts: Both women have discovered the Ernest deception
Gwendolen and Cecily have left in anger
Algernon invented Bunbury to deceive and has been posing as Ernest
His own double life as Ernest has been exposed
hidden_secrets: He still intends to be christened Ernest to win Gwendolen back
false_beliefs: That shaming Algernon will somehow improve the situation
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack knows the full scope of the disaster but fixates on Algernon's behavior as a proxy for his deeper panic

beat_states.defense_state: displacement — channeling his anxiety about losing Gwendolen onto the trivial issue of muffin-eating
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He criticizes Algernon for eating muffins heartlessly, then fights to eat the muffins himself — revealing his own desire for comfort while denying it to Algernon
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Jack may be unconsciously competing with Algernon over the muffins as a displaced struggle for control after losing control of the larger situation

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Algy, I wish to goodness you would go.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: You can’t possibly ask me to go without having some dinner. It’s absurd. I never go without my dinner. No one ever does, except vegetarians and people like that. Besides I have just made arrangements with Dr. Chasuble to be christened at a quarter to six under the name of Ernest.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: My dear fellow, the sooner you give up that nonsense the better. I made arrangements this morning with Dr. Chasuble to be christened myself at 5.30, and I naturally will take the name of Ernest. Gwendolen would wish it. We can’t both be christened Ernest. It’s absurd. Besides, I have a perfect right to be christened if I like. There is no evidence at all that I have ever been christened by anybody. I should think it extremely probable I never was, and so does Dr. Chasuble. It is entirely different in your case. You have been christened already.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Yes, but I have not been christened for years.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Yes, but you have been christened. That is the important thing.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Quite so. So I know my constitution can stand it. If you are not quite sure about your ever having been christened, I must say I think it rather dangerous your venturing on it now. It might make you very unwell. You can hardly have forgotten that some one very closely connected with you was very nearly carried off this week in Paris by a severe chill.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Yes, but you said yourself that a severe chill was not hereditary.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: It usen’t to be, I know—but I daresay it is now. Science is always making wonderful improvements in things.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Oh, that is nonsense; you are always talking nonsense.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Jack, you are at the muffins again! I wish you wouldn’t. There are only two left. I told you I was particularly fond of muffins.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: But I hate tea-cake.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Why on earth then do you allow tea-cake to be served up for your guests? What ideas you have of hospitality!

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Algernon! I have already told you to go. I don’t want you here. Why don’t you go!

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I haven’t quite finished my tea yet! and there is still one muffin left.

beat_states.desire_state: To stay, finish his tea and muffins, and secure his own christening as Ernest so he can win Cecily
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To pursue pleasure and romantic adventure without constraint — specifically to win Cecily by becoming Ernest
beat_states.obstacle: Jack demanding he leave and claiming exclusive right to the name Ernest

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is remarkably unperturbed and even enjoying himself. He maintains an air of serene entitlement. His arousal is moderate — he's engaged in the sparring but never truly threatened. He feels confident he can outlast Jack's attempts to remove him.

social_state.rationale: Algernon is effectively dominating the interaction despite being a guest who's been asked to leave. He's commandeering Jack's house, food, and even his clergyman. His warmth is neutral-cool — playfully combative rather than genuinely hostile.

known_facts: He has arranged his own christening as Ernest at 5:45
Jack was also never really Ernest
Cecily was attracted to the name Ernest
Jack has been christened status unknown
Jack wants him gone
There is one muffin left
hidden_secrets: He came to the country under false pretenses as Jack's fictional brother Ernest
He is genuinely falling for Cecily and is not merely Bunburying
false_beliefs: That being rechristened Ernest will resolve everything with Cecily
That his pseudo-scientific argument about chills being hereditary has any validity
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon deploys absurd knowledge claims (hereditary chills, science improving things) as rhetorical weapons rather than sincere beliefs. His real knowledge is that possession is nine-tenths of the law — staying and eating is winning.

beat_states.defense_state: Using absurd pseudo-logical arguments and fixation on food to neutralize Jack's anger and avoid being expelled
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Criticizes Jack for eating muffins while fiercely guarding the muffins for himself — uses trivialities to mask genuine romantic stakes
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Algernon's fixation on the muffins could be read as a genuine anxiety-displacement behavior rather than pure comic insouciance — he may be more worried about Cecily than he lets on

beat_states.desire_state: To get Algernon to leave his house immediately and to secure the exclusive right to be christened Ernest
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To maintain his fabricated identity and win Gwendolen's love by being the only Ernest
beat_states.obstacle: Algernon refuses to leave, has also arranged a christening as Ernest, and keeps deflecting Jack's demands with absurd logic and muffin-eating

affect_state.rationale: Jack is increasingly frustrated and losing control of the situation. His authority in his own home is being undermined. He's exasperated by Algernon's imperturbable calm and absurd arguments, and his own hypocrisy with the muffins reveals his agitation.

social_state.rationale: Despite being the host and property owner, Jack is losing status as Algernon cheerfully ignores every command to leave. His warmth toward Algernon is at a low point — he's openly hostile.

known_facts: He has arranged to be christened Ernest at 5:30 with Dr. Chasuble
Algernon has also arranged a christening as Ernest at 5:45
He has no evidence of ever having been christened
Gwendolen insists on the name Ernest
Both women have withdrawn after discovering the deception
hidden_secrets: Ernest was a complete fiction — he invented a brother to escape to London
His entire courtship of Gwendolen was built on a lie
false_beliefs: That being rechristened Ernest will fully resolve the crisis with Gwendolen
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack operates on the assumption that a name change via christening will fix everything, not recognizing the deeper issue of trust. He genuinely doesn't know if he was ever christened.

beat_states.defense_state: Claiming prior right and logical superiority — asserting that since he may never have been christened, he has more legitimate claim
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: Furiously demands Algernon stop eating muffins while himself stress-eating the very same muffins

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: The fact that they did not follow us at once into the house, as any one else would have done, seems to me to show that they have some sense of shame left.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: They have been eating muffins. That looks like repentance.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: They don’t seem to notice us at all. Couldn’t you cough?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: But I haven’t got a cough.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: They’re looking at us. What effrontery!

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: They’re approaching. That’s very forward of them.

beat_states.desire_state: To follow Gwendolen's lead in performing moral outrage while looking for signs that Algernon is properly sorry
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To experience the romantic adventure she has fantasized about, including the drama of a lovers' quarrel and reconciliation
beat_states.obstacle: Her natural sweetness and naivety make it difficult to sustain the pose of offended dignity; she keeps interpreting the men's behavior charitably

affect_state.rationale: Cecily is less invested in the performance of outrage and more genuinely open to what comes next. She is slightly amused, slightly nervous, and less certain of the social rules than Gwendolen.

social_state.rationale: She defers to Gwendolen as the more socially experienced woman, acting as the willing but slightly clumsy lieutenant. Her warmth leaks through despite attempts at coldness.

known_facts: Algernon deceived her about his name being Ernest
The men are eating muffins in the garden
Gwendolen expects them both to maintain a united front
hidden_secrets: She is already inclined to forgive Algernon
She finds the situation exciting rather than truly distressing
false_beliefs: Eating muffins might genuinely indicate repentance
epistemic_state.rationale: Cecily's diary-driven romantic imagination colors her interpretation of events. Her false belief about muffins is comic but reveals her desire to find reasons to forgive.

beat_states.defense_state: Literalism — deflecting Gwendolen's sophisticated social maneuvering with disarmingly naive, concrete responses
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She is performing solidarity with Gwendolen's indignation while simultaneously finding hopeful signs (muffins = repentance) that suggest she wants forgiveness to happen
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Cecily could be more strategically naive than she appears — using her literalism deliberately to soften the mood and accelerate reconciliation

beat_states.desire_state: To maintain moral superiority over Jack and Algernon while secretly wanting them to approach and make amends
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure a romantic union that meets her idealized standards while preserving her dignity and social control
beat_states.obstacle: The men are not demonstrating sufficient contrition; her pride prevents her from making the first move toward reconciliation

affect_state.rationale: Gwendolen is performing wounded dignity but is highly alert and engaged. She feels in control of the social script she's writing with Cecily, but underneath is anxious about whether the men will properly grovel.

social_state.rationale: She positions herself as the moral authority and social director of the scene, warm enough with Cecily as co-conspirator but cold toward the offstage men.

known_facts: Jack and Algernon deceived her and Cecily about their names
The men are currently eating muffins rather than pursuing them
She and Cecily have formed an alliance against the men
hidden_secrets: She desperately wants Jack to come and beg forgiveness
Her anger is largely performative
false_beliefs: She believes her indifference is convincing
epistemic_state.rationale: Gwendolen knows the facts of the deception but misreads her own emotional state, performing outrage that barely conceals her desire for reunion.

beat_states.defense_state: Projection of indifference — performing disdain to mask her eagerness for reconciliation
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She claims to want them to feel shame and stay away, yet actively monitors their every move and engineers a cough to get their attention
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gwendolen could be genuinely furious and using Cecily as audience for her righteous anger, with no desire for reconciliation yet

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Let us preserve a dignified silence.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Certainly. It’s the only thing to do now.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: This dignified silence seems to produce an unpleasant effect.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: A most distasteful one.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: But we will not be the first to speak.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Certainly not.

beat_states.desire_state: To align with Gwendolen's social authority while secretly hoping the impasse breaks soon
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To experience genuine romantic adventure while being accepted into sophisticated society
beat_states.obstacle: She is subordinate to Gwendolen's lead and cannot break the protocol without seeming weak; the men's absence is genuinely uncomfortable

affect_state.rationale: Less emotionally committed to the stance than Gwendolen; her quick agreements ('Certainly') feel almost automatic. She's uncomfortable with the silence but lacks the authority to change course.

social_state.rationale: Cecily defers to Gwendolen as the social superior and strategist, playing the loyal second. Warmth exists through their shared predicament.

known_facts: Algernon lied about being Ernest
Gwendolen is leading their joint response
The silence is not working
hidden_secrets: She is likely more forgiving toward Algernon than she is performing
false_beliefs: That following Gwendolen's lead will produce the best outcome
epistemic_state.rationale: Cecily is less worldly and follows Gwendolen's cues, but her awareness that the silence is 'distasteful' suggests she'd prefer a different approach.

beat_states.defense_state: Compliance as camouflage — hiding her own impulses behind agreement with Gwendolen's directives
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She echoes Gwendolen's resolve perfectly while her brief, emphatic agreements suggest she is less invested in the principle and more in the performance of solidarity
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Cecily's quick agreements may mask a growing impatience and desire to simply forgive Algernon and move on

beat_states.desire_state: To punish Jack and Algernon by withholding attention while maintaining superiority over the situation
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure a romantic life that meets her idealized standards while never relinquishing social control
beat_states.obstacle: The men are not responding to the silent treatment as hoped, and her own desire to resolve things pulls against her performative dignity

affect_state.rationale: She is irritated that the silence isn't producing the desired groveling, but maintains composure through sheer willpower. The admission that it produces 'an unpleasant effect' reveals cracks in her confidence.

social_state.rationale: She positions herself as the leader and choreographer of this joint protest, directing Cecily's behavior. Some warmth toward Cecily as fellow conspirator against the men.

known_facts: The men have deceived both women about their names
The men did not immediately follow them inside
The silent treatment is not having its intended effect
hidden_secrets: She is more eager to reconcile with Jack than she lets on
false_beliefs: That maintaining dignified silence is a genuinely effective strategy rather than a self-defeating pose
epistemic_state.rationale: Gwendolen recognizes the tactic is failing ('unpleasant effect') but her social code prevents her from abandoning it. She narrates the failure without adjusting course.

beat_states.defense_state: Ritualized formality — converting genuine hurt into a theatrical performance of wounded dignity
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She desperately wants the men to speak first but is increasingly aware the tactic isn't working, yet she doubles down on it rather than adapt
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gwendolen may be deliberately setting up a scenario where the silence becomes untenable so she can eventually 'yield' without losing face

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Mr. Worthing, I have something very particular to ask you. Much depends on your reply.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Gwendolen, your common sense is invaluable. Mr. Moncrieff, kindly answer me the following question. Why did you pretend to be my guardian’s brother?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: In order that I might have an opportunity of meeting you.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: That certainly seems a satisfactory explanation, does it not?

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Yes, dear, if you can believe him.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I don’t. But that does not affect the wonderful beauty of his answer.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: True. In matters of grave importance, style, not sincerity is the vital thing. Mr. Worthing, what explanation can you offer to me for pretending to have a brother? Was it in order that you might have an opportunity of coming up to town to see me as often as possible?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Can you doubt it, Miss Fairfax?

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: I have the gravest doubts upon the subject. But I intend to crush them. This is not the moment for German scepticism. Their explanations appear to be quite satisfactory, especially Mr. Worthing’s. That seems to me to have the stamp of truth upon it.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I am more than content with what Mr. Moncrieff said. His voice alone inspires one with absolute credulity.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Then you think we should forgive them?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Yes. I mean no.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: True! I had forgotten. There are principles at stake that one cannot surrender. Which of us should tell them? The task is not a pleasant one.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Could we not both speak at the same time?

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: An excellent idea! I nearly always speak at the same time as other people. Will you take the time from me?

beat_states.desire_state: To forgive Algernon and reunite with him without having to bear the discomfort of saying so directly
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To live out the romantic adventure she has already scripted in her diary — to be with her beloved 'Ernest'
beat_states.obstacle: Social propriety and Gwendolen's lead require her to perform reluctance; her own contradictory impulses ('Yes. I mean no.') reveal she cannot maintain the pretense

affect_state.rationale: Cecily is flustered and uncertain — her self-contradiction betrays genuine emotional confusion. She feels less in control than Gwendolen and more exposed. Moderate arousal from the anxiety of the situation.

social_state.rationale: Cecily defers to Gwendolen's leadership throughout this beat, accepting the subordinate role. Her warmth is high — she is naturally more open and less guarded than Gwendolen, and her suggestion of simultaneous speech is a generous, collaborative impulse.

known_facts: Algernon is not actually named Ernest
He invented 'Bunburying' to visit her
Gwendolen also wants to forgive but won't say so plainly
Both men are waiting to be addressed
hidden_secrets: She has already forgiven Algernon in her heart
false_beliefs: She half-believes that maintaining anger is a genuine moral obligation rather than a social performance
epistemic_state.rationale: Cecily is less self-aware about the performative nature of her stance than Gwendolen; her 'Yes. I mean no.' is an honest eruption of her real feelings breaking through the social script.

beat_states.defense_state: Displacement — proposing they speak simultaneously so neither has to individually own the act of forgiveness
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She openly contradicts herself ('Yes. I mean no.'), revealing that her desire to forgive is at war with her sense that she should be angry
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Cecily's 'Yes. I mean no.' could be read as genuine moral uncertainty rather than a comic slip — she may authentically struggle with whether deception warrants forgiveness

beat_states.desire_state: To maintain the appearance of principled indignation while engineering a path toward forgiveness and reunion with Jack
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To secure a romantic destiny that matches her idealized narrative — marrying a man named Ernest
beat_states.obstacle: Her own self-imposed 'principles' and dignity require her to perform outrage she no longer fully feels

affect_state.rationale: Gwendolen is enjoying her role as stage-manager of this scene. She feels in control and mildly pleased, channeling her desire to forgive through the pleasurable exercise of social choreography. Low vulnerability because she's performing rather than exposing.

social_state.rationale: She positions herself as the senior partner and leader in this alliance with Cecily — she sets the terms, proposes the framework, and will 'give the time.' Warmth toward Cecily has increased as they are co-conspirators in a shared theatrical ritual.

known_facts: Neither Jack nor Algernon is actually named Ernest
Both men have been deceptive about their identities
Cecily also wants to forgive but feels she shouldn't
The men are waiting anxiously outside or nearby
hidden_secrets: She has already internally decided to forgive Jack
false_beliefs: She believes she is maintaining genuine principled resistance rather than performing a face-saving ritual
epistemic_state.rationale: Gwendolen knows the facts of the deception but is constructing an elaborate social performance to bridge the gap between her wounded pride and her desire to reconcile.

beat_states.defense_state: Intellectualization — converting genuine emotional wavering into an abstract discussion of 'principles at stake'
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She is actively constructing the mechanism of forgiveness while verbally insisting on the impossibility of surrender
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Gwendolen may be genuinely torn and using the collaborative speech idea as a way to diffuse personal responsibility for capitulating

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Certainly. GWENDOLEN and CECILY Your Christian names are still an insuperable barrier. That is all! JACK and ALGERNON Our Christian names! Is that all? But we are going to be christened this afternoon.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: For my sake you are prepared to do this terrible thing?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I am.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: To please me you are ready to face this fearful ordeal?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I am!

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: How absurd to talk of the equality of the sexes! Where questions of self-sacrifice are concerned, men are infinitely beyond us.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: We are.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: They have moments of physical courage of which we women know absolutely nothing.

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Darling!

utterances.speaker: MERRIMAN
utterances.text: Ahem! Ahem! Lady Bracknell!

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Good heavens!

beat_states.desire_state: to continue reconciling with Gwendolen and resolve the christening/name situation
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to secure Gwendolen's love and maintain his constructed identity as Ernest
beat_states.obstacle: Lady Bracknell's sudden, unexpected arrival threatens to expose his deceptions and block his engagement to Gwendolen

affect_state.rationale: The exclamation 'Good heavens!' reveals genuine shock and dread; Lady Bracknell has already refused his suit and her arrival at the Manor House where all his deceptions converge is catastrophic

social_state.rationale: His status as host and suitor is instantly diminished by the arrival of the formidable authority figure who has already rejected him; warmth is suspended by panic

known_facts: Lady Bracknell has forbidden his engagement to Gwendolen
Gwendolen and Cecily are present and have just begun to forgive him and Algernon
He has been pretending to be Ernest
He has arranged to be christened Ernest
hidden_secrets: He invented a brother named Ernest as a cover for his double life
He is Jack in the country and Ernest in town
He has no idea of his actual parentage
false_beliefs: He may have believed Lady Bracknell would not pursue Gwendolen to the country
epistemic_state.rationale: Jack knows Lady Bracknell is his most dangerous obstacle and her presence here means his carefully managed worlds are colliding

beat_states.defense_state: shock response — momentary freeze before he can formulate a plan
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: he was just on the verge of triumph with Gwendolen but now faces the one person who can destroy everything
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Jack's 'Good heavens!' could contain a tiny element of dark comedy or performative dismay for the benefit of those present, rather than pure genuine shock

beat_states.desire_state: to fulfill his duty by properly announcing Lady Bracknell's arrival
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: to maintain professional decorum and serve the household flawlessly
beat_states.obstacle: the charged emotional atmosphere among the couples may make his announcement unwelcome

affect_state.rationale: Merriman is performing his role with characteristic composure; the double 'Ahem!' suggests slight discomfort at interrupting an intimate scene, but he proceeds with professional certainty

social_state.rationale: As a servant, his status is formally low, but he wields momentary power as the bearer of significant news; warmth is minimal and professional

known_facts: Lady Bracknell has arrived at the Manor House
He is interrupting an emotionally charged gathering
epistemic_state.rationale: Merriman knows only the immediate practical fact of the visitor's arrival; he is not privy to the romantic and identity complications

beat_states.defense_state: professional detachment — uses formal announcement protocol to distance himself from the social drama

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Gwendolen! What does this mean?

utterances.speaker: GWENDOLEN
utterances.text: Merely that I am engaged to be married to Mr. Worthing, mamma.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Come here. Sit down. Sit down immediately. Hesitation of any kind is a sign of mental decay in the young, of physical weakness in the old. Apprised, sir, of my daughter’s sudden flight by her trusty maid, whose confidence I purchased by means of a small coin, I followed her at once by a luggage train. Her unhappy father is, I am glad to say, under the impression that she is attending a more than usually lengthy lecture by the University Extension Scheme on the Influence of a permanent income on Thought. I do not propose to undeceive him. Indeed I have never undeceived him on any question. I would consider it wrong. But of course, you will clearly understand that all communication between yourself and my daughter must cease immediately from this moment. On this point, as indeed on all points, I am firm.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I am engaged to be married to Gwendolen, Lady Bracknell!

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: You are nothing of the kind, sir. And now, as regards Algernon! . . . Algernon!

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Yes, Aunt Augusta.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: May I ask if it is in this house that your invalid friend Mr. Bunbury resides?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Oh! No! Bunbury doesn’t live here. Bunbury is somewhere else at present. In fact, Bunbury is dead.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Dead! When did Mr. Bunbury die? His death must have been extremely sudden.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Oh! I killed Bunbury this afternoon. I mean poor Bunbury died this afternoon.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: What did he die of?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Bunbury? Oh, he was quite exploded.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Exploded! Was he the victim of a revolutionary outrage? I was not aware that Mr. Bunbury was interested in social legislation. If so, he is well punished for his morbidity.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: My dear Aunt Augusta, I mean he was found out! The doctors found out that Bunbury could not live, that is what I mean—so Bunbury died.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: He seems to have had great confidence in the opinion of his physicians. I am glad, however, that he made up his mind at the last to some definite course of action, and acted under proper medical advice. And now that we have finally got rid of this Mr. Bunbury, may I ask, Mr. Worthing, who is that young person whose hand my nephew Algernon is now holding in what seems to me a peculiarly unnecessary manner?

beat_states.desire_state: To cleanly dispose of the Bunbury fiction so he can pursue Cecily without interference from his aunt
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To live pleasurably and freely while maintaining social respectability
beat_states.obstacle: Lady Bracknell's interrogation keeps catching his improvised lies, forcing increasingly absurd explanations

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is flustered and improvising badly — 'I killed Bunbury' slips out before he corrects himself, 'he was quite exploded' is another panicked overreach. He's on the back foot throughout, scrambling to satisfy Lady Bracknell's relentless questioning.

social_state.rationale: Thoroughly subordinated to Lady Bracknell's authority; he addresses her as 'My dear Aunt Augusta' trying to invoke familial warmth to soften her interrogation, but he has no leverage here.

known_facts: Bunbury never existed — he was a fiction to escape social obligations
He is holding Cecily's hand and Lady Bracknell has noticed
He needs to permanently retire the Bunbury excuse
hidden_secrets: Bunbury was always a complete invention
He has been 'Bunburying' to visit Cecily in the country under a false name
epistemic_state.rationale: Algernon knows the full truth but must navigate killing off a fiction as though it were fact, leading to comically tangled language.

beat_states.defense_state: Comic evasion through escalating absurdity — each correction tries to paper over the last slip
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He needs to kill off a fictional person convincingly while simultaneously being unable to keep his story straight, revealing his chronic dishonesty even as he tries to appear honest

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: That lady is Miss Cecily Cardew, my ward.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: I am engaged to be married to Cecily, Aunt Augusta.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: I beg your pardon?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Mr. Moncrieff and I are engaged to be married, Lady Bracknell.

beat_states.desire_state: To announce his engagement to Cecily and gain Aunt Augusta's acceptance
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To marry Cecily and secure love and excitement against the constraints of society
beat_states.obstacle: Lady Bracknell's authority and likely disapproval; the deceptive circumstances of his courtship

affect_state.rationale: Algernon is buoyed by genuine romantic feeling and his characteristic insouciance, but is acutely aware that his aunt holds enormous power over his fate

social_state.rationale: As nephew, he's subordinate to Lady Bracknell but tries to charm his way through with affectionate familiarity

known_facts: He is engaged to Cecily
He courted her as 'Ernest'
Lady Bracknell will scrutinize the match
Jack disapproves of the engagement

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: I do not know whether there is anything peculiarly exciting in the air of this particular part of Hertfordshire, but the number of engagements that go on seems to me considerably above the proper average that statistics have laid down for our guidance. I think some preliminary inquiry on my part would not be out of place. Mr. Worthing, is Miss Cardew at all connected with any of the larger railway stations in London? I merely desire information. Until yesterday I had no idea that there were any families or persons whose origin was a Terminus.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Miss Cardew is the grand-daughter of the late Mr. Thomas Cardew of 149 Belgrave Square, S.W.; Gervase Park, Dorking, Surrey; and the Sporran, Fifeshire, N.B.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: That sounds not unsatisfactory. Three addresses always inspire confidence, even in tradesmen. But what proof have I of their authenticity?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I have carefully preserved the Court Guides of the period. They are open to your inspection, Lady Bracknell.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: I have known strange errors in that publication.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Miss Cardew’s family solicitors are Messrs. Markby, Markby, and Markby.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Markby, Markby, and Markby? A firm of the very highest position in their profession. Indeed I am told that one of the Mr. Markby’s is occasionally to be seen at dinner parties. So far I am satisfied.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: How extremely kind of you, Lady Bracknell! I have also in my possession, you will be pleased to hear, certificates of Miss Cardew’s birth, baptism, whooping cough, registration, vaccination, confirmation, and the measles; both the German and the English variety.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Ah! A life crowded with incident, I see; though perhaps somewhat too exciting for a young girl. I am not myself in favour of premature experiences. Gwendolen! the time approaches for our departure. We have not a moment to lose. As a matter of form, Mr. Worthing, I had better ask you if Miss Cardew has any little fortune?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Oh! about a hundred and thirty thousand pounds in the Funds. That is all. Goodbye, Lady Bracknell. So pleased to have seen you.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: A moment, Mr. Worthing. A hundred and thirty thousand pounds! And in the Funds! Miss Cardew seems to me a most attractive young lady, now that I look at her. Few girls of the present day have any really solid qualities, any of the qualities that last, and improve with time. We live, I regret to say, in an age of surfaces. Come over here, dear. Pretty child! your dress is sadly simple, and your hair seems almost as Nature might have left it. But we can soon alter all that. A thoroughly experienced French maid produces a really marvellous result in a very brief space of time. I remember recommending one to young Lady Lancing, and after three months her own husband did not know her.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: And after six months nobody knew her.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Kindly turn round, sweet child. No, the side view is what I want. Yes, quite as I expected. There are distinct social possibilities in your profile. The two weak points in our age are its want of principle and its want of profile. The chin a little higher, dear. Style largely depends on the way the chin is worn. They are worn very high, just at present. Algernon!

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Yes, Aunt Augusta!

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: There are distinct social possibilities in Miss Cardew’s profile.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Cecily is the sweetest, dearest, prettiest girl in the whole world. And I don’t care twopence about social possibilities.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Never speak disrespectfully of Society, Algernon. Only people who can’t get into it do that. Dear child, of course you know that Algernon has nothing but his debts to depend upon. But I do not approve of mercenary marriages. When I married Lord Bracknell I had no fortune of any kind. But I never dreamed for a moment of allowing that to stand in my way. Well, I suppose I must give my consent.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Thank you, Aunt Augusta.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Cecily, you may kiss me!

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Thank you, Lady Bracknell.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: You may also address me as Aunt Augusta for the future.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Thank you, Aunt Augusta.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: The marriage, I think, had better take place quite soon.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Thank you, Aunt Augusta.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Thank you, Aunt Augusta.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: To speak frankly, I am not in favour of long engagements. They give people the opportunity of finding out each other’s character before marriage, which I think is never advisable.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I beg your pardon for interrupting you, Lady Bracknell, but this engagement is quite out of the question. I am Miss Cardew’s guardian, and she cannot marry without my consent until she comes of age. That consent I absolutely decline to give.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Upon what grounds may I ask? Algernon is an extremely, I may almost say an ostentatiously, eligible young man. He has nothing, but he looks everything. What more can one desire?

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: It pains me very much to have to speak frankly to you, Lady Bracknell, about your nephew, but the fact is that I do not approve at all of his moral character. I suspect him of being untruthful.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Untruthful! My nephew Algernon? Impossible! He is an Oxonian.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: I fear there can be no possible doubt about the matter. This afternoon during my temporary absence in London on an important question of romance, he obtained admission to my house by means of the false pretence of being my brother. Under an assumed name he drank, I’ve just been informed by my butler, an entire pint bottle of my Perrier-Jouet, Brut, ’89; wine I was specially reserving for myself. Continuing his disgraceful deception, he succeeded in the course of the afternoon in alienating the affections of my only ward. He subsequently stayed to tea, and devoured every single muffin. And what makes his conduct all the more heartless is, that he was perfectly well aware from the first that I have no brother, that I never had a brother, and that I don’t intend to have a brother, not even of any kind. I distinctly told him so myself yesterday afternoon.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Ahem! Mr. Worthing, after careful consideration I have decided entirely to overlook my nephew’s conduct to you.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: That is very generous of you, Lady Bracknell. My own decision, however, is unalterable. I decline to give my consent.

beat_states.desire_state: To block Algernon's engagement to Cecily as leverage and payback for Algernon's Bunburying deception
beat_states.superobjective_reminder: To control his own social world and secure his desired marriage to Gwendolen on his own terms
beat_states.obstacle: Lady Bracknell dismisses his objections and considers Algernon perfectly eligible

affect_state.rationale: Jack is enjoying his position of power as guardian — he relishes the cataloguing of Algernon's offenses with comic specificity (the Perrier-Jouet, the muffins). He feels firmly in control, exercising his legal authority, though there's underlying irritation at having been outmaneuvered by Algernon.

social_state.rationale: Jack wields his guardian authority with clear satisfaction, positioning himself above both Algernon and Lady Bracknell in this negotiation. His politeness ('I beg your pardon,' 'It pains me') is weaponized coldness.

He wants to use this leverage to pressure Lady Bracknell into consenting to his marriage to Gwendolen

epistemic_state.rationale: Jack has full knowledge of Algernon's deceptions but is spectacularly blind to his own parallel ones. His itemized prosecution of Algernon's crimes is comically precise yet utterly hypocritical.

beat_states.defense_state: Righteous indignation masks his own identical deceptions — he projects his own Bunburying sins onto Algernon
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: He accuses Algernon of untruthfulness and false pretence while he himself has been living a double life as Ernest/Jack — the hypocrisy is total yet he seems entirely unaware of it
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Jack may be fully conscious of his hypocrisy and is playing this strategically — withholding consent purely as a bargaining chip for Gwendolen's hand, with the moral objections being a performative smokescreen.

affect_state.rationale: Lady Bracknell is moderately confident but increasingly aware she doesn't hold all the cards — Jack's legal authority is real. Her 'Ahem!' signals a tactical recalibration. She maintains composure but is working to find leverage.

social_state.rationale: She still commands the room through sheer force of personality and social rank, but Jack's legal authority as guardian has created an unusual power dynamic where she must negotiate rather than simply decree.

She previously rejected Jack as a suitor for Gwendolen — this may become leverage against her
false_beliefs: That being an Oxonian is a genuine guarantee of truthfulness
That her magnanimous 'overlooking' of Algernon's conduct should settle the matter
epistemic_state.rationale: Lady Bracknell processes social transactions rather than moral truths. Her logic is internally consistent within her value system — eligibility trumps ethics, class markers equal character.

beat_states.defense_state: Class-based deflection — she parries every accusation with aristocratic non-sequiturs (Algernon is an Oxonian, therefore cannot be untruthful) and simply declares she 'overlooks' his conduct
beat_states.psychological_contradiction: She has just learned of Algernon's thorough deception but chooses to magnanimously 'overlook' it, revealing that her moral standards are entirely subordinate to financial and social calculation
beat_states.alternative_hypothesis: Lady Bracknell may fully recognize the absurdity of defending Algernon and is simply performing aristocratic imperiousness as a negotiating stance, fully expecting a transactional resolution.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: Come here, sweet child. How old are you, dear?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Well, I am really only eighteen, but I always admit to twenty when I go to evening parties.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: You are perfectly right in making some slight alteration. Indeed, no woman should ever be quite accurate about her age. It looks so calculating . . . Eighteen, but admitting to twenty at evening parties. Well, it will not be very long before you are of age and free from the restraints of tutelage. So I don’t think your guardian’s consent is, after all, a matter of any importance.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Pray excuse me, Lady Bracknell, for interrupting you again, but it is only fair to tell you that according to the terms of her grandfather’s will Miss Cardew does not come legally of age till she is thirty-five.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: That does not seem to me to be a grave objection. Thirty-five is a very attractive age. London society is full of women of the very highest birth who have, of their own free choice, remained thirty-five for years. Lady Dumbleton is an instance in point. To my own knowledge she has been thirty-five ever since she arrived at the age of forty, which was many years ago now. I see no reason why our dear Cecily should not be even still more attractive at the age you mention than she is at present. There will be a large accumulation of property.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Algy, could you wait for me till I was thirty-five?

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Of course I could, Cecily. You know I could.

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: Yes, I felt it instinctively, but I couldn’t wait all that time. I hate waiting even five minutes for anybody. It always makes me rather cross. I am not punctual myself, I know, but I do like punctuality in others, and waiting, even to be married, is quite out of the question.

utterances.speaker: ALGERNON
utterances.text: Then what is to be done, Cecily?

utterances.speaker: CECILY
utterances.text: I don’t know, Mr. Moncrieff.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: My dear Mr. Worthing, as Miss Cardew states positively that she cannot wait till she is thirty-five—a remark which I am bound to say seems to me to show a somewhat impatient nature—I would beg of you to reconsider your decision.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: But my dear Lady Bracknell, the matter is entirely in your own hands. The moment you consent to my marriage with Gwendolen, I will most gladly allow your nephew to form an alliance with my ward.

utterances.speaker: LADY BRACKNELL
utterances.text: You must be quite aware that what you propose is out of the question.

utterances.speaker: JACK
utterances.text: Then a passionate celibacy is all that any of us can look forward to.
